Count 'N Catch: A Skip Counting Game Encourage your child to practice skip counting by turning number patterns into an interactive game of catch. It will get your child moving and learning, ball in hand! What You Need: - One soft ball, two players What You Do: - Begin by telling your child you are going to practice skip counting while playing catch with the ball. Be sure to briefly review counting by 2's, 5's, and 10's. - Sit or stand about six feet away from your child with the ball in your hands. Say the first number in the skip counting pattern aloud, and as you're saying it, toss the ball to your child. Now it's his turn to say the next number in the pattern! For example, when skip counting by 2's, you say, "two," and pass the ball to your child who will say, "four" and then pass the ball back to you. Then you say "six," the next number in the pattern, and toss it back to him. Continue to play the counting game this way, and challenge your child to count as high as he can. - After playing the counting game with multiples of 2, play the same way with multiples of 5 and 10. For an extra challenge, try skip counting by 3's and 4's. Turn the counting game into a fun language arts activity by practicing with parts of speech in place of numbers. Play catch back and forth as you take turns listing nouns. See how many nouns your child can list before getting stuck! Next, pass the ball to each other while naming adjectives. Afterwards, play the game while listing verbs. For an extra challenge, try playing the game with adverbs, too. The possibilities are endless with Count 'N Catch. And getting some of those terms and concepts off the page and into the body, really helps kids remember them! yeah, what are you going to play with? Know how to open them, but I guess I do. Title: How to Choose a Perfect Name for Your Cat Introduction: Choosing a name for your cat is an exciting task that requires careful consideration. The name you select will be used throughout your cat's life, so it should be something you love saying out loud and find fitting for their personality. This article will guide you through various steps, providing insightful tips and guidelines along the way to help you choose the perfect moniker for your feline companion. Step 1: Understand Your Cat's Personality Observe your new cat closely to understand its unique traits, behaviors, and quirks. By doing this, you can pick a name that reflects its character or appearance. For example, if your cat is playful, consider names like Dart, Skipper, or Pounce. If it has distinctive markings, such as spots, think about naming them Speckles or Spotty. Key Tips: - Wait a few days after adopting your cat before deciding on a name to ensure ample time to observe their distinct characteristics. - Avoid choosing human baby names since they may cause confusion when addressing both your pet and family members. Step 2: Consider Names with Meaning Research different languages and mythologies to discover meaningful names related to qualities you admire in your cat or symbolize what it represents to you. For instance, Apollo (Greek god of light) could suit a bright orange tabby, while Luna (Latin word for moon) would make sense for a gray cat with striking eyes resembling a full moon. Guidelines: - Opt for simple pronunciation to avoid miscommunication between you, your family, and veterinary staff. - Stay away from overly complicated names containing multiple syllables, which might confuse your cat. Step 3: Select a Short Name Keep your cat's name short - ideally one or two syllables - making it easier for them to recognize and respond to. Longer names tend to get abbreviated, leading to potential misunderstandings during training sessions or calls for attention. Examples include Bella, Gizmo, or Tiger. Key Points: - Shorter names are more effective at capturing your cat's attention compared to lengthier ones. - Refrain from using names similar to common commands (e.g., "No," "Stay," "Fetch") to prevent confusion. Step 4: Test Potential Names Once you have narrowed down your list of possible names, test them by calling out each option several times in succession. Pay close attention to your cat's reactions; if it responds positively or shows curiosity towards a specific name, take note of it. Additional Guidelines: - Call your cat using various tones and volumes to assess whether they react consistently across all instances. - Eliminate any options causing disinterest or negative associations, focusing instead on those eliciting positive responses. Step 5: Finalize the Decision After testing potential names, analyze your observations to determine the most suitable choice based on your cat's reaction(s). Consult other household members and seek consensus regarding the final decision. Remember, selecting a name should involve everyone who interacts regularly with your furry friend. Final Thoughts: Naming your cat is an essential part of building a strong bond with them. It allows you to communicate effectively, establish trust, and create cherished memories together. Take your time, enjoy the process, and remember that no matter what name you ultimately choose, your cat will always be a beloved member of your family. Mommy doesn't like fingers in your mouth. Title: How to Block an Email Address on Yahoo Yahoo Mail is a popular email service used by millions of people worldwide. However, spam emails are an unfortunate reality that comes with using any email service. One way to combat this issue is by blocking email addresses from which you no longer wish to receive messages. This tutorial will guide you through the process of blocking an email address on Yahoo Mail. **Step 1:** Log into your Yahoo Mail account ----------------------------------------------- To begin the process of blocking an email address, first log into your Yahoo Mail account using your username or email address and password at <https://mail.yahoo.com/>. It's important to ensure that you have access to the correct account before proceeding. **Step 2:** Open the message from the sender you want to block ------------------------------------------------------------- Locate an email within your inbox sent from the address you would like to block. Click on the email subject line to open the full conversation. By starting the process from inside the email thread, you can be sure that you're blocking the right person or organization. **Step 3:** Access the sender's information panel ---------------------------------------------- Once the email has been opened, look towards the top of the page where the sender's name and email address appear. On the far-right side of their contact details, click on the three vertical dots (also known as the 'More Options' button). A dropdown menu should now be visible. **Step 4:** Select 'Block Sender' option ------------------------------------- From the dropdown menu, select the "Block Sender" option. Upon clicking this selection, a confirmation popup window will appear asking if you truly want to block the chosen email address. Keep in mind that once you confirm the action, all incoming messages from this address will automatically move to the Spam folder. **Step 5:** Confirm blocking the sender ------------------------------------ Review the information displayed in the confirmation box to make certain that you've selected the correct email address. If everything looks accurate, click on the "OK" button to finalize the process. The sender will then be officially blocked. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Be aware that after blocking someone, they won't be notified about being blocked. They may still attempt sending emails thinking they went unnoticed. * Check your Spam folder periodically just in case legitimate mail ends up there due to overzealous filtering caused by excessive blocking. You might need to adjust your filters accordingly. * To manage your list of blocked senders, navigate to Settings > Filters > Blocked Addresses. Here, you can add new entries manually, remove existing ones, or edit current ones if needed. * Remember that while blocking solves immediate issues related to unwanted emails, reporting abusive content also helps maintain a safe environment for everyone else. Use the Report Abuse feature when necessary. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. She loved to play with her toys and run around outside. But one day, her mom told her she had to study for a test at school. Lily didn't want to study because it wasn't easy for her. Instead, she decided to play with her toys and forgot all about studying. The next day, she went to school and took the test. But she didn't do well because she didn't study. Her teacher was disappointed and gave her a mop to clean the classroom as a punishment. Lily realized that studying was important and promised to do better next time. She cleaned the classroom with the mop and thought about how she could make studying easier for herself. From then on, she made sure to study every day and never forgot the importance of it. Title: How to Heal a Broken Friendship ===================================== A broken friendship can be a painful experience that leaves you feeling hurt, confused, and unsure about how to move forward. However, with patience, understanding, and effort from both parties, healing is possible. This guide will walk you through the steps needed to mend a fractured relationship and provide useful tips along the way. Remember, every situation is unique, so tailor these suggestions to fit your specific circumstances. ### Step 1: Assess the Situation Before taking any action, take some time to reflect on what led to the breakdown in the friendship. Consider the following questions: * What were the contributing factors? * Was there a single event or a series of events that caused the rift? * Have you tried talking about the issues before? If so, what was the response? Understanding the root cause(s) of the problem will help you determine the best course of action moving forward. #### Key Tips * Be honest with yourself during this assessment process. * Avoid blaming yourself or the other person entirely; instead, focus on identifying the underlying issues. Disable Autoplay on Spotify: A Comprehensive Tutorial ===================================================== Are you tired of Spotify automatically playing songs or playlists when you don't want them to? This comprehensive tutorial will guide you through disabling autoplay on Spotify with clear, concise, and actionable steps. By following these instructions, you can regain control over your music listening experience. Step 1: Open the Spotify Desktop Application -------------------------------------------- Firstly, launch the Spotify desktop application on your computer by double-clicking its icon. If you haven't installed Spotify yet, download and install it from their official website. Using the desktop app ensures easier access to settings compared to the web player. Key tip: Make sure you are logged into your account before proceeding. ### Step 2: Navigate to Settings Click on the arrow next to your profile picture located at the top right corner of the screen. From the dropdown menu, select 'Settings'. Alternatively, press `Ctrl + ,` (Windows) or `Cmd + ,` (Mac) as a shortcut. This action takes you to the configuration hub where all essential preferences are managed. The changes made here directly affect the behavior of the Spotify app during usage. Step 3: Locate the Playback Section ----------------------------------- Inside the 'Settings' window, scroll down until you find the 'Playback' section. It should be positioned around the middle of the page under other categories like 'Display', 'Devices', etc. The 'Playback' section contains various options related to sound quality, crossfade, gapless playback, and most importantly, autoplay. Manipulating these settings allows customization of how audio content is delivered within the platform. Step 4: Toggle Off Autoplay ----------------------------- Within the 'Playback' section, locate the toggle switch labeled 'Autoplay'. Its current status is indicated visually - if enabled, the slider moves towards the right appearing blue; if disabled, it shifts left showing gray color. Click on the switch to turn off autoplay. Disabling this feature prevents Spotify from automatically playing recommended tracks once your queue ends or after finishing an album or playlist. Instead, it pauses until you manually initiate another song, providing uninterrupted musical enjoyment according to your preference. Step 5: Confirm Changes & Exit Settings --------------------------------------- After successfully turning off autoplay, ensure that the change has been saved correctly. No further confirmation prompt usually appears since toggling switches generally saves modifications instantly. However, always check whether the 'Autoplay' label now reads 'Off.' Finally, close the 'Settings' tab either by clicking the 'X' button at the upper right corner or pressing `Alt + F4` (Windows) or `Cmd + Q` (Mac). Now, resume listening to music without worrying about unexpected track transitions caused by autoplay. Conclusion ---------- By meticulously executing every stage outlined above, users have effectively learned how to disable autoplay on Spotify. With full command over playback controls, they can immerse themselves completely in their curated playlists and favorite albums. Anna Beth Sanders recognition is one of the best predictors of a child's future reading achievement (Adams 36). To read, children must be able to automatically recognize letters. In this lesson, children are taught how to recognize D, write uppercase and D, and the sound and mouth move that D=/d/ makes. Materials: Primary writing paper and pencils for each student, dry erase or chalk board with marked primary writing lines, with tongue twister: Ding Dong, David's Daddy is at the door, picture deer, door, diamond, dog, cat, hat, dinosaur, sun, and rope, and the book The Doorbell Rang, by Pat Hutchens. - Introduce the letter D. "Today we are going to learn about the letter D. Ding Dong begins with D. Lets all say Ding Dong together, and ring the door bell( show motion). Do you hear the D=/d/? Lets say ding dong making the D=/d/ sound like a machine gun, like this, dddding ddddong. Now you try. What does your mouth do when you say D=/d/? Did you feel the tip of your tongue just barely touch the roof of your mouth, right behind your top teeth, and then did you feel your mouth open a little bit and your tongue pop down? That is what our mouths do when we say D=/d/. Let's say Ding Dong and ring the doorbell together and see if we feel it. Ding Dong." - Name words to the whole class, some beginning with d, some not, some ending with d, some not. Ask the class to ring the door bell when they hear D=/d/. Teacher rings doorbell on the first couple of D=/d/ words to model for the students. "Now I am going to call out some words. If you hear D=/d/ in the word, I want you to ring the door bell. You may here D=/d/ at the beginning or end of a word. Deer, dog, cat, man, bed, mad, happy." - Introduce the tongue twister, Ding Dong, David's Daddy is at the door. Tongue twister is written on chart paper for the class to see. Repeat tongue twister with the class. "Here is a tongue twister with D=/d/: (point to chart) Ding Dong, David's Daddy is at the door, let's say it together. Ding Dong, David's Daddy is at the door. Did you hear D=/d/ in that tongue twister? This time when we say it, ring the door bell when you hear D=/d/. Ding Dong, David's Daddy is at the - Model and instruct how to write D and d. Use dry erase or chalk board with primary lines. Have the children use their primary paper. "We Know what D=/d/ sounds like, now we will learn how to write the letter D. Take out your paper and pencil and watch what I do. (Model while instructing) For big D we start at the roof, go straight down, pick up, and go around. Now you try to make a D, start at the roof, go straight down, pick up, and go around. Good. For little d, first we make little c, then little d. Now you try, first make little c, then little d. Continue to make big D and little d, while I walk around the room to see how well you are doing. (Teacher will help those having trouble). - Introduce and read The Doorbell Rang. Ask children to ring the doorbell every time they hear D=/d/ in the book. "Now we are going to read The Doorbell Rang, this is a book about some kids who are trying to share cookies, and more people keep coming to the door, so they have to keep sharing the cookies with more and moor people. What sound does a doorbell make when it rings? And when we say ding dong we say D=/d/, don't we? Every time you hear D=/d/ in this story I want you to ring the doorbell. - Assess the children using the picture page. "Now I am going to pass out a page with some pictures on it. If the picture begins with D, like duck, I want you to circle the picture. If the picture does not begin with D, like cow, do not circle it. Adams, Marilyn-Jager. [with a mouth full of cracker crumbs] [BEF places a paper clip in the hands of a silver man] Becoming part of a crowd can be a fun and exhilarating experience, whether you're attending a concert, protest, or sporting event. However, blending in with a large group of people can also present challenges, such as maintaining your personal space and avoiding conflicts. Here is an in-depth guide to help you "be in the crowd" safely and enjoyably: **Step 1: Dress Appropriately** Research the dress code or common attire for the type of event you will be attending. For example, if you are going to a music festival, wear comfortable clothing that allows easy movement, along with sturdy shoes. Avoid wearing valuable jewelry or carrying expensive items that could make you stand out as a target for theft. Additionally, consider dressing in layers so you can adjust to changing temperatures throughout the day. *Key Tip*: Choose clothes that match the color scheme of the event. This simple trick can help you blend in more effectively. **Step 2: Familiarize Yourself with Surroundings** Before entering the crowded area, take note of emergency exits, first aid stations, restrooms, and other important facilities. If possible, study maps or layouts provided by organizers beforehand. Having this knowledge will enable you to navigate the crowd efficiently and confidently. *Guideline*: Keep track of landmarks or unique features around you to maintain orientation within the crowd. **Step 3: Arrive Early** To secure a good spot and avoid rushing through densely packed areas, arrive early at the venue. This strategy gives you ample time to find a suitable location and acclimate yourself to the environment. *Key Tip*: Upon arrival, observe the behavior of those already in the crowd. Imitate their actions to minimize attention drawn towards yourself. **Step 4: Maintain Personal Space** While immersed in a crowd, remain aware of your body language and posture. Stand upright but relaxed, keeping arms loose at your sides. Avoid making exaggerated movements or gestures that may invade others' personal spaces. *Guideline*: When moving through the crowd, apologize politely if you accidentally bump into someone. Acknowledging these minor collisions can prevent potential confrontations. **Step 5: Stay Hydrated and Nourished** Long periods spent in crowds can lead to dehydration and fatigue. Carry water and snacks to keep energy levels up and stay hydrated. Remember to take breaks when needed to rest and recharge. *Key Tip*: Opt for refillable bottles over single-use plastics to reduce waste and save money. **Step 6: Establish Visual Contact with Friends** If attending the event with friends, establish meeting points and agree on signals or codes in case you get separated. Maintaining visual contact reduces anxiety about losing one another amidst the chaos. *Guideline*: Use height differences, distinct clothing, or accessories to quickly locate your companions in a sea of people. **Step 7: Remain Calm and Patient** Crowds can be unpredictable and stressful. To ensure safety and enjoyment, practice deep breathing exercises to stay calm during overwhelming situations. Exercise patience when navigating through dense areas and wait for natural openings instead of forcing your way through. *Key Tip*: Trust your instincts; if something feels off or unsafe, remove yourself from the situation immediately. By following these steps and incorporating our key tips and guidelines, you'll be well prepared to "be in the crowd" while maximizing both comfort and security. Light's speed remained about the same when measured by an observer traveling in its field, however, although "addition of velocities" predicted the field to be slower or faster "relative" to the observer traveling with or against it. So, versus the electromagnetic field, the observer kept losing speed. Still, this did not violate Galileo's Principle of relativity that says the laws of mechanics work the same for all objects showing inertia. Rain are droplets of water falling from clouds in the sky that are bigger than 0.5 mm. Droplets of water that are about 0.2mm to 0.5mm big are called drizzle. Rain is a kind of precipitation. Precipitation is any kind of water that falls from clouds in the sky, like rain, hail, sleet and snow. It is measured by a rain gauge. Rain is part of the water cycle. Convectional rain [change] Convectional rain happens in places of the world that are hot and wet. Sometimes, it also takes place in tropical deserts and inland areas during summer, when temperatures are hot. During the day, the sun makes the ground very hot. Air near the ground surface is heated by conduction. The heated air expands, becoming less dense and rises in a strong upwards air current. When the temperature of the rising air falls to the dew point, water vapor shrinks into thick clouds and forms convection rain. Depending on the temperature it may fall as sleet or snow. Relief rain [change] Relief rain usually happens along coastal areas where a line of hills runs along to the coast. When wet onshore wind from the sea meets a mountain, hill or any other sort of barrier, it is forced to rise along the slope and cools. When the air temperature falls to its dew point, water vapor condenses to form clouds. When the clouds can no longer hold the water droplets, relief rain begins to fall on the windward slope of the mountain. On the leeward slope, air sinks, it is warmed and further dried by compression. Therefore, the leeward slope is known as rain shadow. Moist winds blow in from the sea and are forced to rise over the land. The air cools and the water vapour condenses, forming rain drops. Relief rain is also a very dense and cold mixture of precipitation. Relief or orographic rain is formed when air is forced to cool when it rises over relief features in the landscape such as hills or mountains. As it rises it cools, condenses and forms rain. The highest annual rainfall totals occur in mountain areas. There is often a rain shadow effect whereby the leeward (downwind) slope receives a relatively small amount of rain. Frontal rain/Cyclonic rain [change] Frontal rain happens when a cold front meets a warm front. The less dense warm air rises and condenses forming clouds. These clouds grow and eventually create rain. In some places on the northern temperate zone the cold air front tends to come from the north west and the warm air front comes from the south west. A Rainstorm is a sudden heavy fall of rain. It may cause floods on land which is not much above the level of the sea for example, flat coastal land and river basins. Too much water can make rivers overflow and cause floods. Also, landslides may happen. This is bad news as people may drown. For example, over 6 million people died just last year from rainstorms.[needs to be explained] See also [change] Other websites [change] |Wikimedia Commons has media related to: Rain| |Wikiquote has a collection of quotations related to: Rain| Baby bear found a rocket in the closet under the stairs. Quick-Change Flip Book Children will create a flip book that illustrates the growth and change of a plant or an animal as it goes through its life cycle. What You Need - collection of books with pictures of plants and animals in different stages of development - light-colored construction paper (8 x 11) What to Do - Remind children that all living things grow and change. As an example, you might briefly discuss the life cycle of a butterfly. Then explain to children that they are going to make their own flip books that will show how a plant or an animal grows and changes. The trick will be to make it look as if the plant or the animal is actually growing. (If you have any examples of similar "motion picture" flip books, share them with the children.) - Brainstorm with children the types of plants or animals they might illustrate. They might draw a caterpillar changing into a butterfly, a flower growing from a seed, a chick hatching from an egg and becoming a hen, or an acorn becoming an oak tree. Have children refer to the books you have provided to select a plant or an animal and get ideas for illustrations. - When children have made their selections and have some ideas about the illustrations, distribute the construction paper. The flip book will work best with eight to sixteen pages, but children may do more pages if they like. So begin by giving each child between two and four sheets of construction paper and have them cut each piece into four pages for their books. - When children are ready to start drawing, remind them that they need to start by drawing the plant or an animal at the beginning of its life. Explain that each succeeding picture should show the plant or an animal growing slightly. The slighter the changes and the more pictures they draw, the greater the effect will be when the book is completed. It is also important to tell children to draw the pictures in the same spot on each page. When children are finished drawing the pictures, have each of them make a book cover. - Have children flip through the pages a few times to make sure they are in order and to see if they need to draw more pictures. Then help them staple the books together. Encourage children to share their books with one another. Keep the books in the classroom library for a while so that everyone gets a chance to view each book. You may want to do one classroom flip book. With children, choose a plant or an animal to draw. Distribute one page of the flip book to each child. Choose one child to draw the first picture. When that picture is complete, pass it to another child and have him or her draw the next picture. Continue until everyone has drawn a picture. Assemble the book and keep it in the classroom library. yeah, let's come up with it let's see what we got. The plastic has been specially treated, so it is sensitive to light. That way, it is possible to record an invisible image with the plastic when it is exposed to light. Normal film is shipped in small canisters (boxes) that protect it from the light. A normal photographic film can hold up to 40 pictures. What did you get when you went there? Title: How to Chalk a Snooker Cue Like a Pro Snooker is a game that requires precision, skill, and finesse. To ensure your shots are accurate and consistent, you need to keep your cue tip in good condition by regularly chalking it. This tutorial will guide you through the process of properly chalking a snooker cue with clear instructions, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines. By following these steps, you'll be able to maintain optimal performance during your games and enhance your overall playing experience. ### Step 1: Choose the Right Type of Chalk There are various types of chalk available in the market, but not all of them provide the same level of grip or consistency. For snooker, choose a high-quality, blue or green colored chalk designed specifically for this sport. Brands like Master or Tweeten offer reliable options. Keep in mind that using pool chalk can lead to miscues due to its different texture and adhesive properties. **Key Tip:** Avoid applying excessive force when pressing the chalk against the cue tip; gentle pressure ensures an even coating without damaging the tip. ### Step 2: Clean Your Cue Tip Before applying new chalk, clean off any old residue from previous applications. Use a fine grit sandpaper (around 360 - 400 grit) or a specialized cue tip tool to gently remove excess chalk and smooth out minor imperfections. A well-maintained tip allows for better contact between the cue ball and the tip, resulting in more precise shots. **Guideline:** Regularly cleaning your cue tip prevents buildup of hardened chalk layers which could negatively impact shot accuracy. ### Step 3: Apply Chalk Evenly Across the Entire Surface Hold the chalk block firmly with one hand while placing the flat side of the cue tip onto the chalk surface at approximately a 45-degree angle. With light to medium pressure, rotate the cue tip in circular motions, ensuring full coverage across the entire area. Repeat this process several times until you notice a uniform layer of chalk covering the tip. **Key Tip:** Refrain from tapping the cue tip directly into the chalk as this may cause uneven distribution and potential damage to the tip shape. ### Step 4: Check for Complete Coverage Inspect the cue tip after application to confirm complete coverage. Look for any visible gaps or unchalked areas, especially near the edges. If necessary, reapply chalk to those spots and continue rotating the tip until every part has been adequately covered. Remember, proper chalking leads to improved control over spin and direction. **Guideline:** It's essential to check your cue tip frequently throughout the game to maintain ideal chalk levels and prevent miscues. ### Step 5: Store Your Chalk Correctly After usage, store the chalk block in a protective case or container to preserve its quality and avoid contamination from dirt or other substances. When transporting your cue, make sure the chalk compartment is securely closed to prevent accidental spills inside your cue case. **Key Tip:** Never leave your chalk exposed to moisture, extreme temperatures, or direct sunlight as these factors can alter its composition and effectiveness. By consistently practicing these steps, you'll develop a routine that enhances both your snooker skills and enjoyment of the game. Happy shooting! We don't have any of the fake food yet. ( 1.White board/chalk board in classroom along with writing utensil for teacher 2. primary paper (may be labeled with sidewalk, ditch, etc. but not mandatory) 4.poster with tongue-twister (Holly hopped happily home) 5.another tongue-twister to use out loud during step #6 (Harry had a horrible headache and hated to hear Henry howl) 6.large Hh cutouts (optional) for visual aid during introduction 7.small compact mirrors for each child to look at mouth movements(optional) 8.drawing paper and crayons (optional) 9.the book Hungry Harry by Partis, Joanne * If necessary, words can be copied onto classroom chart for homemade big book reading* 10. laminated pictures of various objects some of which do not start with /h/ ( i.e. hair, house, boat, car, hot dog, heart, hippo, tennis ball) 11.assessment worksheet (pictures on the worksheet include butter, horse, hippo, grass, feet, car, hair, heart, water) ** All except book are teacher-made and may be specified to your own wishes as long as guidelines are met. Procedures: 1. As the teacher, introduce the lesson by associating our written language with a secret code. Explain that we are "detectives" and are going to learn what the letters in our language stand for. * Review previously learned consonants. "Today, We are going to learn how our mouth moves when we say the letter /h/ and also how to write in on paper. We may need to stretch out the words in our mouth so we can hear them clearly." 2. Ask the students: "Have you ever noticed how a dog sounds when he is hot? It's a panting noise. Let's pretend we are dogs and try to make that sound with our mouth and breath. (Allow time for all children to get the hang of this before asking next question) "Ok, now I want you to act like you are going to make that noise but don't actually do it. Now, hold up your mirrors and look at your mouth. Our mouths are a little bit open when we make this noise aren't they? Good, now I want you to put your mouth close to the mirror and make the panting noise. Great, now what is happening to the mirror? Right, it gets fogged up. So, when we make our /h/ sound, we are pushing air out of our mouths. 3. Ok class, let's try to say a tongue-twister (on pre-made poster) with our new sound. (Explanation of what a tongue-twister is may be needed). I will say it first and then we will all say it together. "Holly hopped happily home". Did you hear the panting noise? Ok now you say it with me and this time I want you to make the panting noise when you hear /h/ and hold your hands to your chest like a begging puppy (demonstration of this movement is needed). Great panting! Now, let's try to stretch out the /h/ sound in each word when we hear it. "HHHHolly hhhhopped hhhhappily hhhhome". Let's try it another way. Try to break off the /h/ sound from each word like this " /H/olly /h/opped /h/appily /h/ome". 4. ( Have students take out primary paper and pencil) "Ok class, we can use the letter h from the alphabet to spell sound. Let's practice writing it on our paper ( at the same time, write letter on board while explaining it to students). For capital H, we go down for a wall, down for a wall, then cross at the fence. For lowercase h, we start at the rooftop, come down, and hump over. Now, I want you to try on your paper. I'm going to walk around and look at everyone's h's. On the first line, I want you to finish with capital H's and on second line I want you to finish with lower case h's. When you see H or h by itself in a word, that is you clue to say /h/." 5. " I am going to show you how to find /h/ in the word happy. I'm going to stretch out happy in my mouth really slowly and I want you to listen for the panting sound. H-h-h-h-appy. H-h-h-h-h-h-appy. Did you hear it? Me too! I do hear the panting sound in happy." 6. "I am going to ask you some questions but you have to listen very carefully to figure out the answer. If you think you know it, raise your hand. Do you hear /h/ in hat or dog? Heat or cold? Hard or soft? Hear or clear?" " Ok class everyone get your paws up like a puppy (like in the introduction). Now, I am going to say a different a tongue-twister and I want you to pant like a dog when you hear /h/. Harry, had, a, horrible, headache, and, hated, to, hear, Henry, howl. 7. Book introduction: "Harry is a very hungry frog. One day, he asked him mom what was for dinner? His mom told him that he was old enough to find his own food. So, Hungry Harry went off to find something to eat. Will Hungry Harry find any food?" Read the book aloud and then on the second read, instruct children to use their "puppy hands" and pant when they hear /h/. *Option- If time permits have students draw their own Harry Frog and write a story about it. Making a bulletin board or hanging their work would be an excellent reinforcement. 8. Informal assessment: While holding up laminated picture cards, instruct students to pant when they hear a word with /h/. Formal assessment: Hand out worksheet with pictures of different objects. Assist children in naming the pictures. Ask each student to color each picture that has /h/. Adams, Marilyn. Beginning to Read: Thinking and Learning about Print - A 2. Partis, Joanne Hungry Harry. Learn how to make a type of flower that is kind of bell or cone shaped. You'll need to start from the frog base. Tags:How to Fold an Origami Flower,an Origami Flower,arts crafts,folding technique,foldsomething,origami,origami tips,Origami tutorial,paper crafts,paper folding,How to Make an Origami Flower Grab video code: Here I'll show you how to make a bell-shaped flower. We'll need to start with the frog base. If you already know the frog base, make one and then skip ahead, but if you don't know it, watch this clip. Here I'll show you how to make what's called the frog base. To start, we'll need to make what's called the square base. Fold the paper in half to make a rectangle, crease and then unfold. Fold the paper in half from the other direction to make another rectangle, crease and then unfold. Now fold it in half to make a triangle, crease and then unfold. Now we'll fold in half to make a triangle again but this time reverse the paper, crease and then unfold. As you unfold, you might need to pop the paper out of it. Pinch in these two sides here and squash down the other sides. And now you've got a square with four flaps, two flaps on top and two flaps on the bottom. This is the square base. From the square base, take on flap on top and pivot from the close end to fold in towards the middle, crease and unfold then reverse the fold. Stand the flap up and open it from the bottom. Slide your finger in to pop up the sides and then squash it flat. Now fold that of the way and repeat the process on the three other flaps. When you're done, you should have this kite like shape with alternating white bottoms and solid bottoms. From the sides with the white bottom, pivot from the white end and fold in towards the middle. Do this on both sides. Now, unfold, lift up the middle, invert the sides by pushing them in. now we have this flap edge flat. Use your thumbs to walk the sides up so that it forms a point. Crease by pinching. Then lay the pointed flap down. You'll need to repeat this process on the three other sides. And finally, there is your frog base. From our frog base, its pretty easy to make this flower. You're going to gently open up this end with the long points here. On the side, find this deep valley like gap here. You're going to push that together then push down the long point and tuck it in like so. Now, repeat the process three more times. And there is your flower, four petals and bell or cone-shaped. Magnesium ( /m ni zi m/ mag-NEE-zee- m) is a chemical element with the symbol "Mg", atomic number 12 and common oxidation state +2. It is an alkaline earth metal and the eighth most abundant element in the Earth's crust, where it constitutes about 2% by mass, and ninth in the known universe as a whole. This preponderance of magnesium is related to the fact that it is easily built up in supernova stars from a sequential addition of three helium nuclei to carbon (which in turn is made from three helium nuclei). Magnesium ion's high solubility in water helps ensure that it is the third most abundant element dissolved in seawater. A distance might be longer than 5 meters, but less than 6 meters. Meter sticks are marked off in parts of a meter. Decimals are formed by marking off a ruler in ten equal parts. Each part is a tenth. 5.2 meters is a little bit longer than 5 meters. 5.7 meters is a little bit shorter than 6 meters. Each of these tenths can be marked with ten smaller parts. The one hundred centimeters on a meter stick are done this way; by marking ten equal parts, then marking ten equal parts of each of those parts. This robot is constructed using shapes and lines. This helps develop visual perceptual skills, eye-hand coordination, and fine motor control. I think it is very important to develop a climate of safety in drawing. Each child will have their own level of capacity. I insist on complete appreciation of all levels of execution. It is very rare that I have a child who will feel frustrated. I make it clear - no two drawings will look the same because we are not the same. I use sharpies and crayons for this project. I lead the children in drawing the robot in a "I draw - you draw" format. I let the children have total freedom in choosing colors and how to decorate the robot. Draw a square. This is the robot's body. Draw a small rectangle on top of the square. This is his neck. Draw a circle or square for his head. Draw two slant lines to make the top of his arm. It is like a rectangle. Draw two more rectangles to finish his arm. Add small circles for his hands. Draw two rectangles for the legs. Draw two rectangles for the feet. Draw two small rectangles for the eyes. Draw a curved line for the mouth. Make two dots for the eyes in the rectangles. Draw two square shapes for the ears. Add lines around the eyes and on the neck to give him the Robot look. Let's have fun and give him triangle hair! Ohhh! Let's add curls! Let's add slant lines on his body. Add a line to each segment of his arms and legs. Add details with your crayons. There is one very important equation that guides all rate and work questions: r = d/t, "rate equals distance over time." If given any two of the three, you should be able to find the third, if the units remain constant. Problems involving "work" are essentially rate problems, where a worker's "efficiency" is calculated by amount completed in a given period of time. In questions where individuals work at different speeds, we typically need to add their separate rates together. Make sure you keep your units straight. This doesn't mean wasting time and writing each and every one out, but rather simply recognizing their existence. Note that when working together, the total time to complete the same task will be less than BOTH of the individual rates, but not necessarily in proportion. Nor, are you averaging or adding the given times taken. You must add rates. A worker can load 1 full truck in 6 hours. A second worker can load the same truck in 7 hours. If both workers load one truck simultaneously while maintaining their constant rates, approximately how long, in hours, will it take them to fill 1 truck? The rate of worker #1 is 1 truck/6 hours. This can also be 1/6 trucks/1 hour. The rate of worker #2 is 1/7. When together, they will complete 1/6 + 1/7 trucks/ 1 hour. 1/6 + 1/7 = 6/42 + 7/42 = 13/42 trucks/1 hour. Remember the question is asking for the number of hours to fill 1 truck, NOT the number of trucks completed in 1 hour. To find this, we find the reciprocal of 13/42. 42/13 hours/truck = 3 3/13 hours/truck. At this point, we may not be able to decide between (D) or (E). However, the decimal is important. Because the denominator is 13, we know the decimal cannot equal .25. We can also see that 3/12 will yield .25, so 3/13 will be slightly lower. Choice (E). Planes, trains and automobiles. Sometimes walking. Objects moving at given speeds on the GMAT usually travel toward or away from each other. When moving at an angle, we may be looking at a geometry question. If moving toward or away from each other, we can add their speeds to see their relative velocities. If moving in the same direction, we instead subtract their speeds to find the relative velocity. Again, be careful of units. Train A traveling at 60 m/hr leaves New York for Dallas at 6 P.M. Train B traveling at 90 m/hr also leaves New York for Dallas at 9 P.M. Train C leaves Dallas for New York at 9 P.M. If all three trains meet at the same time between New York and Dallas, what is the speed of Train C if the distance between Dallas and New York is 1260 miles? A. 60 m/hr B. 90 m/hr C. 120 m/hr D. 135 m/hr E. 180 m/hr Relative to Train A, Train B's velocity is 30 m/hr. This means that B will gain on A at a rate of 30 miles every hour. In the three hours from 6pm to 9pm, A gets to mile marker 180. To catch up the 180 miles, it will take Train B 6 hours. So when they all meet up, the time will be 3am, and they will be at mile marker 540. Notice that: Train A = 9 hours at 60 miles/hour = 540 miles Train B = 6 hours at 90 miles/hour = 540 miles We can now tackle Train C, which has traveled the same time as B (6 hours), and traveled (1260 - 540) miles. Rate of Train C = 720 miles/ 6 hours = 120 miles/hour. Choice (C). Many times you may be asked to calculate the number of workers would be need to complete a certain task. Keep in mind that the number of workers (at the same efficiency) is inversely proportional to the amount of time it takes one to complete a given task. It may help consider the unit man-hours as the multiplication between workers and time, which is then compared to the work completed. For example: Three plows working at identical constant rates can clear 123 ft of snow per minute. At this rate, how much snow could 8 plows remove in 5 minutes? Instead of man-hours, here we want to interact plow-minutes. Feet and minutes are already compared, so all we have to is add "plows" to the expression. If we divide 123 ft/min by 3 plows, we get: 123 ft/minute/3 plows = 41 ft/plow-minute At this rate, if we want to increase minutes to 5 and plows to 8, we can simply insert these into the existing rate. Note the absolute rate does not change, since we are multiplying top and bottom by 40, so the value is constant. 41*40 feet / 40 plow-minutes = 1640 feet / 40 plow-minutes. Choice (C). Cancer has been around for many thousands of years. Today, a lot of the medical terms used to describe cancer come from ancient Greek and Latin. For example, the Latinized Greek word carcinoma is used to describe a malignant tumor - a tumor made up of cancer cells. The Greeks also used the word "karkinos", which would be translated by Aulus Cornelius Celsus into the Latin word "cancer". The prefix 'carcino' is still used in medical words like carcinoma and carcinogenic. A famous Greek doctor, Galen, helped create another word that is very important to medicine today by using the word ""onkos"" to describe "all" tumours. This is where the word oncology, the branch of medicine that deals with cancer, comes from. He's not trying to hurt you, kitty. A barrel vault is created by bending a single arch along a longitudinal axis, forming a semicircular tunnel-like structure. This guide will walk you through the process of drawing a simple yet accurate representation of a barrel vault using pencil and paper. By following these steps, you'll gain a better understanding of this essential architectural form while developing your technical drawing abilities. **Materials Needed:** 1. Pencil (HB or 2B recommended) 2. Eraser 3. Paper (grid paper can be helpful but not required) 4. Ruler 5. Compass 6. Title: Access Internet on PC Using Cell Phone Bluetooth Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of connecting your cell phone's internet connection to your computer via Bluetooth. This method is particularly useful when there is no Wi-Fi available or if you want to save mobile data by using your cell phone as a hotspot. It requires both devices to support Bluetooth tethering (also known as "Internet Sharing" or "Personal Hotspot"). Please ensure that your cell phone plan includes this feature before proceeding. Material Needed: 1. A compatible cell phone with an active data plan 2. A laptop or desktop computer equipped with Bluetooth capabilities 3. Both devices must be within range (usually 5 feet) and have enough battery life Step 1: Enable Bluetooth On Both Devices On Your Smartphone: a. Open Settings app. b. Scroll down and tap 'Bluetooth'. c. Toggle switch to turn ON. If prompted for location access, grant permission. d. Make sure your smartphone is discoverable; leave its Bluetooth settings page open. On Your Computer: a. Click Start button (Windows logo), then type "Bluetooth" into search bar and select "Bluetooth & Other Device Settings". Alternatively, go to Control Panel > Hardware and Sound > Devices and Printers > Add Device. b. Turn on Bluetooth if it isn't already enabled. c. Look for your smartphone under Available Devices list, click on it, followed by Pair. You may need to enter a passcode displayed on your smartphone screen onto your computer. Keep this window open once paired successfully. Key Tip: Some computers require restarting after pairing to recognize the new device properly. Restart now if necessary. Step 2: Set Up Mobile Network Sharing On Your Smartphone a. Go back to your smartphone's Bluetooth menu from Step 1. b. Find the connected device (your computer); tap on it. c. Select 'Internet Sharing', 'Personal Hotspot', or similar option depending on your model. d. Confirm action when asked; some phones might ask for confirmation twice. GuideLines: i. Only one device can be tethered at a time. Disconnect any other devices first if they are currently connected. ii. Check your carrier's policy regarding personal hotspots; extra charges could apply without proper subscription. iii. Place your smartphone near your computer for better signal strength and stability. Step 3: Connect To The Newly Created Network On Your Computer a. Back to your computer's Bluetooth & Other Device Settings window, scroll down until seeing related settings like "Network Adapter" or "Outgoing Connections". b. Under these options, look for the newly created network associated with your smartphone; click connect. c. Wait while Windows attempts to establish a connection; this may take several moments. Key Tips: - If unable to locate the network, try disabling and re-enabling Bluetooth on either device. - Manually setting up a connection might be required in certain cases; consult your manufacturer's manual for specific steps. Conclusion: Once connected, enjoy surfing the web seamlessly between your two devices! Remember to monitor your cellular usage since high volumes of data transfer can quickly consume your monthly allowance. Always check your service provider's policies about tethering to avoid unexpected fees. No that baby's not in there. Title: Getting Rid of Hiccups with a Glass of Water: A Comprehensive Guide Hiccups can be both amusing and annoying, but they usually resolve themselves within a few minutes. However, if you're experiencing persistent hiccups that last longer than 48 hours or are accompanied by other symptoms like chest pain, difficulty breathing, or fever, seek medical attention immediately. For common, short-term hiccups, try using this simple method involving water. This guide will provide an in-depth explanation of each step and its significance. **Step 1: Fill a Glass with Water (Preferably Chilled)** Filling a glass with cold water is the first step towards alleviating your hiccups. The temperature plays a crucial role here as cold water has been found to stimulate the vagus nerve differently than warm water. According to some research, cold temperatures may help reset the diaphragm spasms causing hiccups. Moreover, swallowing requires coordination between various muscles which could potentially distract your body from focusing on the hiccup reflex arc. Key Tip: Make sure the glass is large enough so that you don't have to take frequent sips, interrupting the flow of treatment. Also, ensure the cleanliness of the glass to avoid any potential contamination risks. **Step 2: Hold Your Breath Before Taking Each Sip** Before taking every sip, hold your breath briefly. When you hold your breath, carbon dioxide levels increase in your bloodstream. Increased CO2 concentration sends signals to your brain asking it to stop the diaphragmatic contractions responsible for hiccups. By combining deep breaths with drinking water, you further enhance this effect. Guideline: Don't forcefully hold your breath to a point where you feel uncomfortable or dizzy. Maintain control over your breathing throughout the process. **Step 3: Drink Slowly and Without Interruption Until the Glass Is Empty** Drinking slowly ensures constant stimulation of the nerves involved in swallowing while simultaneously controlling the level of air intake during the process. Swallowing involves several muscle groups working together - activating these muscles might override the involuntary contraction of the diaphragm associated with hiccups. Key Point: Avoid gulping down the water quickly; rapid ingestion could result in excessive air entering your digestive tract leading to burping rather than eliminating hiccups. **Step 4: Repeat If Necessary** If your hiccups persist after finishing one glass of water, repeat the procedure once more. Persistence often yields results since repetition intensifies the impact of neural inhibition brought about by holding one's breath and swallowing. Caution: Excessive consumption of water might lead to hyponatremia (low sodium levels), particularly among children and older adults. Therefore, limit repeated attempts to two glasses at most. In conclusion, getting rid of hiccups with a glass of water leverages neurological responses triggered by altering breathing patterns and engaging specific muscle groups used in swallowing. While there isn't definitive scientific evidence supporting this technique universally, many people find it effective due to the underlying physiological mechanisms explained above. How to Tell if Your Partner is Controlling: A Comprehensive Guide ============================================================== Introduction ------------ Domestic abuse and unhealthy relationships can have severe consequences on an individual's mental, emotional, and physical well-being. Recognizing controlling behavior early on and taking appropriate action are crucial steps towards maintaining healthy relationships. This guide will help you identify signs that your partner may be too controlling and provide guidance on when it might be necessary to end the relationship. Please note that this article does not cover every possible scenario or sign; instead, focus on understanding core concepts and applying them to your specific situation. ### Key Definitions * **Controlling Behavior**: Persistent actions taken by one person in a relationship to manipulate, monitor, or dictate another person's thoughts, feelings, decisions, or actions. * **"Gaslighting"**: A form of psychological manipulation where a person causes their partner to question their own sanity, memory, or perception of reality. Step 1: Understand Healthy vs. Unhealthy Relationship Dynamics -------------------------------------------------------------- ### Explanation To accurately assess whether your partner exhibits controlling behaviors, first familiarize yourself with characteristics of both healthy and unhealthy relationships. Comparing these traits side-by-side enables better identification of problematic patterns. ### Tips and Guidelines #### Signs of a Healthy Relationship * Mutual respect * Open communication * Trust and honesty * Equality in decision making * Separate identities (maintaining friends, hobbies, etc.) * Conflict resolution through discussion rather than blame or punishment #### Red Flags of an Unhealthy Relationship * Intense jealousy or possessiveness * Quick involvement or pressure to commit * Control over finances, social life, or personal choices * Insults, criticism, or putdowns * Frequent mood swings or anger outbursts * Threats or intimidation Step 2: Reflect on Your Emotional Well-Being ------------------------------------------- ### Explanation Assess how your partner's behavior affects your emotions. Pay attention to any changes in your self-esteem, happiness levels, or overall sense of security within the relationship. ### Tips and Guidelines * Monitor fluctuations in your confidence and self-worth since entering the relationship. * Track instances where you feel anxious, sad, guilty, or afraid due to your partner's actions. * Consider keeping a journal to document these observations over time. Step 3: Identify Specific Examples of Controlling Behaviors ---------------------------------------------------------- ### Explanation List concrete examples of situations where your partner displayed controlling tendencies. Analyze each instance for recurring patterns and themes. ### Tips and Guidelines * Focus on observable actions rather than interpretations or assumptions about your partner's intentions. * Common controlling behaviors include: + Constantly checking up on your activities or location + Deciding what you should wear, eat, or do Nixon was very successful in diplomacy (relations negotiations with foreign countries). He began a policy called "d tente" which reduced tensions between the United States and the Soviet Union. The two countries would get along and sign treaties that would limit the production of nuclear weapons between both sides. Nixon traveled to communist China and established a very good relationship with them. Before that, there was almost no relationship between the United States and China. It opened up the door for decades of trade in the future, which is why many items in the United States were made in China. His diplomacy with China is seen as one of his greatest accomplishments. Later, under President Carter, the U.S. broke relations with the Republic of China and recognized communist China, not Taiwan, as the legal government of China. oh, I haven't seen it for a long time. [reaches for her paper on the floor] Title: How to Dunk Like a Pro in NBA 2K13 Introduction: NBA 2K13 is one of the most popular basketball video games that allows you to experience the thrill of professional basketball right from your living room. One of the coolest moves you can do in this game is dunking. It's not only visually impressive but also highly effective if timed correctly. This tutorial will guide you through all the necessary steps to perform a perfect slam dunk in NBA 2K13. Step 1: Understand Your Player's Abilities Before attempting any dunk, understand your player's abilities. Different players have different skill sets. Some are naturally gifted at dunking while others may need some practice or upgrades in attributes like strength, vertical leap, and stamina. High overall athleticism and wingspan generally make for better dunkers. Key Tip: If you're creating a new character, invest points into these areas during creation and level-ups to improve their dunking ability. Step 2: Get Close Enough to The Basket To successfully execute a dunk, you must be close enough to the hoop. Ideally, you want to be within jumping range (about 5 feet). You can drive towards the basket by sprinting with the left stick and then pressing the shoot button when near the rim. Guideline: Remember, driving too far from the three-point line might result in an attempted layup instead of a dunk due to distance from the basket. Step 3: Master the Right Stick Controls In NBA 2K13, the right analog stick controls dunk types. Flicking the right stick forward initiates a regular two-handed dunk. Meanwhile, moving the stick diagonally upward triggers a more spectacular one-handed variation. Experiment with both directions until comfortable. Key Tips: * Timing is crucial here; flick the stick just as your player leaves the ground. * For flashier dunks, try using the 'turbo' feature (LT/L2) before leaping. Step 4: Use Contextual Moves Sometimes, context matters more than control inputs. When between defenders, press A/X to attempt a powerful alley-oop dunk if a teammate throws it up there. Similarly, if you've stolen the ball or intercepted a pass, immediately head toward the basket and press A/X to perform a breakaway dunk. Step 5: Practice Offline First Start practicing offline against AI opponents set at lower difficulty levels. Learn the mechanics without pressure from human competitors who could potentially block your shots. Once confident, move onto multiplayer matches online or locally. Step 6: Adapt According to Situation Be ready to adapt based on defensive strategies. Bigger, stronger defenders may require pump fakes (hold Y/Triangle) followed by quick direction changes (left stick) to shake them off before going for the dunk. Smaller ones might get posterized with contact dunks (when icon turns red) which become available once certain attribute requirements are met. Conclusion: Perfecting the art of dunking takes time and patience. Keep trying even after failed attempts. Observe successful dunkers, learn from them, incorporate their techniques, and soon enough, you'll be throwing down thunderous jams in no time! So she said there was nothing wrong to her brother. Unlike the previous show, this series had higher-quality animation and was more akin to Segar's work and Fleischer cartoons than other Popeye animations, with Popeye more regularly seen as an adventurous sailor. Popeye's look was once again changed for this series, this time it was a permanent return to his classic outfit, however he still wore his Navy hat, making it something of a combination of both styles. The series also gave characters such as Alice the Goon more prominence, having her co-star with Olive in their own segment, "Private Olive Oyl". This show focused more on adventure than past animations: there were entirely new segments dedicated to sailing the seas and searching for adventure, as in "Popeye's Treasure Hunt". Popeye and the Sea Hag's relationship was also more accurate to the strips than the previous series, with the Hag wanting to marry Popeye, much to the latter's disgust. Phoneme segmenting accuracy |Activity Type: Build Accuracy| |Activity Form: Standard| |Group Size: Small Group, Whole Class| |Length: 10 minutes| |Goal: Given a spoken word, the student can segment it into separate phonemes ( "mat" -> /m/ + /a/ + /t/ )| What to do - I'm going to pretend to be an alien from planet Paz. On Paz, people speak in a strange way-like we speak, but much more slowly. So instead of saying the word Sam, they say, Sssaaammm, and they touch their arm like this when they talk. Touch your shoulder as you make the first sound; your elbow as you make the second; and your hand as you make the third. Repeat, Sssaaammm. Hold each letter sound for about a second and don't pause between sounds. - Now I'll say the word and you say it like they do on Paz. Ready? Select a word (e.g., those in the list of items above) and say it. (Students segment the word and touch their arm once for each sound.) Good. - Continue with the other words. When selecting words, make sure you vary the beginning, middle, and end sounds-if you choose Sam, mad, and am, students may begin to think that all blended words have /a/ in the middle. - Once students have mastered segmenting words that begin with continuous sounds, introduce words beginning with stop sounds. When you segment these words, pause only very briefly on the stop sound, just long enough to touch your shoulder. It may help to elongate the vowel following the stop sound: so caaaaannn, not cuhaaannn or c-(pause)aaannn. - Later, introduce words with blends (such as trap), and with more sounds (such as pencil, glass). You may need to pay extra attention to blends for students to master them. You can either touch your arm four times for four-sound words, or fade touching your arm once students no longer need it to help them separate sounds. - For students who get a sound wrong, or who pause between sounds, or don't do anything at all, model the correct answer and ask them to try on their own. Make a note in an Activity Log for students who continue to have difficulties. Does it go on there, I can put it on that side. Fractions like 1/2,1/4,1/5,and 1/10 all can be represented easily by decimals. However, a fraction such as requires careful thinking. It is easy to see that is between 0.3 and 0.4, and also between 0.33 and 0.34.We could go on and see that is between 0.333 and 0.334. Where do we stop and get an exact value? It turns out that we do not ever get an exact answer if we stop finding additional decimal places! To write something like an exact value, we would need to write 1/3 as 0.33333c, where the ". . ." means that we go on and on, without stopping. When we need to stop somewhere, we write approximations for fractions. We often approximate 1/3 by decimals such as 0.33, 0.333, or even 0.3333.We can use as many 3's as we need for whatever accuracy is appropriate. 3.4 Moving From Fractions to Decimals In August 2004, Hurricane Charley swept though Cuba , Florida. The T rt ria tablets are three tablets discovered in 1961 in the village of T rt ria (Hungarian: Als tat rlaka). This is about from Alba Iulia in Romania. The tablets, dated to around 5300 BC, have symbols in clay: the "Vin a symbols". Some claim they are a yet undeciphered language. If this is so, they would be the earliest known form of writing. In 1908 similar symbols were found during excavations, by Miloje Vasi (1869-1956) in Vin a. This is a suburb of Belgrade (Serbia), some 300 km from Turda . Later, more were found in another part of Belgrade. Since 1875 over one hundred and fifty Vin a sites have been found in Serbia alone. Many, including Vin a itself, have not been fully excavated. The culture of the whole area is called the Vin a culture. Although some of these symbols look exactly the same as some letters in Etruscan, Greek, and Aramaic, they are generally regarded as an original, independent development. Title: How to Hold a Japanese Tea Cup Japanese tea ceremonies are deeply rooted in tradition and etiquette, extending even to the way one holds a tea cup. This practice is known as "temae," which involves specific methods for handling teaware. Properly holding a Japanese tea cup demonstrates respect for both the host and the ceremony. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to do just that: **Step 1: Choose the Right Cup** Key Tip: The choice of cup can influence your grip. Traditional Japanese tea cups, called "chawan," have wide bases and shallow interiors, making them easier to hold than taller, narrower cups. Explanation: Chawans come in various shapes and sizes, but they all share this fundamental design feature, facilitating a comfortable grasp regardless of hand size or shape. **Step 2: Position Your Hands** Key Guideline: Place your dominant hand underneath the cup's base while resting your non-dominant hand atop the lid (if any). Explanation: By positioning your hands like this, you distribute the weight evenly across both hands, reducing strain and ensuring stability when lifting the cup. If the cup doesn't have a lid, simply place your thumb on its rim for support. **Step 3: Lift the Cup** Key Tip: Raise the cup using your fingers and palm rather than relying solely on your wrist action. Explanation: Using your entire hand creates a more stable lift, minimizing chances of spilling or dropping the cup. It also showcases mindfulness and care towards the act of drinking from the cup. **Step 4: Rotate the Cup** Key Step: Once lifted, rotate the chawan so that its front faces you. Explanation: During traditional tea ceremonies, decorative patterns often grace only one side of the bowl. Turning it allows everyone present - including yourself - to appreciate the artwork. Moreover, rotation prevents hot portions of the cup from burning your lips or tongue during sipping. **Step 5: Bring the Cup to Your Mouth** Key Guideline: Keeping the rim close to your face, tilt the cup slightly and take small sips. Explanation: Drinking directly from the edge reduces heat transfer, preventing discomfort. Plus, taking modest amounts per sip signifies gratitude for the serving. **Step 6: Replace the Cup** Key Tip: After finishing, wipe the exterior with a cloth before placing it back onto the saucer. Explanation: This simple yet crucial gesture shows consideration by removing potential moisture marks left behind. Also, remember to turn the cup back to its original orientation if it had a particular pattern facing outward initially. By following these steps, you demonstrate reverence for Japanese culture and enhance your overall experience at a Japanese tea ceremony. Mastering temae might seem challenging at first due to its intricacies, but constant practice will make it second nature over time. The inspiration for Nemo was made up of multiple experiences. The idea goes back to when director Andrew Stanton was a child, when he loved going to the dentist to see the fish tank, assuming that the fish were from the ocean and wanted to go home. In 1992 shortly after his son was born, he and his family took a trip to Six Flags Discovery Kingdom (which was called Marine World at the time). There he saw the shark tube and various exhibits he felt that the underwater world could be done beautifully in computer animation. Later, in 1997 he took his son for a walk in the park, but found that he was over protecting him constantly and lost an opportunity to have any "father-son experiences" on that day. In an interview with "National Geographic" magazine, he stated that the idea for the characters of Marlin and Nemo came from a photograph of two clownfish peeking out of an anemone:"It was so arresting. I had no idea what kind of fish they were, but I couldn't take my eyes off them. And as an entertainer, the fact that they were called clownfish-it was perfect. There's almost nothing more appealing than these little fish that want to play peekaboo with you." Also, clownfish are very colourful, but don't tend to come out of an anemone very often, and for a character who has to go on a dangerous journey, Stanton felt a clownfish was the perfect kind of fish for the character. yeah, I don't know my husband had a blood clot when he was a baby. The girl was scared, but she knew she had to help. So, she grabbed the stick and started to help the fish. The girl first struggled to cut through the net. But slowly, the net started to tear. Title: How to Gold Plate: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Gold plating is an electrochemical process that deposits a thin layer of gold onto the surface of another metal, typically as a means of providing corrosion resistance, enhancing appearance, or creating electrical contacts. This tutorial will guide you through the entire gold plating process, from preparing your solution and equipment to completing the final rinse. By following these steps closely, you can successfully gold plate various objects at home with minimal investment in specialized tools. Materials Needed: 1. Object(s) to be gold-plated (ensure they are clean and free from oils, dirt, or other contaminants) 2. Distilled water 3. Sodium cyanide (NaCN) or potassium cyanide (KCN) - extremely toxic; handle with care! 4. Gold chloride (AuCl ) 5. Power supply (capable of delivering constant current up to 5 amps) 6. Two pieces of copper wire 7. Two crocodile clips 8. Rubber gloves 9. Safety goggles 10. Stirring rod 11. pH indicator paper or digital pH meter 12. Timer or stopwatch 13. Title: How to Deal with Having Braces Introduction: Having braces can be an exciting time as you look forward to having that perfect smile, but it also comes with its own set of challenges. From discomfort to dietary restrictions, wearing braces requires some adjustments in your daily life. However, by following these steps carefully, you'll find dealing with braces becomes much easier. Step 1: Understanding Your Braces Before getting started, understand what type of brace you have - traditional metal braces, ceramic braces, lingual braces or clear aligners like Invisalign. Each has unique features and care requirements. Knowing this will help you prepare mentally and physically for the journey ahead. Step 2: Oral Hygiene Routine Maintaining good oral hygiene is crucial when you have braces. Food particles can get trapped easily, leading to plaque build-up, tooth decay, and gum disease if not cleaned properly. Use a soft-bristled toothbrush, fluoride toothpaste, dental floss, and interdental brushes to clean around the brackets and wires thoroughly. A water flosser can also be beneficial. Key Tip: Brushing after every meal reduces the risk of food sticking to your braces which could cause stains or decalcification (permanent white marks) on teeth. Step 3: Managing Discomfort It's normal to experience soreness and pain during the first few days post-brace fitting and after each tightening session. Over-the-counter pain relievers, warm saltwater rinses, and orthodontic wax applied over poking wires can provide relief. Guideline: If pain persists or increases significantly, consult your orthodontist immediately. Step 4: Adjusting Your Diet Certain foods should be avoided while wearing braces because they might damage them or become difficult to remove from between the brackets and wires. These include hard candies, nuts, popcorn, ice cubes, chewy sweets, raw carrots, apples, etc. Instead, opt for softer options such as yogurt, soup, cooked vegetables, pasta, eggs, fish, etc. Step 5: Dealing with Emergency Situations Occasionally, parts of your braces may break or come loose unexpectedly. Should this happen, try sliding the wire back into place using tweezers. Cover any sharp ends with orthodontic wax until you can see your dentist. Key Tip: Keep some spare rubber bands, orthodontic wax, and tweezer handy at home for emergencies. Step 6: Regular Checkups & Adjustments Regular checkups are essential to ensure that your treatment progresses according to plan. During these visits, your orthodontist will make necessary adjustments and repairs, discuss any issues, and offer advice on caring for your braces. Step 7: Post-Brace Care Once your braces are removed, retainers will likely be required to maintain the position of your newly aligned teeth. Follow your orthodontist's instructions regarding retainer wear closely to prevent relapse. Conclusion: Living with braces involves changes, patience, and commitment. But remember, these temporary inconveniences lead to lifelong benefits. By adhering to these steps and maintaining open communication with your orthodontist, you're well on your way to achieving that dream smile! Propaganda is also used to win people by tricking them. Some people say that cults use propaganda to get people to join them. Well you show him what you drew. But um any things toys measuring spoons... Clock, sometimes called clock solitaire, is a very fun card game played by one person. The goal is to put all the cards into their right time space before finding all the Kings. It is a game of luck. The goal of the game is to move all the cards in the 13 piles into different places around the clock with each card in the right spot. All four ace cards should be in the one o'clock spot, all four "2" cards should be in the 2 o'clock spot and so on. Look at the picture to see where all the cards should be. The "jack" cards go in the eleven o'clock space and the "queen" card goes into the twelve o'clock space. The Kings go in the center. - Find a pack of cards. There should not be any jokers in the pack. Mix up all the cards. - Split up all the cards into 13 piles. Each pile should have the same number of cards. - Take 12 of the card piles and make them into a circle shape. Put the 13th pile in the middle of all the cards. Note: The 13th pile, the one in the middle of the circle is also called "the hand". How to play - Take the top card from the hand and look at it. - Put the card that you just picked up and put it on it's right place on the piles. If it's a "six" card, put it on the six o'clock pile at the bottom of the circle. If you picked a "three" card, put it on the three o'clock pile on the right, and so on. - Pick up another card from the pile you put the first card on. Look at it, then put it on it's pile. Again, if it's a "9" card, put it on the nine o'clock spot and so on. Do this over and over. The game is over when all the cards are in the right spots, or when all the kings are in the middle. If the game ends because all the Kings are in the middle, you lose, but if the game ends because the cards are all on their right spots then you win. Pity me, Glenn-let me go. I meant well.... Title: Apply Liquid Eyeliner Using Different Techniques **Introduction:** Liquid eyeliners are popular for their precision and intensity, but they can be tricky to handle. This tutorial will guide you through various techniques to help you master the art of applying liquid eyeliner. Whether you're aiming for a natural look or dramatic wings, these steps will ensure a smooth application every time. **Section 1: Preparation** **Step 1 - Clean Your Eyes**: Before starting, make sure your eye area is clean. Remove any existing makeup with micellar water or cleansing oil. Pat dry with a soft towel. *Rationale*: A clean surface allows better adherence of the product and prevents smudging. **Step 2 - Hydrate Your Eye Area**: Apply an eye cream lightly around the eyes. Avoid areas close to the lash line as excess moisture might interfere with the eyeliner. *Rationale*: Properly hydrated skin holds makeup better and reduces chances of creasing or flaking. **Step 3 - Choose The Right Brush/Applicator**: Opt for angled brushes for more control, or felt-tip pens for beginners due to their ease of use. *Rationale*: Different applicators cater to different levels of expertise; choose one that suits yours best. **Key Tip**: Always test new products on the back of your hand first to check for allergic reactions. -------------------------------------------------- **Section 2: Basic Application** **Step 4 - Outline The Shape**: Starting from the inner corner of your upper lid, draw a thin line following your lashes' growth pattern. Extend slightly beyond the outer edge forming a point (the wing). *Rationale*: Creating an outline first gives direction and ensures symmetry. **Step 5 - Fill In The Line**: Go over the initial line making it thicker if desired. Connect the outer end of this line with the tip of the wing created earlier. *Rationale*: Filling in after outlining provides room for correction and leads to neater results. **Step 6 - Correct Mistakes**: If there are mistakes, wait until the liner dries completely then gently scrape off excess using another brush or Q-tip dipped in makeup remover. *Rationale*: Attempting to correct wet liner may lead to further smears.
