Title: How to Evolve Budew into Roselia and Then Roserade in Pok mon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum Introduction: In this extensive guide, you will learn the process of evolving your Budew into Roselia and then further evolving it into Roserade in the popular Nintendo DS games - Pok mon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum. Each stage requires specific conditions that need to be met before evolution can occur. Follow these steps carefully to ensure successful growth for your beloved digital monsters! Step 1: Catch or Hatch a Budew The first step is obtaining a Budew. You can either catch one in the wild (found primarily in the early stages of the game) or hatch one from an egg using two compatible Pok mon such as a male Roserade with a female Roselia at the Pok tch's Vs Seeker function. Key Tips: - Be sure to save your game just before attempting to capture Budew so if you accidentally knock it out, you won't have to start over again. - If breeding, remember that only Ditto can breed with any Pok mon, regardless of gender. Step 2: Level Up Budew to Lv. 16 To initiate the evolution process, level up your Budew to at least level 16. This could happen naturally during battles or by using Rare Candies which instantly increase a Pok mon's level by one. Key Guidelines: - Make sure Budew participates in battles; experience points earned by watching others battle do not count towards levelling up. - Keep track of Budew's current level; most random encounters provide minimal EXP making grinding time-consuming. Step 3: Evolution Stage One - Budew to Roselia Once your Budew reaches level 16, it will evolve into Roselia automatically once you engage in a new battle or end the current one. No special items are required for this initial evolution. Step 4: Increase Roselia's Friendship Before proceeding to the final evolution phase, you must raise Roselia's friendship meter. To do this, interact with your Roselia frequently through various methods like playing with them in Amity Square, feeding them Pok blocks, or simply walking around holding their Pok ball. Key Points: - High friendship levels also boost happiness-related moves like Return and Frustration. - Actions that lower friendship include putting your Pok mon in daycare centers, using certain field moves, or letting them faint during battles. Step 5: Reach Night Time and Level Up Again Unlike the previous evolution, timing plays a crucial role here. Wait until nightfall (in real life), save your progress, and participate in another battle while carrying your fully friendships-maxed Roselia. As soon as you enter combat, make sure to win quickly because winning isn't necessary but staying in battle does help trigger evolution. After gaining some experience points, check whether your Roselia has transformed into Roserade. Conclusion: Congratulations! Patience and dedication paid off, resulting in a powerful new addition to your team: Roserade. Remember, every Pok mon journey presents unique challenges and rewards. Happy training! Typically, gender has been described by using terms such as man, woman, or even transsexual. These terms are applied to people based on their sex, or sometimes on the basis of culturally constructed characteristics. Although many people may be biologically assigned a sex, the gender they believe themselves to be may not align with this sex. An individual's gender is often assumed by outsiders based on cultural norms: outward appearance, body shape and size, physical activity, etc.. Although an individual's gender identity is determined by psychology, a gender identity can be constructed by wearing particular clothing, language, actions and such, that are associated with that gender in a given society. Psychologist and Feminist Anthropologist Anne Fausto-Sterling calls this embodiment. So she kept going back to those things that made noise? The boy ran outside with a big grin and his hat tilted to the side. He had so much fun playing outside, feeling the sun on his face with his hat keeping him cool. He spent the whole day outside, playing till it was time to go home. When he got home, he leaned against the wall again and took off his hat. Certain properties of a particle, such as their position, speed of motion, direction of motion, and "spin", cannot be talked about until something measures them (a photon bouncing off of an electron would count as a measurement of its position, for example). Before the measurement, the particle is in a "superposition of states," in which its properties have many values at the same time. Each carbon atom usually forms four chemical bonds, which are strong connections to other atoms to form molecules. The kind of bond that carbon makes is called a covalent bond. These bonds allow carbon to form many kinds of small and large molecules. A molecule of methane is the smallest; it has four hydrogen atoms bonded to carbon. The bonds can be double bonds, meaning that two bonds form between carbon and another atom to make a stronger connection. For example, carbon dioxide has two oxygen atoms, and each one is double bonded to carbon. Carbon can even form three bonds with another atom, called a triple bond. For example, in the gas acetylene carbon forms a triple bond with another carbon atom. I've never seen one of those before. Nope 'cause it pasta be dried again, huh? The word comes originally from the Greek "Theatron", meaning roughly, 'a place to behold'. In American English, the word 'theater' can mean either a place where films are shown (this is also called a cinema) or a place where live stage plays are performed. In British English, 'theatre' means a place where live plays are performed. Some people, both English and American, use the spelling 'theatre' to mean a place where live plays are performed, and the spelling 'theater' to mean a cinema. Title: How to Fold an American Flag The United States flag is more than just a piece of cloth; it's a symbol of freedom, unity, and pride. When displaying or storing the American flag, it should be properly folded in a triangular shape, similar to that of a military hat. This folding method pays respect to the flag while also making it compact enough for storage. Here's a detailed guide on how to do this: **Things You'll Need:** * A clean, undamaged U.S. flag ### Step 1: Lay Out the Flag Lay the flag flat on a table or other surface with its right side (the field of stars) facing up. Make sure there are no wrinkles or creases in the fabric as you prepare to fold it. ### Step 2: Begin the First Fold Bring the bottom edge of the flag up to meet the top edge, so that the ends of the stripes align with the blue field of stars. This will create a rectangle. Straighten out any folds or twists in the material at this point. The resulting rectangle represents the lives lost defending our country. ### Step 3: Perform the Second Fold Take the upper half of the folded rectangle and bring it down over the lower half, once again lining up the edges. Now your flag resembles a square. Each fold has a specific meaning - this second fold represents the concept of equal justice under law. ### Step 4: Start Triangle Formation Start forming the first triangle by taking the lower corner of the folded edge (closest to you) and folding it towards the upper corner (away from you). It should look like a triangle with the tip pointing away from you. Tuck the excess material underneath the outer edge of the triangle to keep everything neat and tidy. ### Step 5: Alternate Corner Folding Alternately fold the exposed corners into the open end of the triangle until only the blue field remains visible. Each fold signifies a virtue such as valor, bravery, sacrifice, or perserverance. As you perform these folds, remember those who have served and sacrificed for our nation. ### Step 6: Final Triangle Shape Once all corners have been folded into the triangle, only the blue field should be showing. If necessary, adjust the folds so that they are straight and even. At this stage, your flag should closely resemble a classic military-style hat. ### Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always handle the flag gently and respectfully during the folding process. - Keep the folds tight and crisp for a neater appearance. - Remember that each fold carries historical significance and symbolism related to American values. - Never let the flag touch the ground during the folding process. - After folding, store the flag in a cool, dry place protected from dust, dirt, and moisture. By following these steps and understanding their meanings, you can take part in honoring the United States flag and those who have fought to protect it. Ross can see that that's how I know that. Title: How to Be Liked During the First Date: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Going on a first date can be both exciting and nerve-wracking. You want to make a good impression, but you might also feel pressure to be perfect. The truth is, there's no such thing as a perfect date, but there are ways to increase your chances of being liked by following some simple yet effective strategies. This guide will walk you through 15 steps that will help you be more likable during a first date, along with key tips and guidelines for each stage. By focusing on being genuine, attentive, and respectful, you can create a positive atmosphere where both you and your date can enjoy getting to know one another better. Step 1: Prepare Beforehand Research shows that preparation increases confidence, which in turn makes you more attractive to others (Aronson, Wilson, & Akert, 2019). To prepare for your date, learn about your match's interests, hobbies, or work. Casually bring up these topics during conversation to show that you have done your homework and genuinely care about their passions. Additionally, plan out what you will wear, ensuring that your outfit reflects your personal style while remaining appropriate for the venue. Key Tip: Avoid over-rehearsing conversations; instead, focus on general topics related to your date's profile or common ground between the two of you. Step 2: Arrive On Time Punctuality demonstrates respect for your date's time and commitment. It sets a positive tone right from the start and communicates reliability (Cottone, 2017). If unforeseen circumstances cause delays, inform your date promptly so they aren't left waiting anxiously. Key Guideline: Make sure to account for potential traffic or parking issues when planning your route to the meeting spot. Step 3: Greet Your Date Warmly A friendly greeting goes a long way toward making someone feel comfortable around you. Offer a firm handshake or a gentle hug if it feels natural and appropriate based on your relationship thus far. Maintain eye contact during this initial interaction to establish an emotional connection (Guerrero, Andersen, & Afifi, 2018). Key Tip: Smile sincerely - genuine smiles are contagious and set the stage for a pleasant encounter (Hess, Blairy, & Kleck, 2002). Step 4: Show Genuine Interest People appreciate when others take an interest in their lives. Ask open-ended questions that allow your date to share stories and insights about themselves. Listen actively, maintain eye contact, and respond thoughtfully to demonstrate engagement (Bodie, Vickery, Sir Isaac Newton's laws of motion contain an important formula: "F" = "ma". "F" is force. "m" is mass. "a" is acceleration. An object with a mass ("m") of one kilogram will accelerate ("a") at one meter per second per second when acted upon by a force ("F") of one newton. Title: How to Edit Song Information in iTunes Introduction: iTunes is a popular media player that allows users to organize, play, and manage their digital music library. One useful feature of iTunes is its ability to edit song information. This can help you keep your music collection organized and up-to-date with accurate metadata such as artist name, album title, track number, genre, year, and more. Here's an in-depth guide on editing song information in iTunes. Step 1: Open iTunes and Locate Your Music Library Start by launching iTunes on your computer. Once opened, navigate to your music library by clicking 'Music' from the top menu bar (Windows) or selecting the 'Library' tab at the top left corner (Mac). Key Tip: If you have multiple libraries or playlists within iTunes, make sure you select the correct one before proceeding. Step 2: Find the Song You Want to Edit Browse through your music library until you find the specific song whose details need changing. Use filters like artists, albums, genres etc., available at the right sidebar for easier navigation if required. Guideline: It might be helpful to sort your songs alphabetically or based on other attributes so that finding the particular song becomes less time consuming. Step 3: Select the Song Once located, click once on the song to highlight it. Multiple tracks can also be selected using Shift+Click or Ctrl+Click (Command+Click on Mac). Step 4: Display Song Info Window Right-click (Control-click on Mac) any selected track(s), then choose "Get Info" from the contextual menu. A new window titled "Song Info" will appear displaying all relevant data about the chosen track(s). Step 5: Make Necessary Changes In this newly opened window, there are several tabs containing different categories of info related to the song - Details, Artwork, Options, Lyrics & Custom. Navigate among these tabs depending upon what changes you wish to make. Here are some common edits made under each category: - Details Tab: Change basic info including Artist Name, Album Title, Track Number, Genre, Year etc. Also, ensure Capitalization consistency across various fields following either CamelCase or ALL_CAPS format. - Artwork Tab: Add, remove or modify existing cover art associated with the song. Drag & drop an image file into the box provided here. - Options Tab: Adjust settings like rating, volume leveling, start/stop times, etc. - Lyrics Tab: Enter lyrics manually or import them directly from the internet. - Custom Tab: Create custom text fields according to personal preference or requirement. Step 6: Confirm Edits After making all necessary modifications, confirm them by clicking 'OK'. Note that modifying certain fields may affect how they show up elsewhere, say Apple Music recommendations. Key Tip: Remember, altering certain tags could result in losing synced data over devices; hence do so thoughtfully. Conclusion: Editing song information in iTunes ensures accuracy, promotes organization, and enhances user experience while managing their music collections. With practice, these steps become second nature, enabling efficient maintenance of even large libraries effortlessly. The following is a list of places in Malaysia. They are in order by the date they were given city status. Alright and what else did you do? Title: How to Become a Bookworm Introduction: Becoming a bookworm can be an incredibly rewarding experience. Not only does reading expand your vocabulary and improve your comprehension skills, but it also allows you to explore new worlds, meet fascinating characters, and gain knowledge about various subjects. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to become a bookworm. Step 1: Identify Your Interests Determine what type of books you enjoy reading. This could include fiction or non-fiction genres such as mystery, fantasy, science fiction, biography, history, self-help, travelogue, etc. Knowing your preferences will make reading more enjoyable and increase your motivation to read regularly. Key Tip: Don't limit yourself to just one genre; diversifying your reading list exposes you to different writing styles, perspectives, and ideas. Step 2: Set Aside Time for Reading Dedicate specific time slots during your day for reading. It could be early mornings, lunch breaks, commute hours, or before bedtime. Consistency is crucial in forming any habit, including becoming a bookworm. Guideline: Start with smaller blocks of time (like 15 minutes) and gradually increase them once you get comfortable with this routine. Step 3: Create a Comfortable Reading Space A cozy corner with good lighting and minimal distractions can significantly enhance your reading experience. Whether it's a favorite armchair, a quiet spot in the park, or even curled up under a blanket fort, having a dedicated space encourages regular reading sessions. Step 4: Visit Libraries and Bookstores Surrounding yourself with books stimulates curiosity and desire to read. Regularly visiting libraries and bookstores gives you access to thousands of titles and opportunities to discover new authors and genres. Key Tip: Participate in library programs or sign up for newsletters from local bookstores to stay updated on author talks, literary festivals, and other literacy events. Step 5: Join a Book Club Book clubs provide social interaction around shared interests while encouraging regular reading habits. They offer discussions that deepen understanding and appreciation of selected texts and often introduce members to books they might not have chosen themselves. Step 6: Utilize Audiobooks and E-books If physical books are challenging due to vision issues or mobility constraints, consider using audiobooks or e-books. These formats allow you to consume literature conveniently and accessibly, making them excellent alternatives for those who struggle with traditional print media. Step 7: Document Your Reading Journey Keeping track of what you read helps you reflect on your progress and growth as a reader. You can maintain a journal, start a blog, or simply note down thoughts on Goodreads or similar platforms. Sharing reviews and recommendations strengthens connections within the reading community too! Step 8: Stay Curious & Open-Minded Embrace challenges by stepping out of your comfort zone occasionally. Try reading classics, award-winning novels, or books outside your usual preference. Approaching reading with a sense of adventure enriches your overall experience and broadens horizons. Conclusion: Remember, there's no right way to be a bookworm - everyone's journey is unique. The most important thing is enjoying the process and letting reading transport you to places near and far, real and imagined. Happy reading! XXX this is gonna stupid WWW [laughs]. [picks up the other car and puts it on the page, not in identification] Title: How to Draw the Human Body Drawing the human body can be a challenging yet rewarding experience. This tutorial will guide you through the process, providing in-depth explanations for each step and offering key tips along the way. By the end, you'll have created a basic outline of the human form. **Step 1: Gather Your Materials** You will need: - A pencil (preferably HB or 2B) - An eraser - Paper or a sketchbook Using these simple tools allows for easy corrections as you learn. Once you become more comfortable with drawing the human body, feel free to experiment with other mediums such as charcoal or pens. **Step 2: Understand Basic Proportions** Before starting your drawing, familiarize yourself with average human body proportions. Here are some general guidelines: - The head is typically about seven to eight heads tall when measuring from top to bottom of the figure. - Arms extend downward from the shoulder line, reaching slightly past the hips. - Legs start at the base of the pelvis and extend beyond the head height. - The torso is roughly three heads wide at its broadest point. These ratios may vary depending on individual differences, but they provide a good starting point for beginners. **Step 3: Sketch the Head** Lightly draw an oval shape representing the skull. Add two intersecting lines within the oval - one vertical dividing the oval into halves, and another horizontal line one-third down from the top of the oval. These lines help align facial features later. **Step 4: Outline the Torso** Directly below the head, sketch a large rectangle for the chest area. Connect this to a smaller triangle beneath it, forming the lower back and pelvic region. Remember, the torso should be approximately three heads wide. **Step 5: Position the Shoulders** Atop the torso, place two small circles where the arms meet the body. These represent the joint sockets of the shoulders. **Step 6: Create the Arm Structure** From each circle, draw two curved lines extending outwards, then downwards towards the hip level. Add rounded rectangles near the ends of these lines to indicate hands. **Step 7: Form the Leg Structure** Below the pelvic region, sketch two overlapping shapes for each leg - a longer, thinner rectangle for the thigh, followed by a shorter, wider shape for the calf. Terminate each leg with a foot shape consisting of five extended lines. **Step 8: Refine the Figure** Erase any unnecessary construction lines, leaving only the main body parts visible. At this stage, focus on maintaining correct proportions while shaping muscles and contours. **Step 9: Define Muscles and Contours** Referencing real models or photographs, add details like muscular definition and natural curves. Be mindful of light sources and shadows, incorporating them subtly to give depth to your drawing. **Key Tips:** - Practice regularly to improve skill and confidence. - Break complex tasks into manageable steps. - Use references for accuracy. - Don't worry about perfection; embrace imperfections as part of the learning process. By following these steps and keeping our key tips in mind, you're well on your way to mastering the art of drawing the human body! Louisa Stark was stout and solidly built. Title: Adding a Link Button to the Home Screen of an iPhone 1. **Open the Safari browser:** The first step is to launch the Safari app on your iPhone. This built-in browser allows you to create web clip icons or shortcuts that can be added to your home screen. Using other browsers like Google Chrome may not provide this feature. 2. **Navigate to the website:** Enter the URL of the site you want to add as a link button in the address bar at the top of the page and tap 'Go'. Make sure this is a frequently visited site so having quick access via your home screen becomes beneficial. 3. **Tap the share icon:** Once the webpage has fully loaded, look for the square with an arrow pointing outwards symbol (the Share Icon) usually located at the bottom middle part of your screen. Tapping on this will open up a menu with various options. 4. **Select 'Add to Home Screen':** Scroll through the list until you find the option labeled 'Add to Home Screen'. It's typically found under the 'Actions' section towards the end of the list. This selection opens another window. 5. **Edit the title (optional):** Here, you'll see a preview image along with a text field containing the name of the website. You can keep the default name or edit it into something shorter or more recognizable. Keep in mind that longer names might get truncated due to space limitations on the home screen. 6. **Confirm placement:** Below the title field, there should be a representation showing where the new icon will be placed among your current apps when added to the home screen. While moving the newly created icon around later is possible, deciding its initial position now could save time. 7. **Tap 'Add' to finalize:** Finally, press 'Add' at the upper right corner of the screen. Immediately after doing so, you'll notice the new button appearing on one of your home screens - likely wherever you positioned it during step six. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Web clips are essentially bookmarks but presented differently. They allow faster access to specific sites without needing to navigate through folders within the Safari app. - Although only visible on iPhones, these links also work on iPads and iPod touches using iOS. - If you change your mind about keeping the web clip, simply hold down the icon until all apps start wiggling, then hit the small 'x' displayed on the top left corner of the unwanted icon. Confirm removal by tapping 'Delete'. - Remember, since these are just shortcuts to websites, they won't function if your device isn't connected to the internet unless the site supports offline viewing features. Title: How to Change What Syncs Between Windows 10 Devices Windows 10 has a feature called "Sync your settings" that allows you to synchronize various settings across multiple devices running the same operating system. This can be convenient if you want to have consistent settings across all your Windows 10 devices. However, there might be situations where you want to customize what data gets synced or even stop syncing altogether. Here's an in-depth guide on changing what syncs between your Windows 10 devices. **Step 1: Access Settings App** Click on the Start button (Windows icon) located at the bottom left corner of your screen, then click on the gear icon labeled as 'Settings.' Alternatively, press `Win + I` keys simultaneously to open the Settings app directly. _Key Tip:_ If you find yourself frequently accessing certain sections within the Settings app, consider pinning it to the taskbar for quicker access. **Step 2: Navigate to Accounts Section** In the Settings window, look for the category named "Accounts." Click on it to proceed. **Step 3: Select Sync Your Settings Option** Under the 'Accounts' heading, choose the option titled "Sync your settings" from the left side panel. You will now see several toggles representing different types of data that can be synchronized. **Step 4: Manage Individual Data Types** Each toggle represents a specific type of data that can be synced: * **Theme:** Includes background images, colors, font sizes, etc. * **Desktop wallpaper:** Changes made to desktop background image. * **Taskbar:** Position of taskbar, pinned apps, etc. * **Start menu:** Arrangement of tiles, default folders shown, etc. * **Language preferences:** Keyboard layout, display language, speech recognition, etc. * **Ease of Access:** Magnifier, Narrator, Closed captions, Color filters, etc. * **Others:** Passwords, browser settings, file explorer options, etc. To change what syncs, simply slide the corresponding toggle switch to On (blue) or Off (gray). When a setting is turned off, it stops syncing immediately but doesn't affect any existing syncs. _Guideline:_ Turning off unnecessary sync categories reduces bandwidth usage and increases privacy by preventing personal information from being uploaded unnecessarily. **Step 5: Sign Out Of Microsoft Account (Optional)** If you wish to completely disable syncing, sign out of your Microsoft account associated with this device. Go back to the main page of the Settings app, select 'Sign-in options', and under 'You are signed in as', click on 'Sign out'. Remember, doing so will remove all linked services like OneDrive, Office applications, Skype, Cortana, etc., requiring reconfiguration upon signing back in. Remember, changes take effect instantly once applied. Depending on your internet connection speed, full propagation may vary. It's also worth noting that while these steps help manage what data gets synced between your Windows 10 devices, they do not control individual application settings which often maintain their own cloud saves and sync methods. And then you can look at the book too. Title: How to Get a Guy Who Hates You to Like You Introduction: Changing someone's feelings towards you is never an easy task, especially when that person seems to dislike you. However, with patience, understanding, and the right approach, it's possible to turn things around. This guide will provide you with a detailed, step-by-step process on how to get a guy who hates you to like you. Remember, every individual is unique, so adapt these steps as necessary to fit your specific situation. Step 1: Analyze the Situation Determine why he dislikes you. Is it due to miscommunication, misunderstandings, or past experiences? Understanding the root cause will help you address the issue effectively. Key tip: Avoid making assumptions; instead, rely on facts and evidence. Step 2: Give Him Space Give him time to cool down and reconsider his feelings. Respect his personal space and avoid confronting him until he appears more open to communication. This strategy can prevent further escalation of negative emotions and create room for positive interactions in the future. Step 3: Apologize if Necessary If your actions contributed to his disdain, offer a genuine apology without expecting anything in return. Express remorse for any harm caused and take responsibility for your mistakes. A sincere apology can pave the way for forgiveness and mutual respect. Step 4: Communicate Openly Engage in open, honest conversations to clear up any misunderstandings. Be a good listener, show empathy, and validate his feelings. By fostering effective communication, you demonstrate maturity and willingness to understand his perspective. Step 5: Show Interest in His Life Ask about his interests, passions, and daily activities. Display genuine curiosity and engage in discussions related to his hobbies. Sharing common ground and discovering shared interests can build connections and foster positivity between you two. Step 6: Be Kind and Considerate Treat him with kindness, compassion, and respect. Go out of your way to do small acts of kindness, such as offering help or support when needed. Consistently demonstrating caring behavior can shift his perception of you over time. Step 7: Maintain Self-Respect While trying to win him over, don't compromise your values, beliefs, or self-worth. Stay true to yourself and set boundaries when necessary. Demonstrating strength and integrity can earn his admiration and respect. Step 8: Remain Positive and Patient Transforming animosity into friendship or affection takes time. Keep a positive attitude, even when progress seems slow or nonexistent. Persistence and optimism are crucial elements in this process. Step 9: Seek Professional Advice (Optional) Consult a therapist, counselor, or relationship coach if you find it challenging to navigate this complex situation independently. These professionals can provide tailored advice, strategies, and techniques based on your unique circumstances. Conclusion: Changing someone's opinion of you is no simple feat, but it's far from impossible. Approach the situation with care, understanding, and determination. With time, effort, and adherence to these steps, you can transform a hostile relationship into a positive one. Better come leave your sock on and feed your cow. " left foot right foot right. Behaving in an arcade is an important part of ensuring that everyone has an enjoyable experience while visiting this popular entertainment destination. By following these steps, you can help create a positive atmosphere at the arcade and make sure that you are behaving appropriately. Step 1: Familiarize yourself with the rules of the arcade. Before entering the arcade, take a moment to look for any posted rules or regulations. These may include information about age restrictions, behavior expectations, game-specific rules, and payment policies. Understanding and following these rules will help ensure a smooth and enjoyable visit for both you and other guests. Key tip: Many arcades have staff members available to answer questions or clarify any rule uncertainties. Don't hesitate to ask if needed. Step 2: Keep noise levels reasonable. Arcades can be loud and exciting places, but excessive shouting, screaming, or disruptive noises can negatively impact the experience for others. Speak at a normal volume and avoid engaging in activities that could cause unnecessary disturbances. This includes refraining from running, jumping, or rough play inside the arcade. Key guideline: If someone appears disturbed by your noise level, consider lowering your voice as a courtesy. Step 3: Treat arcade equipment properly. To keep games functioning optimally and prevent damage, handle all gaming machines gently and carefully. Refrain from slamming buttons, kicking machines, or applying undue force to controllers. Additionally, do not attempt to climb on top of or move gaming stations unless explicitly permitted. Key tip: Report any malfunctioning games or broken equipment to arcade staff immediately so they can address the issue promptly. Step 4: Share games fairly with others. In many cases, multiple people may want to play the same game simultaneously. When waiting for your turn, stand back from the machine and allow enough space for those actively playing. Once it's your turn, respect time limits and share the game equitably with fellow players. Avoid hogging machines or refusing to give up control when asked politely. Key guideline: Practice patience and good sportsmanship during high-demand situations. " Mommy I'm going crazy I'm losing my marbles. Title: How to Create an Organic Gift Basket ========================================== Creating an organic gift basket is a thoughtful way to show someone you care while promoting healthy living and environmental sustainability. By selecting organic products, you are giving a gift that supports eco-friendly farming practices, reduces exposure to synthetic pesticides, and contributes to better health and wellbeing for both the recipient and the planet. This article will guide you through creating your own unique and meaningful organic gift basket with clear and practical steps. ### Things You'll Need * A basket or container (preferably reusable) * Organic fruits and vegetables * Organic snacks (e.g., nuts, dried fruit, granola) * Organic body care items (e.g., soap, lotion, shampoo) * Optional: tea, coffee, honey, or other local artisanal treats * Tissue paper or cellophane wrapping material * Ribbon or twine * Scissors * Cardstock or small card for personalized message ### Step 1: Select Your Container It will make a great big hole if you put it in there again. With the simple cycle, the length of the Julian year is exactly 365.25 days (365 days and 6 hours), but the actual time it takes for the Earth to go around the Sun once is closer to 365.2422 days (about 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes and 46 seconds). This difference is about 365.25 - 365.2422 = 0.0078 days (11 minutes and 14 seconds) each year, although Greek astronomers knew that. This made the seasons get out of track, since the real first day of spring in western Europe (the equinox - day and night the same length) was happening earlier and earlier before the traditional 21 March as the centuries went by. His face grew stern again. Emergent Literacy Design: And a Baby Cries Rationale: In order for children to learn to read and write they must have phonemic awareness. This is the ability to recognize phonemes in the spoken language. Vowels are very important for the student to learn in order to read words more accurately. As students learn the vowel correspondences, they will become equipped with the decoding knowledge to work with longer words. Also, as they accomplish these goals they can begin to create meaning and make written communication possible. This lesson will help students identify a = /a/ (short a). The goals of this lesson are to help the students learn to recognize /a/ in spoken words through a letter symbol and meaningful modeling, and then they will practice finding /a/ in words. Materials: Primary paper and pencil; poster with tongue twister on it; cards with pictures on them; Cards with thumbs up on one side and thumbs down on the other side; drawing paper and crayons; picture worksheets with pictures of bat, bug, cap, drum, stamp, van, book, axe, bath, mat, meat, pan, cab. Pat's Jam. 1. Introduce the lesson by explaining to the children that they are going to learn how to recognize the vowel sound /a/ in written and spoken language. "Today we are going to work on making the vowel /a/ sound and finding it in words. This may seem hard at first, but once we practice some it will become easier to spot the /a/ in all kinds of words." 2. Ask students: Have you ever heard a baby cry? That's the sound we will be looking for today. Can you all make the crying sound, "aaa aaa". Good now lets use it in a word. We will stretch out the word rat. Let me show you, rrra-a-a-at. Did you here it? Raaa...aa. That is the baby crying sound, /a/. 3. Now let's look at the tongue twister on the poster. "Adam said an apple a day makes us not afraid." Everyone say it together. Now let's try stretching out the /a/ sound at the beginning of each word. "Aaaadam said aaaan aaapple a day makes us not aaafraid." Good job. 4. (Have the students get out some primary paper and pencil). Now let's put the letter with the sound /a/. We use the letter "a" to spell /a/. Let's practice writing it. Start a little below the fence line. Curve to the left coming up to the fence line and back down around like the letter "c". Lift your pencil and come back to the close up the "c" by drawing a straight line from the fence line to the sidewalk. Hold up your "a" when you are done and I will come around and check it. Then you can continue making your "a" across the rest of the page. Now we know that when we see this letter written alone we know to make the baby crying sound /a/. 5. Allow students to explain how they knew the right answer: Hold up two pictures and ask the student if they hear the /a/ sound in the picture of a cat or dog. (Pass out the thumbs up/down cards to each student.) Say: Let's see if you can hear the /a/ sound in these pictures. Show me the thumbs up if you hear /a/ and the thumbs down if you do not. Example: Do you hear the /a/ sound in "cat" or "dog" (Ask while holding up the picture and give the students time to answer after each word in the comparison group). 6. Read Pat's Jam and talk about the story. Then read it one more time and have the students raise their thumbs up card when they hear words with the /a/ sound. List the words on the board. Then allow the students to draw Pat and write a new short ending to the story read earlier using invented spelling. Let a few children share their new ending and display the rest of the work. 7. Assessment: Distribute a picture worksheet and name the pictures with the students. Tell them to circle the pictures that have the /a/ sound in it. Lastly, ask the students to look around the room and find things that have the /a/ sound in the name. Write these on the board. (Note which children are still having problems identifying the /a/ sound) J. Lloyd Eldredge (1995). Teaching Decoding in Holistic Classrooms. Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Merrill. 52-61. Cushman, Shelia. Title: Draw Escher's Impossible Cube **Introduction:** M.C. Escher was a Dutch graphic artist known for his mathematically-inspired artworks. His 'impossible constructions', optical illusions that appear to be three-dimensional objects but cannot actually exist, are among his most famous works. The "Impossible Cube" or "Penrose Triangle," is one such creation. This tutorial will guide you through drawing an accurate representation of this fascinating illusion. **Step 1: Understand the Concept** Before starting, understand what makes the Penrose Triangle impossible. It seems like a continuous loop where each side connects to another at right angles, yet all corners rise from the page. However, if you try to build it physically, you'll find it can't be done due to spatial constraints. **Step 2: Sketch Guidelines** Lightly draw two parallel horizontal lines apart by about twice the height you want your final cube to have. These lines represent the horizon and give stability to your drawing. Next, sketch perpendicular vertical lines intersecting these horizons - these will serve as guides for the edges of the cube faces. **Step 3: Create Base Corners** At both ends of either line (left or right), create a square corner using your guidelines. Each square should touch both horizontals and one vertical guideline. Make sure they're equal squares; consistency ensures proper perspective. **Step 4: Connect Diagonal Sides** Now comes the tricky part. Instead of connecting sides vertically between the two base cubes, we'll do so diagonally. From the top left edge of the lower square, draw a diagonal line upwards to meet the bottom right edge of the upper square. Repeat on the other side, creating a zigzag effect. **Key Tip**: Keep these newly drawn diagonal lines parallel to the original vertical guidelines. Perspective creates the illusion of impossibility here. **Step 5: Complete Lower Faces** Connect the open ends of the zigzagging lines with straight horizontal ones. Fill in the remaining space below with smaller squares similar to those forming the bases. Remember to align them with your initial guidelines. **Step 6: Add Depth Illusion** The next set of shapes adds depth perception confusion. On the backside of the structure, mirror the pattern seen on the front. That means repeating the zigzagged connection, followed by flat surfaces underneath. You should now see something resembling a staircase disappearing into itself. **Step 7: Refine Lines & Erase Unnecessary Guides** Once satisfied with the placement of everything, go over your pencil marks with ink, ensuring clean, solid lines. Afterward, erase any unnecessary construction lines carefully. **Step 8: Shading For Realism** Shade selectively to enhance the illusion. Darken areas appearing further away while leaving closer parts lighter. Be subtle; heavy shading might make the piece look flat rather than dimensional. **Conclusion:** Drawing Escher's Impossible Cube requires careful planning and understanding of perspective distortion principles. By following this tutorial, you've managed to recreate one of M.C. Escher's iconic designs. Practice regularly to improve proficiency and explore variations based on this concept. Happy drawing! Title: How to Delete Your Snapchat Account Permanently Snapchat is a popular social media platform that allows users to share ephemeral photos and videos with friends. However, if you've decided that you no longer want to use Snapchat, this tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting your account permanently. Keep in mind that once your account has been deleted, all your data, including Memories, Friends, and Snapstreaks, will be lost and cannot be recovered. Before proceeding, consider downloading your data from Snapchat ([https://support.snapchat.com/en-US/a/download-your-data](https://support. snapchat.com/en-US/a/download-your-data)). ### Part 1: Logging into Your Snapchat Account #### Step 1: Open the Snapchat App Find the yellow ghost icon labeled 'Snapchat' on one of your device's home screens or within the app drawer, then tap on it to open the application. #### Step 2: Access Your Account Enter your username or email address and password to log in. Make sure you are using the correct credentials associated with the account you wish to delete. If you have forgotten your login details, visit the Snapchat support page for assistance (<https://support.snapchat.com/en-US/forgot-password>). ### Part 2: Deleting Your Snapchat Account #### Step 3: Visit the Snapchat Account Portal Once logged in, navigate to the following URL on your desktop browser or mobile web browser: <https://accounts.snapchat.com/>. This link directs you to the official Snapchat account portal where you can manage various aspects related to your account. Note that you must complete the account deletion process via the website; it cannot be done directly within the Snapchat app. #### Step 4: Select 'Delete My Account' After logging in to your account at the Snapchat account portal, locate and click on the 'Delete My Account' option under the 'Account Actions' section. This action takes you to another page requiring confirmation before initiating the deletion process. #### Step 5: Re-enter Your Password To ensure security measures are met, re-enter your Snapchat password when prompted. Type your password carefully as any errors may prevent you from successfully completing the account deletion process. #### Step 6: Confirm Account Deactivation Click on the 'Continue' button after entering your password correctly. By clicking this button, you acknowledge that you understand the consequences of deactivating your account, such as losing access to all stored data and content associated with the account. Additionally, note that deactivated accounts remain active for 30 days post-deactivation before being permanently erased. During these 30 days, should you change your mind about deleting your Snapchat account, simply sign back in to reverse the deactivation request. After 30 days, however, there would be no way to recover your account or its contents. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be certain about your decision to delete your Snapchat account because once completed, it cannot be undone. * Double-check your spelling while typing your username, email, or password during the login and verification stages. Mistakes could hinder the successful completion of the account deletion process. * Downloading your data prior to account deletion ensures retaining copies of cherished memories and interactions made through Snapchat. Title: How to Cook Bacon in the Oven Introduction: Baking bacon in the oven is an easy, mess-free alternative to traditional stovetop cooking methods. This method allows you to cook large quantities at once, reduces shrinkage, and results in uniformly crispy slices. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to do it perfectly every time! Materials Needed: 1. Aluminum foil or parchment paper 2. A baking sheet with edges (to catch grease) 3. Tongs (for flipping the bacon) 4. Paper towels (for draining excess fat) 5. A plate or cooling rack (for resting cooked bacon) 6. Optional - wire cooling rack placed inside the baking sheet (for even crispier bacon) Step 1: Preheat Your Oven Preheat your oven to 400 F (200 C). Preheating ensures that when the cold bacon hits the hot oven temperature, it will start rendering its fat immediately which leads to perfect crisping. Step 2: Line The Baking Sheet Line your baking sheet with aluminum foil or parchment paper. It makes cleanup easier as all the drippings are collected on the lining. If using a wire cooling rack, place this inside the lined baking sheet now. The purpose of the rack is to allow air circulation around the bacon strips resulting in extra crispy texture. Step 3: Arrange The Bacon Lay out the bacon slices flat onto the prepared baking sheet or directly onto the wire rack if used. Avoid overlapping them because doing so can result in uneven cooking and steaming instead of roasting. Step 4: Seasoning Optionally Though many prefer plain bacon, some like adding brown sugar, black pepper, maple syrup, etc., before baking. Feel free to sprinkle any preferred seasoning over the arranged bacon slices at this stage. Step 5: Time To Bake Place the bacon-filled baking sheet into your preheated oven. Set your timer according to your preference of crispiness level. Generally, bacon takes about 12-18 minutes but could take less or more depending upon factors such as thickness of slice, individual oven temperatures, etc. Check after 10 minutes initially then frequently thereafter until desired doneness achieved. Key Tips During Baking Process: - Keep checking regularly post-10 minute mark since ovens vary greatly and burnt bacon isn't tasty! - Flip the bacon halfway through cooking for even browning and blistering of the fat layer. However, if you're aiming for curly bacon, skip this part. Step 6: Remove From The Oven & Drain Excess Fat Once done, carefully remove the tray from the oven. Use tongs to transfer the cooked bacon onto a stack of paper towels to drain off excessive grease. Press another towel gently on top to absorb further fat. Step 7: Serve Or Store Serve warm bacon immediately for best taste. Alternatively, let cool completely before storing leftovers in an airtight container and refrigerate up to five days or freeze up to six months. Conclusion: Oven-baked bacon provides consistent deliciousness without constant supervision required by stove-top methods. With these steps and tips, we hope you master this skill and enjoy perfect bacon every time! Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Sally. She loved to dance. Everyone was having so much fun! Even the birds and the bees were dancing to the music. When their dance was finished, Sally's mother called her home. Sally said goodbye to the other children and skipped away. As she walked, she could still feel the rhythm of the music in her heart. So um well any toys that he played with this time more than he did last time or...? Not with that boo, boo in there. Moles can be used to see how many molecules are in chemical compounds, too. Copper(II) chloride is an example. CuCl is its chemical formula. There is one copper atom (63.55) and two chlorine atoms (35.45 2 = 70.90). Add all the molar masses of the elements together to get the molar mass of the chemical compound (63.55 + 70.90 = 134.45). That means in 134.45 grams of copper(II) chloride, there is one mole of copper(II) chloride molecules. This concept is used to calculate how much chemicals are needed in a chemical reaction if no reactants (chemicals that are reacted) should be left. If too much reactant is used, there will be some reactants left in the chemical reaction. But then I out of the corner of my eye I realized it was this huge dog. You want to draw a picture with this? What's The Point The purpose of this activity is to help your child explore numbers and counting, an important step toward learning how to add and subtract. This Activity Will Help Your Child - Understand "how many" - Practice counting and doing simple number problems - Understand the connection between a group of things and a number - Feast for 10 by Cathryn Falwell - Tikki Tikki Tembo by Arlene Mosel Everyday objects (e.g., buttons, shoes, toys) How Do I Do It? Help your child explore counting in everyday activities. - When setting the table, help your child count how many people will be eating together and figure out how many plates, napkins, cups, etc. you'll need. Then let your child count them out. - Make up "number stories" using objects from around the house, such as stuffed animals, small toys, or even fruit. For example: "You have 3 toy cars. If I give you 2 more, how many will you have?" or "We have three apples. You and I are each going to eat an apple. How many apples will there be?" - Include your child in child safe cooking activities. Have him/her help count out ingredients and help with measuring and pouring. Take It Further Move from counting around the house to counting everywhere. Count apples as you load them into a bag at the grocery store, or count the number of pumps at a gas station. You can also count people in line at stores and the number of items that you purchase. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV1/TP/justp1.cha = = = We both like to read it together period. [Chi stacks blocks, they start building together again] Make Name Pattern Art! One of the first words every kindergartener learns to read or spell is his own name. And because it's personal, children enjoy seeing, playing with, and creating artwork with their names. So what better way to practice reading and basic math than with a word your child is familiar with? In this activity, your child will write and color in his name to improve his letter recognition and patterning skills. In the Second World War, bombings damaged the stadium. While the stadium was being fixed, they played "home" games at Manchester City's Maine Road. The stadium was converted to an all-seater stadium in 1993 for safety reasons, but the capacity fell to 44,000. In 1995, expansions in the North Stand raised the capacity to 55,000. In the middle of 1999, the East and West Stands were expanded to give the stadium a capacity of 67,000. Then between 2005 and 2006 8,000 more seats were added to make a capacity of 75,000. [the doll has various clips and a barrette in her hair.] Let's Get Icky and Sticky Rationale: This lesson will help beginning readers to learn to spell and read words. They will learn to recognize i=/i/ in written and spoken words. They will learn a meaningful representation and practice spelling and reading words with i=/i/ using a letterbox lesson. Also, they will read a new book. Letterboxes: A set of 2, 3, 4, and 5 for each student and teacher Letterbox letters for each student and teacher: i (2), s, t, f, a, n, b (2), k, l (2), e, d, r, F, h, p, m, c Overhead projector Picture of "icky sticky glue" with hand motion description Poster with tongue twister: "Nikki is an icky sticky piggy living in an igloo made of itchy, icky, sticky mud. " Primary Paper (2 sheets, one for teacher and one for student) Pencils (2 wooden) Book Liz Is Six Worksheet with pictures for assessment (pictures of two choices, which picture do you hear i=/i/? (pig or horse? Spill or drip? Cook or grill? Stink or smell?) 1. First, I will show the students the letter i on the overhead projector. I will use the upper and lower case I from my set of letterbox tiles. "Can you tell me what letter this is?" "That's correct, it is the letter I." "Who can tell me what sound it makes?" "Great job!" Now I will place the picture of someone touching icky sticky glue on the overhead. "The i makes the sound /i/ like you have icky sticky glue all over your hands." I will then stretch out the i sound to sound like''I have glue all over my hands,'' just like the picture shows. "Now I want everyone to try and get the icky sticky glue off their hands!" 2. Next, I will show the tongue twister on the overhead projector. "I am going to read this silly sentence to you and then I want you to read it after me." I will read the sentence stretching out the i to sound like icky sticky glue . "Now it's your turn to repeat after me: "Nikki is an icky sticky piggy living in an igloo made of itchy, icky, sticky mud. " 3. Now, I want you to pay really close attention because I am going to ask you some questions. "I am going to read two words to you and I want you to be listening for the icky sticky i. After I read the words, I want you to raise your hand and tell me what word you heard icky sticky in, and then show me the hand motion that represents it." Word list: o Bed or sit o Fix or kite o Lip or nose o Trick or Treat 4. Hand out letterbox tiles and have students turn them over to the lowercase side. Now I want everyone watch me as I model how to use our letterboxes. For this word, I am going to need three letterboxes. That means there are three sounds in my word. This also means that our mouths are only going to move three times when we say this word. The word is fix. The f says /f/ so we need to put the letter f in our first letterbox. The second sound is i so we need to put the letter i in the second letterbox. The last sound is /x/ so we need to put the letter x in the last letterbox. Now it is your turn. The students will begin by reading each word and then spelling it. o Words: (2) is, it (3) fat, fit, sit, tin, lip, kit (4) bled, bred, fled, sled, spill, grip (5) drift, twist, split, blest, slept. o The student will use their letterboxes and letter tiles to spell the words. I will walk around the room and monitor the students and help them if needed. 5. I will now have students read words off the overhead projector. I will show a list of words that they spelled in step 3. If a child cannot read a word, I will use body-coda blending to facilitate reading. I will start with the vowel i and then add the letters that correspond with the phoneme from left to right. 6. Next, I will introduce the decodable book: Liz Is Six. We are going to read Liz Is Six. This story is about a girl who is your age and gets a brand new mitt for her birthday. They decide to play a baseball game and pig gets a great big hit! Let's read to see what happens in the rest of the game with the pig and Liz. The student will then read the book aloud to me, using his yellow pointer to help him read each word. 7. Finally, we are going to write a message to each other about what our favorite animal is. I remind him how to write an /i/ and have him write a couple of words for him to practice. He may use invented spelling when writing. As I work with my student, I will note miscues that I hear as he is reading. I will be able to check his phonemic awareness by anecdotal notes that will collaborate throughout the semester to check reading progress. To end the lesson, I will present my student with a worksheet with pictures on it, some containing the /i/ sound in them. The goal will be to circle the picture that contains the /i/ sound. Under the picture, he will write the word of the picture. He will receive a sticker if he completes the entire lesson. Shumock, Emily. "Icky Piggy." Battles, Ellen. "The Old Creaky Door." Murray, B.A. & Lesniak, T (1999). The Letterbox Lesson: A hands-on approach for teaching decoding. The Reading Teacher, 52, 644-650. Cushman, S (1990). Liz Is Six. Carson, CA: Educational Insights. Today, let's learn about graphing systems of linear inequalities in two variables. It's like solving puzzles by using graphs and shapes! First, what is a linear inequality? It's just a fancy way to say "an equation with an inequality sign," like >, <, , or . You might have seen these before when comparing numbers or lengths. Now, imagine having more than one of these linear inequalities together - that makes a system of linear inequalities. Our goal is to find out where their solutions live on a graph. Let's start with an example problem: Ms. Walochek gave you this system of linear inequalities for homework: x > 0 y < 1 y > -2x + 3 She wants you to figure out which quadrant (or quadrants!) the solution lives in. So, grab your pencil and paper, and follow along! 1. **Draw a coordinate plane**: That's just two perpendicular number lines crossing each other at zero, like a big plus sign (+). Label them x and y axes. 2. **Shade the solution regions**: Since x has to be greater than zero, shade everything to the right of the vertical y-axis because those are the positive values for x. Next, since y must be less than 1, shade below the horizontal line representing y = 1, but don't forget to leave a small gap under the line showing that y cannot touch it. Finally, for y being greater than -2x + 3, plot points (-2, 0), (0, 3), and connect them with a straight line while shading above it; remember to exclude any points touching the line itself. 3. **Identify the quadrants**: Count how many quadrants your shading covers. In our case, it should cover Quadrant I entirely. But why didn't Caren and Bahir agree? Well, Caren thought that x had to be both greater than 0 and also less than some value, so she limited herself to Quadrant I. Meanwhile, Bahir incorrectly assumed that every possible combination was allowed, covering all four quadrants. However, we know better now! The actual answer is simply Quadrant I due to our given conditions. And voil ! Just like that, you learned how to graph systems of linear inequalities in two variables. Keep practicing, and soon enough, you'll become a master puzzle solver! Happy graphing, my little math whizzes! Can You See What I See? Rationale: To become expert readers, children need learn an important skill to comprehend what they are reading. Visualization is one way readers can build comprehension. This is important for students so they can have "a movie" of what's going on throughout their reading. When readers can actively engage themselves in the story, they are able to grasp the meaning of the text. This lesson will help students learn to visualize by creating mental pictures in their mind. Students will listen and read to practice this. Class Copies of Shel Silverstein's Cloony Clown poem A Box Can be Many Things by Dana Rau Class Copies Drawing paper and crayons 1. "Today we are going to do something called visualize. Does anyone know what this means?" Allow for student's answers. "Well, this means to read and see a picture or movie in our mind. I am going to say a sentence and I want you to close your eyes. Once I am done reading, I want you tell me what you see." I was awoken to the smell of hickory bacon and sweet cinnamon rolls. I knew that there would be fresh squeezed orange juice, and gravy for our biscuits. "What did you see from this sentence I said?" Discussion. "That's right; you may have seen your mom cooking a big breakfast with delicious bacon, and orange juice. What you just did was visualize." 2. "When we read to ourselves, what do we call this?" Silent reading. "That's right, silent reading. We do this so we can read as much as we want, quietly so we don't bother anyone else." We are going to silent read a poem by Shel Silverstein. 3. Have students silent read the poem "Cloony the Clown." Allow time for students to read the poem. "When I read this poem I saw a clown that was unfunny, but he was sad. I got this from the poem because it said that he was floppy and sloppy, and unfunny and made everyone cry." Show a picture of what you drew previously, have students share what they pictured. 4. Give students a copy of A Box Can be Many Things. "This book is about a little boy who finds a box his mom has thrown out. The boy makes the box many things, let's read to find out all the exciting things he discovers." Have students read the book silently, or back and forth with a partner. Once the students read through the book, have them draw a picture of what they just read. Let students share their drawings. Have students come to the front of the class and explain their drawings. Assess students with a checklist. Students accurately depicted the story using pictures. Students accurately retold the story. "Can You See It?" Abby Smith. A comet is a ball of mostly ice that moves around in outer space. Comets are often described as "dirty snowballs". They are very different from asteroids. The orbital inclinations of comets are usually high and not near the ecliptic where most solar system objects are found. Most of them are long-period comets and come from the Kuiper belt. Age: 2-5 years Children will conduct an experiment where they watch ice turn to water. What You Need: A hot plate or a microwave oven A pot or microwave-safe dish What You Do: - Heat up the hot plate (not necessary if using a microwave oven). - Make sure you warn children that the plate is very very hot and not to touch it under any circumstances. - Show children the ice cubes. - Talk about the ice cubes. - Are they hot? cold? soft? hard? wet? dry? - Place the ice in a pot on the hot plate and watch it melt. - If you are using a microwave oven, place the ice cubes in a dish and microwave for a few minutes. - Talk about how the ice cubes look as they melt or after they have melted. - Is it still ice? Is it water? What happened to the ice? - Go over what happened. - Talk about how the ice became water as it was warmed up. - Keep asking questions and devote a good amount of time to letting the children come up with answers. www.KinderArt.com | www.KinderArtLittles.com Jantje Blokhuis-Mulder and KinderArt What else do ya need ta make? Greece is not a federal state like the United States, but a unitary state like the United Kingdom. It is ruled by a parliament, called the "Hellenic Parliament" (or "Greek Parliament" in Simple English), which has 300 members. It is a parliamentary republic, which means that, unlike in the United States, the President has very few powers. The person in charge of the government of Greece is the Prime Minister. Creating a penguin in Club Penguin is an exciting way to express yourself and engage in the virtual world created by Disney. This tutorial will guide you through creating your own unique penguin character using clear, concise, and easy-to-follow steps. By the end, you'll have a personalized penguin that reflects your personality! 1. **Access Club Penguin:** Visit the official Club Penguin website (now closed as of 2017) or play the game via one of the available private servers online. Private servers are fan-made versions of the original game which still allow users to enjoy the Club Penguin experience. Make sure to choose a trusted server to protect your privacy. 2. **Sign up or Log in:** If you're new to Club Penguin, click 'Play Now' or 'Join Now' to start the sign-up process. Enter your parent's email address, create a username, and set a password. Read and accept the terms and conditions before clicking 'Next'. Fill out the required information about your age, gender, and country, then complete the registration process. If you already have an account, enter your login details and proceed to the next step. 3. **Customize Your Penguin:** Once logged in, click on 'My Penguin' at the top right corner, followed by 'Change Appearance.' You can customize various aspects of your penguin: * **Skin Color:** Choose from several skin colors like white, blue, green, purple, etc., depending on the options provided by the server. The color you select doesn't affect gameplay but allows self-expression. * **Clothes & Accessories:** Pick clothing items such as shirts, pants, hats, glasses, gloves, shoes, etc. These choices help reflect your style and preferences. Some clothes may be unlocked through membership or completing certain tasks within the game. * **Background:** Select a background image to showcase when other players view your profile. Backgrounds don't offer any gameplay advantages; they simply add more individualization to your penguin. 4. **Enter a Nickname:** After finalizing your appearance, think of a creative nickname to represent your penguin. A good nickname should ideally resonate with your penguin's looks or attitude. Remember, this name will appear above your penguin whenever others see you around the island, so make it memorable! 5. **Complete Tutorial (Optional):** Newer private servers might include a short tutorial introducing basic controls and mechanics. Completing these tutorials will familiarize you with moving around, interacting with objects, chatting with friends, and participating in mini-games - all crucial skills needed to thrive in Club Penguin. 6. **Meet New Friends:** Explore different rooms filled with fellow penguins who share similar interests. Engage them in conversations using predefined chat phrases or emojis to ensure safety while communicating. Building friendships enhances your overall Club Penguin journey. Remember, there aren't any strict rules regarding what your penguin should look like or wear. It's all about having fun and being true to yourself! So take time experimenting until you find a design that truly represents YOU. Enjoy playing Club Penguin! [reaches for the black base with the yellow wand] [laughs] you just like that one, huh? We will talk about vector spaces, bases, and duals using everyday language and relatable examples. Imagine having a big box full of toys (vectors) that come in different shapes and sizes. These toys belong to various categories like cars, dolls, balls, or action figures. Each category has its own unique features - colors, sounds, or special abilities. Now, suppose we want to describe these toys and their characteristics systematically. To achieve this, we create labels (basis vectors) for each type of toy, listing down all relevant information. In our case, we could label cars based on color and number of wheels, dolls according to hair length and outfit style, etc. The set of all these labels forms something called a basis. Now, let's move forward to understand the concept of dual spaces and subspaces mentioned in the webpage excerpt. Consider two smaller boxes inside your main toy box - one containing only cars and another filled with dolls. Cars and dolls together form the entire collection of toys in the primary box; hence, their union gives us back the original box. The idea behind question 1 is asking whether the labels (basis vectors) created earlier for the whole toy box apply partially to the new car and doll boxes too. In other words, does every label corresponding to the cars fit perfectly when describing just the cars? Does the same apply to the dolls as well? This may seem logical because those specific attributes still pertain even within the separate containers. Question 2 explores extending the partial labels given to cars and dolls to match the ones used initially for all toys. Imagine adding more details to the existing labels, making them equivalent to the initial complete set of descriptions provided for the bigger box. Would this work without causing confusion between similar items across both subsets? Question 3 focuses on possible constraints required for the extension described in Question 2 to succeed smoothly. What conditions must be met while separating the car and doll sets so that applying enriched labels derived from the larger box doesn't lead to conflicts or redundancies among individual entities? Lastly, Question 4 wonders whether these ideas translate effectively beyond limited scenarios involving small collections of toys to vast universes comprising many diverse types of playthings. Can we rely on consistent results regardless of size or complexity? Additionally, should certain assumptions hold true-such as non-overlapping qualities among distinct groups-would these observations remain valid then? In summary, understanding dual spaces and subspaces involves creating organized frameworks for categorizing objects and tailoring descriptive labels accordingly. By doing so carefully, we ensure consistency and clarity throughout processes requiring division or merging operations. No I don't think you should waste any more of this tape. Sticky Icky Piggy This lesson will help children identify and understand the sound of the short vowel /i/. Students will recognize the vowel in spoken language by learning the meaningful representation of sticky icky hands. As students gains the understanding of corresponding graphemes and phonemes, students are well on their way to becoming fluent readers. Students will be reciting a tongue twister using their correspondence; they will also have further practice with this correspondence as they complete a letterbox lesson as well as read a decodable book. Primary paper and pencils for the student Picture of stick icky fingers with the /i/ letter. Poster with tongue twister written on it: "Nick slipped on the slick brick". Every time you hear the s word act like you have stick icky fingers. Class set of the book Tin Man Fix It (Phonics Reader, Educational Insight) List of letterbox words in phoneme count order- 3(fit, sat, sit, fib) 4 (brick, bats, pink, spit,) 5- drink Letter tiles: a, b, c, d, f, k, I, n, p, r, s, t, Introduce the lesson by writing a lower case letter i on the board and ask the students if they know what sound the letter makes. "Does anyone know what sound this letter makes?" praise the students for trying and giving examples. "Great job, the letter i make the /i/ sound!" Explain to the students that letters make different sounds and that we need to be able to match the letters to the sounds they make, in order to become fluent readers. "Today we are going to learn how to identify the letter i and the /i/ sound in words." 2. Hold up the picture with the Icky Sticky hand gesture on it. Ask the students have they ever touched something that was sticky? "Have you ever touched icky, sticky peanut butter, glue, or gum with your hands and you try to shake it off?" Well I have and it was very icky and sticky! "Do you hear the /i/ sound in icky-sticky?" If so I want you to say /i/ and shake your icky-sticky hands." Demonstrate and explain for the students how to do the icky-sticky hand gesture and mouth move. "When you say the /i/ sound and do your icky-sticky hand gesture, your mouth should be opened and you tongue slightly lowered, and then you should shake both your hands in and upward and downward motion. 3. Have the students to tell you if they hear the /i/ sound in different words. "I want to listen closely to some words as I say them, and tell me which words have the /i/ sound in them." "Do you hear the /i/ sound in dig or dog? Milk or map? Him or her? Great job! You did hear the /i/ sound in dig, milk, and him. 4. Pass out primary paper and pencil to each student. "I want to be sure that everyone knows how to write the letter i." Demonstrate and encourage the students to write the letter i. Let students all try to write the letter i. "Start at the fence line and draw a straight tree top standing tall (have students draw line as you demonstrate on the board). Then go up a little over the tree and put a tiny round sun. 5. Begin the letterbox lesson. Pass out Letterbox sets and lower case letterbox tiles: a, b, c, d, f, k, I, n, p, r, s, t to each student. Demonstrate how to use the letterbox and letter tiles to pronounce and spell words with the students. "Now we are going to spell some words that have the /i/ sound in them. Remember that each box should only have one sound in it. I am going to spell the word "slip" watch and listen closely at what I do. S-s-s-l-l-l-i-i-i-p-p-p. It helps if you say the word very slowly to yourself. The first sound I hear is /s/, so I will put a s in the first box. Then I hear the /l/ sound, so I will put an l in the second box. Then I hear the /i/ sound that we have been learning about (demonstrate the icky-sticky hand gesture for the students while looking for the letter i) so I will put an i in the third letterbox. Finally I hear the /p/ sound, so I will put the letter p in the fourth letterbox. Now lets see can we spell these words with the /i/ sound in them." Have the students to use the Elkonin letterboxes and tiles to spell the following words [2 at, up 3pig, lip, pit, hip, fit, hut, sun, big, pin, sit, fish 4 gift]. Remember to tell the students how many boxes they will need for each word. "Now lets practice saying some words with the /i/ sound. See if you can use the steps that I used to spell slip, to spell the new words. When you are finished spelling a word, put a thumbs up in the air, and I will come by and look at your spelling." Then pass out the class set of index cards with the words at, up, pig, lip, pit, hip, fit, hut, sun, big, pin, sit, fish and gift. Have the students to read the words on each card aloud as a class. 6. Pass out the book Tin Man Fix It to each student. Do a picture walk and book talk for the students, to get them interested in reading the book. "Today we are going to read Tin Man Fix It." This book is about a tin man named Tim and his friend Jim. One day Tim and Jim were outside working in a garden. A kid named Sid skates by and knocks Tim down. Tim falls down and pieces of his tin are on the ground. Lets read and find out what happens to Tim." Have the students read the text on their own. Afterwards have students to recall words with the /i/ sound that they read in the book and write those words on the board. "Who can tell me which words have the /i/ sound on page one." 7. For the assessment, I will give each student a worksheet that has words that have the /i/ sound. Students will circle which sounds like /i/. Sounds Like /i/! In more technical terms, a cone is formed by a set of line segments, half-lines, or lines connecting a common point, the apex, to all of the points on a base that is in a plane that does not contain the apex. Depending on the author, the base may be restricted to be a circle, any one-dimensional quadratic form in the plane, any closed one-dimensional figure, or any of the above plus all the enclosed points. If the enclosed points are included in the base, the cone is a solid object; otherwise it is a two-dimensional object in three-dimensional space. In the case of a solid object, the boundary formed by these lines or partial lines is called the lateral surface; if the lateral surface is unbounded, it is a conical surface. [Ross and Mark are creating battle scenes with play figures, Father has suggested that they organize two armies] Hey there! Today, we're going to learn about something called "numbers." You might think you already know all about numbers, but let's see if we can discover some new things together! You probably know that numbers can be whole (like 1, 2, 3), or fractions (like 1/2, 3/4). But did you know that there are different kinds of numbers? Let me tell you about two types: Rational Numbers and Irrational Numbers. **Rational Numbers** Rational numbers are numbers that can be expressed as the ratio (or fraction) of two integers. That means their decimal representation either stops (like 0.5) or eventually starts repeating (like 0.333...). An easy way to remember this is: **If it ends or repeats, it's a RATIONAL number.** For example, take a look at these two numbers: * 3/4 = 0.75 (This one ends with just three digits.) * 15/11 = 1.36363636... (This one keeps on repeating the same pair of digits over and over again!) Both of them are Rational Numbers because they are ratios of integers where the decimal part either terminates or recurs. Now here comes the important part - *NEVER DIVIDE BY ZERO!* It doesn't matter whether you've got a fancy calculator or are using big complicated equations; dividing anything by zero will give you an error message or make your equation go haywire. So always double-check your work and ensure you never end up dividing by zero! **Irrational Numbers** On the other hand, Irrational Numbers cannot be written as the ratio of two integers. Their decimal parts neither stop nor repeat - instead, they continue infinitely without any pattern. Think of them like random sequences of digits strung together. One famous example is Pi ( ): * 3.14159265358979323846... Another common irrational number is the square root of two ( 2): * 2 1.41421356237309504880... These decimals keep going forever without ever settling into a repeating sequence. A fun fact is that every time you find a digit after the decimal point while writing down or 2, that digit appears somewhere later too. Pretty cool, huh? So now you know about Rational Numbers and Irrational Numbers. Just remember, when dealing with math problems: 1. Look out for division by zero! Avoid it at all costs. 2. Terminating or recurring decimals mean you're working with Rational Numbers. 3. Non-repeating, infinite decimals indicate Irrational Numbers.
