Look it the blonde with the pants Josh [exclamation]. [wants to go back to water hydrant?] page > Lesson Plans > Language Arts General > Charlotte's The classic children's novel by E.B. White opens a world of possibilities for classroom projects and lesson plans. Full of themes involving friendship, loyalty, farm animals, this list goes on and on. In fact, we have a rather long list, submitted by another one of the dedicated contributors on our T2T mailing list. While these ideas aren't complete lesson plans, we know a little creativity can go a long way. - Fern thinks that her father is being unreasonable about getting rid of the smallest pig. He says, "A weakling makes trouble." What does that mean to you and what did that mean to Fern? (Fern describes her father's behavior as an injustice to all small and weak. She asks if her own life would have been taken if she were not born an appropriate - What does Wilbur do that shows that although he is cute and cuddly, he still behaves like a pig? (He grunted, he poked around in the straw with his snout, he played in the mud that was warm and moist, and delightfully sticky and oozy.) - The author describes things in this chapter by referring to the senses. Can you find examples of things that the author lets you see, hear, smell, or feel? (The barn smelled of hay, manure, the perspiration of tired horses, the sweet breath of patient cows, grain, harness dressing, axle grease, rubber boots and fish. See: ladders, grindstones, pitch forks, monkey wrenches, scythes, etc., Feel: warm in winter, cool in summer; Hear: the animals, the people yelling, Wilbur eating, Lurvy hammering.) Why do you think the other animals got excited when Wilbur got free? (They were happy that an animal got free; they knew that the people would try to catch him.) Was the goose trying to be helpful when she told Wilbur how to get out or as she trying to get him into trouble? (probably helpful but she might have gotten tired of listening to him - Why do you think someone would want to be Wilbur's friend? If you were there how would you convince someone that Wilbur would make a good - What does, "Your stomach is empty and your mind is full" mean? Can you think of a character in another story who seemed cruel at first but was actually good and true in the end? (The Selfish Giant,The Grinch, etc.) *But what a gamble friendship is. - "Early summer days are a jubilee time for birds" - Are there other animals or insects that you think about as celebrating at this time of the year? - The animals think that the humans are in a conspiracy about butchering the pigs; what do you think the humans would say? - Does Mr. Arable really believe that animals talk or is he teasing Mrs. Arable? * Kids think they hear all sorts of things. - Charlotte says, "I know a good thing when I see it. I stay put and wait for what comes." How does she say that people act? Why does a spider need legs with seven sections? (for crafting the webs) What could you do if your legs had seven sections? How old do you think Charlotte is? What do you think her advice to Wilbur makes her sound - Fern and Avery like to swing. What other things do they like to do that you like to do? (catch a frog, pick raspberries and eat them, want to build a treehouse) How did the various people and animals react to the egg? (Fern screamed, Avery jumped and ran, the animals complained about the odor; Lurvy complained, then covered the egg with dirt.) * People are not as smart as bugs. * Children almost always hang onto things tighter than their parents think they will. - How did the different people react to the words in the web? (Lurvy got faint and then knelt and said a prayer; Lurvy and Mr. Zuckerman trembled; Lurvy and the Zuckermans stared for an hour; Mr. Zuckerman went to talk to the minister; people came from all over to stare; the Zuckermans got so busy with visitors that they forgot to do other things on the farm.) This chapter contains several similes. Tell the children that a simile is a comparison that contains like or as. Give an example. Similes - Grass looked like a magic carpet. Asparagus patch looked like a silver forest. The web glistened in the light and made a pattern of loveliness and mystery, like a delicate veil. *Secrets are hard to keep. - How do you think Templeton feels about what the old sheep says? (He is worried. He doesn't want to have to do things for others but he knows that his life depends on it.) * People believe almost everything they see in print. - How do you think Lurvy feels about Wilbur when he finds out all the extra work he has to do? (Lurvy might feel proud of Wilbur and want him to look good, or he could feel angry that now he has extra work to do.) Is radiant a good description for Wilbur? What word would you - Dr. Dorian says two very important things: "Children pay better attention than grownups" and "Perhaps if people talked less, animals would talk more." Do you agree with him? - If the crickets warn everybody that summer is almost over can you think of some animals and insects that would listen, and what would they do to get ready for the fall and winter? Are you versatile? How? Can you think of someone else who is? When Wilbur says that he realized that friendship is one of the most satisfying things in the world what did he mean? - How do you think Charlotte and Templeton will help at the fair? - Why doesn't "Uncle" have a real name? Do you think that has anything to do with the way he treats others? - What do you think is happening to Fern that she is suddenly so interested in Henry Fussy? (Fern is growing up; she is interested in boys.) Why does Templeton call Charlotte "you old schemer"? Is that supposed to be a compliment? - Charlotte calls her egg sack a "magnum opus" and a "masterpiece." What does this say about how she feels about her eggs? Why do you think so many eggs are contained in the egg sack? (Many of the new spiders will be killed. It is important that there are a lot of them so they will live.) When Fern starts to cry do you think it is because of Wilbur? (probably not, she wants to go and find Henry) - Why does Wilbur get an award? (because he attracts so many visitors to the fair) What do you think it says on his bronze medal? - This chapter covers such important and sensitive information that it is best enjoyed as is. - What would you tell Joy, Aranea, and Nellie about Wilbur? - Students are asked to select two characters from the book and complete sentences that identify when the character behaved that particular way. - Students are asked to identify occasions when Templeton is helpful to the other characters in the book. Students check off boxes as they complete the assignment. - Students are asked to design the medal that Wilbur wins at the fair. - Students are asked to illustrate the sights, sounds, tastes, and smells of either the farm or the fair. - Dream Catcher - Students follow directions to make a dream catcher like the Chippewa and other Native Americans once made. The dream catcher is similar in appearance to a spider web; here bad dreams get caught and disappear when the sun comes up while good dreams float through the web, down the feather, and onto the person sleeping beneath |Dream Catcher Instructions White paper plate, 9" Yarn, about 12" Beads, a feather Masking tape, pencil, scissors, hole punch Draw a ring inside the rim of the paper plate. Cut out the center of the plate to the inside edge of the ring. Then cut off the outside rim of the plate to the outside edge of the ring. Punch about 16 holes around the ring. Wrap masking tape around one end of the yarn. Push the taped end of the yarn through a hole and pull through leaving about 3" extending out. Start to make a web by pulling the yarn through another hole and crisscrossing the yarn across the center to fill every hole. End the web by bringing the taped end of the yarn back to the first hole and tying to the other end. Cut a piece of the remaining yarn and draw it through the bottom 2 holes. Even the ends and place beads on them. Slip a feather into the beads. Hang the dream catcher. - Another example is of how Charlotte's Web can be a great opportunity to review nouns and teach adjectives. One contributor writes: "It's full of singular, plural, possessive, common, proper nouns & 'terrific' adjectives. In October, we brainstormed for adjectives that express characteristics (specifically emotions and feelings). Then each child selected an adjective and created a pumpkin that illustrated this adjective. They used markers & scrap construction paper to create these personality pumpkins. We put them on the hallway board, added a pig & spider, & chose a caption (Awesome adjectives, Charlotte!).You could use something besides a pumpkin. I just do it in October it helps me avoid the more traditional jack-o-lanterns." - Guide the students through a discussion on the qualities of a friend. List their responses. Have them match the traits from their list with the friendship between Charlotte and Wilbur. (This could be extended into a prewriting activity and even into a first draft writing.) - Have students draw their favorite characters. Then they can add things to the picture that the character likes, such as Wilbur eating any food that is given to him. - Write about the adventures of one of Charlotte's children. - Create a "billboard ad" for the County Fair. In the ad, they should feature Zuckerman's pig. - This story is full of facts about spiders. Have the students create a learning log in which they will record different "spider facts" that the learn. They can also add pictures and any other data that they discover. Some things that they could find in the story include: how a spider spins its web, the parts of a spider, different webs made by the different kinds of spiders, how a spider catches its prey (warning: This is somewhat gory), correct terminology (spiderling, spinneret, etc.) In addition, there are many facts about farm life. The students could compile information on how a farm operates and write a short report on it. This could be an oral presentation instead of a writing assignment. - Have the students act out their favorite parts of the stories. One student could be a narrator. *Older students could write out their favorite part as a script, and the students could make simple masks to "become" the character whom they are portraying. - An older class may want to collaborate with a younger class on this. The older class could design a simple puppet show and work with younger students in presenting it to a third class. - Take turns having students pretend that they are Wilbur. Have the rest of the class represent spiderlings asking questions they have about their mother, Charlotte. - Make a list of attractions at a county fair and of food that you can purchase at a fair. Include a price for each item. Then have the students make up "word problems" using the information. The problems can be written on index cards (one per card) and traded so that others can solve them. (Later these could be part of a classroom center or work station activity.) Note: The person who makes up the problem must be able to solve it also! Have the students make up the sign for the attractions and food. They can also include a price. - Have the students compare and contrast the novel with the play. A Venn diagram is a good graphic organizer for this activity. - Charlotte pays the ultimate price for her friendship with Wilbur. What is it? Why would she do such a thing? How does Wilbur try to "repay" - Have the students examine the character traits of Charlotte, Wilbur, Templeton, and several others in the barnyard gang. Have them discuss how the "personalities" of each contributed to the harmony (or disharmony) of living within the barnyard community. Our own hometowns? - If the student were the director, what famous person would he or she get to play the different roles in the play? (You can also purchase plastic webs.) - Write poems about friends: line 1 and 5 are the name of the friend, line 2 is things the friend likes to do, line 3 and 4 are a few adjectives that describe the friend. - Make a diorama of either Zuckerman's farm, the barn, or the county - Write a letter to Wilbur giving him advice about his loneliness - Design a birth announcement for Wilbur - Write a journal entry telling about Fern and Wilbur's first meeting - Make a "friendship chart" (similar to a family tree) and add to it as you read each chapter - Assign the roles of Wilbur, Fern, and the new babies and have students write and develop a dialogue telling the new babies about their mother. In 1939, people studying physics began to understand the theory of nuclear fission weapons, but no country knew how to build one. When World War II started, Germany, the United Kingdom and the United States wanted to build nuclear weapons. Germany did not finish building them, partly because many of the best people studying physics fled Germany after Nazi rule started. The United Kingdom started working in 1939, but it took too much money, so they gave up in 1942. Later that year, the United States started a very large program to build nuclear weapons. It built upon the work done in the United Kingdom. The program was called the "Manhattan Project". Um he's not real big into stuffed animals, although he thought the frog was pretty funny. Some languages have someone to decide the correct spelling, such as the Acad mie fran aise. English does not. English orthography was the work of the early printers. They had to decide how particular words would be spelled in their books. Gradually the number of alternative spellings began to drop. The word which is "merry" today was spelled in about 30 ways in written sources from the 9th to the 16th century. And he gave a name to the little girl. In English, the order of the words is very important. For example, the sentences "Is it?" and "It is." mean different things. Most other international languages have similar problems: in French, Arabic or Hebrew, new readers have difficulty learning to decode words. As a result, children learn to read more slowly. In both Spanish and Italian there is a more direct connection between spelling and pronunciation. Those are languages with low orthographic depth. Humans Affecting the Ocean Habitat Teacher of Lesson: Teresa Moore Grade Level: 2/3 - Students will be able to share some troubles (caused mainly by humans) facing sea turtles. - Students will be able to answer some questions about sea turtles using a fact sheet. (all can be seen on my game board) - Photocopied game boards and pieces - "Turtle Talk" fact sheets - Pass out a sheet of paper with "Turtle Facts" to every student. Have them read along as I read the first paragraph to them. "Here are a list of facts about the sea turtle. Can someone tell me how long the sea turtle can live?" Give them a chance to search the list and raise their hands. Give a turtle playing piece to the table the correct response came from. (incentive) Ask a few more random questions from the list. Keep giving out game pieces to the groups (don t give out more than the number sitting at that group). After awhile stop the questioning. - Show them the game board I made for "Race to the Sea". "What do you think the main objective of this game is? Listen to the title and think about what we just talked about." Open up the game board and tell them that the objective is to get your turtle piece to the end first by rolling a die. Sound easy? Well, everyone s playing piece represents 25 baby sea turtles who just hatched from their nest on the beach. Along the way, you may land on a space that sends you back a few spaces or kills several of your turtles! On the back of your fact sheet I want everyone to write 25. Now put your pencils down again. That's how many sea turtles you are beginning with. If I lost 11 turtles on a space, what would I need to do? (25 - 11 = 14) I keep subtracting along the way. If I run out of turtles before reaching the ocean, I lose the game. - One person in each group raise your hand if there aren't enough pieces at your table for everyone to play. (pass out remaining pieces) - When I hand out a die to each group, roll to see who goes first. Then take turns going around the circle to the first person's left. Remember to keep track of how many turtles you have on your fact sheet. After we play for awhile, I will have everyone stop. If you're not done with your game, you can continue it after our discussion if we have any remaining time. You may start as I pass out the dice. - Circulate as they play. Ask occasionally how many turtles they have left and how they are losing them. - Stop playing the game. Collect all the dice. Call them to gather in a group on the carpet. "How did you do? Did anyone lose all of their turtles? How many had more than 5? 10? 15? 20? Did anyone finish with all of them? What were some of the problems you ran into?" Listen to responses from the class. Ask how they feel about each of these problems. "Is there anything we can do to change it? Would you do the same thing?" - Give them a summary of "Troubles for Turtles". They don t have a copy, but they can look at the list on my game board later. I will put it in our unit game area for when they finish their work. Talk some more about the problems people cause for sea turtles through this listing. As a wrap up, ask everyone to think of one of the problems facing the sea turtles that bothers them the most. When they tell me what bothers them and why, they can return to their seat to do their individual work. - Listen to their comments regarding the troubles they faced during the game to see if they learned something from playing. Listen again to each person after the final discussion on "Troubles for Turtles". Was everybody able to remember a problem that I introduced? Did they voice an opinion about that problem? - Did all the tables receive at least two game pieces for answering my "Turtle Talk" questions (fact sheet)? Follow up/ Teaching Strategies: - Have them fill out a small worksheet using their fact sheet to answer questions. - Have them pick a sea turtle problem and write an opinion paper on it. - Have them color and construct more folder game boards for free time use by the class. The word "Dutch" itself came from the Proto-Germanic word "", which means "language of the common people" and then was also used to refer to the Germans and their language. Before agriculture, humans were hunter-gatherers. They looked for fruit, nuts, stems, leaves, corms, and tubers, scavenged for dead animals and hunted living ones for food. Growing crops in a forest clearing is thought to be the first example of agriculture. Useful types of plant were grown while unwanted plants were removed. Title: How to Change Your Age on Discord Discord is a popular communication platform that allows users to chat, voice call, and video call with others. When you first create your Discord account, you are asked to provide your date of birth. However, if you made a mistake or want to change your age for any reason, this guide will show you how to do so. Please note that Discord has certain restrictions based on age, such as access to NSFW (Not Safe For Work) servers, which may require verification through email or SMS. Changing your age might affect these settings. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Make sure you have access to the email address associated with your Discord account. You'll need to confirm changes via an email link. * Be aware that changing your age too frequently could lead to temporary or permanent suspension of your account. Use this feature responsibly. * If you encounter issues while following this guide, contact Discord Support at <https://support.discord.com/> for assistance. **Step 1: Log into Your Discord Account** Visit the Discord website (<https://discord.com/>) and click 'Log in' at the top right corner. Enter your registered email address and password, then press 'Login'. This action grants access to your account settings. **Step 2: Access User Settings** Once logged in, look at the bottom left corner of the screen where your profile picture appears. Click on the gear icon next to it to open User Settings. **Step 3: Navigate to My Account** Inside User Settings, find the menu on the left side of the page. Scroll down until you see 'My Account', and click on it. The page should display basic information about your account, including username, email, and date of birth. **Step 4: Edit Date of Birth** Next to the 'Date of Birth' field, there should be an 'Edit' button represented by a pencil icon. Click on the 'Edit' button. A new window will appear asking you to input your new birthday details. Remember that only one digit can be changed per day when updating your age. **Step 5: Save Changes** After entering the correct date of birth, click on the blue 'Save' button located below the date picker. A confirmation message will pop up saying that an email was sent to verify the update. **Step 6: Confirm Email Verification** Check your email inbox for a message from Discord titled "[Action Required] Verify your New Date of Birth". Open the email and click on the green 'Verify Now' button inside. It should redirect you back to Discord and notify you that your date of birth has been successfully updated. **Step 7: Check Updated Information** To ensure everything went smoothly, log out of your Discord account and sign back in again. Then repeat Steps 2-3 to navigate to your user account information. The revised date of birth should now be displayed correctly. Congratulations! You've learned how to change your age on Discord. Always remember to adhere to community standards and avoid abusing this feature to maintain a positive experience for all users. I only have cheese bits once a day, and then it's done for it. Ear wax is a normal product of the ear which protects the skin of the ear from water and infection. Ear wax is formed from wax glands in the external ear canal as well as other components such as dead skin, sweat, and oil. The primary component of ear wax is keratin (derived from dead skin). Ear wax thus differs slightly from cerumen which is the secretory product of the ceruminous glands in the external auditory canal (Hawke, 2002). What can go wrong with ear wax? 1. Wax can plug up the ear, causing hearing to be reduced, and a full feeling in the ear. This is called an "impaction". About 2-6% of the population has an ear wax impaction at any time. 2. Wax can trap bacteria in the ear, leading to infection. This is usually painful or at least itchy. 3. Ear wax can obscure vision when the doctor looks in your ears, possibly hiding a dangerous process. 4. Ear wax is the most common cause of hearing aid malfunction. Back to top Title: How to Get a Hot Guy and Keep Him Introduction: This guide is designed to help women attract and maintain relationships with physically attractive men. It's important to remember that every individual is unique, and there is no one-size-fits-all approach to dating. However, this guide provides general advice based on psychological research and anecdotal evidence. Always treat potential partners with respect and kindness, and prioritize your own self-worth and happiness. Step 1: Know Your Value Before seeking a relationship, it's crucial to understand your worth as an individual. Focus on personal growth, set realistic goals, and develop confidence in yourself. This will make you more appealing to high-quality men who value emotional intelligence and independence. Key Tip: Engage in activities that challenge you and boost your self-esteem, such as exercise, hobbies, or volunteering. Guideline: Avoid basing your self-worth on external factors like appearance or validation from others. Step 2: Identify What You Want Determine the qualities you seek in a partner beyond physical attraction. Consider shared values, interests, and life goals. Having clear expectations can help you find a compatible match and prevent wasting time on unsuitable candidates. Key Tip: Make a list of non-negotiable traits (e.g., honesty, ambition) and desirable but flexible ones (e.g., sense of humor, musical talent). Guideline: Remember that nobody is perfect; focus on finding someone who complements you rather than expecting perfection. Step 3: Present Your Best Self Attractive men are often sought after, so standing out requires making a positive impression. Dress appropriately for various situations, practice good hygiene, and display confident body language. Show genuine interest in getting to know him by asking thoughtful questions and actively listening to his responses. Key Tip: Be authentic; pretending to be someone you're not is unsustainable and may lead to disappointment later on. Guideline: Respect his space and avoid being overly aggressive or clingy, which can be off-putting. Step 4: Build Emotional Connection Develop a strong bond through open communication, vulnerability, and trust. Share your thoughts, feelings, and experiences, and encourage him to do the same. By creating a deep emotional connection, you become more than just a pretty face to him. Key Tip: Practice active listening skills, such as summarizing what he said and asking follow-up questions to show understanding and engagement. Guideline: Establish boundaries early on and respect them to foster mutual trust and commitment. Step 5: Maintain Attraction Keep the spark alive by continuing to invest in your appearance, both physically and emotionally. Surprise him with romantic gestures, plan date nights, and express appreciation regularly. Additionally, continue growing as an individual to ensure personal fulfillment and compatibility. Key Tip: Encourage him to pursue his passions and support his endeavors; this demonstrates investment in his well-being and happiness. Guideline: Balance independence with interdependence; maintaining separate identities while nurturing a shared bond strengthens the relationship overall. Conclusion: Remember that attracting and keeping a hot guy involves more than just physical appeal. Cultivate inner beauty, establish clear expectations, present your best self, build emotional connections, and maintain attraction. Most importantly, always prioritize your own self-worth and happiness. Around the city there are a series of lakes and cultural landmarks. In 1974, Marvin returned officially to touring after a four-year sabbatical following the death of Tammi Terrell and began to suffer from the effects of a long-developing cocaine addiction, which started in the late sixties. Marvin went a year without producing a record though through yet another new Motown contract, he built a recording studio in the middle of Sunset Boulevard. In 1976, Marvin released the erotic "I Want You" album, an album that had been delayed by his first wife, Anna Gordy's divorce case against him. The divorce was finalized after Marvin agreed to remit a portion of his royalties from his next album to Ms. Gordy. The result was the controversial "Here, My Dear" project, which came out in early 1979 after two years of holding the project from release. Marvin recorded a disco hit in 1977 titled "Got to Give It Up". The song hit number-one marking his third pop hit to reach the spot and his twelfth on the R&B side. It would end up being his last major hit with the Motown label. Following the release of "Here, My Dear", the album tanked and Marvin, already run dry by dwindling finances, numerous lawsuits, a troubling marriage to a second wife and an even more troubling cocaine addiction, eventually left his wife, three children, and the U.S. settling first in Hawaii and then settling in London in 1980 after a European promoter advised him to tour Europe. Recording most of his final Motown release, "In Our Lifetime", in London, the album was rush-released by Motown in early 1981 much to Marvin's chagrin: he complained that the label had edited certain songs, including one that seemed unfinished, and had taken off a question mark that he put at the end of the title to ask a question about life. Marvin stated he would no longer record for Motown and was granted his wish when CBS Records brought him out of his contract in April of 1982. Title: How to Answer When Asked What You Like About Someone Introduction: When someone asks you what you like about them, it can be a nerve-wracking moment. It's important to answer honestly and thoughtfully, as this question provides an opportunity to strengthen your relationship with that person. This tutorial will guide you through answering this question in a clear, specific, and positive way, fostering understanding and appreciation between you and the other individual. Step 1: Reflect on Your Thoughts Before responding, take a few moments to gather your thoughts. Consider what initially drew you to this person, their unique qualities, actions, or behaviors that make them special in your eyes. By doing so, you ensure your response is genuine and heartfelt. Key Tip: Avoid generic compliments. Instead, focus on personal traits or experiences that have positively impacted you. Step 2: Use Specific Examples Frame your responses using concrete examples rather than broad statements. For instance, instead of saying "I like your sense of humor," say something like, "Your quick wit always makes me laugh, especially during our game nights." Being specific demonstrates sincerity and shows that you truly pay attention to the details of your interactions. Guideline: Choose recent events over distant memories. Recalling shared experiences from the past week or month reinforces your connection and keeps conversations fresh and relevant. Step 3: Focus on Positive Traits Highlight aspects of the person's character that are inherently good, such as kindness, patience, or empathy. Drawing attention to these admirable traits encourages further development while also expressing gratitude for who they are at their core. Key Tip: Be mindful of avoiding superficial attributes like physical appearance. While there may be nothing wrong with appreciating someone's looks, focusing solely on external features may come across as insincere or shallow. Step 4: Express Emotions Reveal the emotions tied to your fondness for this person. Sharing feelings like joy, excitement, or comfort allows the other individual to understand the true extent of your affection and deepens your bond. Example Response: "The warmth and generosity you show towards others never fails to inspire me. I feel incredibly lucky to know someone as caring and compassionate as you." Step 5: Keep Responses Balanced Avoid excessively praising one particular trait or action. Maintain balance by acknowledging multiple facets of the person, ensuring your answer feels comprehensive yet concise. Key Tip: Aim for brevity; three to five short sentences usually suffice. Excessive rambling could dilute the significance of your message or even lead to awkward pauses after finishing your statement. Step 6: Practice Active Listening After sharing your thoughts, give the other person space to respond. Engage in active listening, maintain eye contact, and ask questions if needed. Showing interest in their reaction indicates respect and openness to continued conversation. Conclusion: By following these steps, you can confidently answer when asked what you like about someone. Honesty, specificity, and emotional expression create meaningful connections and allow both parties to grow together. With practice, crafting thoughtful answers becomes second nature, enhancing relationships and building trust among friends, family members, and romantic partners alike. " The radar transmitter's pulses or bursts of radio energy hit and bounce back (they are "reflected") from hard and metallic objects. Hard objects are things like buildings, hills, and mountains. Big things made of metal include aircraft, bridges, or even objects in space, like satellites. The reflected radar energy is detected by radar pulse receivers which use both pulse and digital circuits together. The pulse and digital circuits in radar pulse receivers are used to show the location and distance of objects which have reflected the radar transmitter's high powered pulses. [turns the black base around, so the other side is facing the camera] The fibre gained from the banana plant has been used to make textiles for a long time. In Japan, bananas have been grown to be used for clothing and in the house since at least the 13th century. In the Japanese system, the leaves and shoots are cut from the plant periodically to make sure they are soft. The harvested shoots must first be boiled in lye to prepare the fibres for the making of the yarn. These banana shoots produce fibres of varying degrees of softness. They can be used for yarns and textiles of different qualties, and for specific uses. For example, the outermost fibres of the shoots are the coarsest - they are good for tablecloths. The softest innermost fibres are desirable for kimono and kamishimo. This traditional Japanese banana cloth making process has many steps, all performed by hand. Children will: Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of seasons. Orally use words that describe how the weather is different in different seasons. Membranes in cells are made of lipids (fats) and protein. The lipids keep the inside of the cell or the organelle separate from the outside. The proteins do many things. Title: How to Be a Randomdude in Real Life Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of becoming a randomdude - an individual who is unpredictable, spontaneous, and unique in real life. This lifestyle can lead to exciting experiences, personal growth, and increased creativity. However, remember that being a randomdude should be done responsibly and respectfully towards others. It's essential to maintain balance in your life while embracing this mindset. Step 1: Embrace Uncertainty Explanation: To become a randomdude, start by accepting uncertainty as part of your daily routine. Instead of planning every detail of your day, leave room for improvisation and last-minute decisions. This approach allows you to develop flexibility and adaptability, which are crucial traits for leading a randomlife. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Try new things without overthinking them * Learn to enjoy surprises and unexpected situations * Don't let fear of failure hold you back from taking risks Step 2: Cultivate Curiosity Explanation: Develop a strong sense of curiosity about the world around you. Explore various interests, hobbies, and activities. The more knowledge and skills you acquire, the easier it becomes to incorporate randomness into your everyday life. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Read widely across different genres and subjects * Engage with people from diverse backgrounds * Attend workshops, seminars, or classes to learn something new Step 3: Improve Your Spontaneity Explanation: Practice being spontaneous in small ways before diving headfirst into significant changes. Start making impromptu decisions throughout your day, such as choosing a different route to work or ordering a dish you've never tried at a restaurant. These little acts of spontaneity help build confidence and prepare you for bigger challenges. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Set aside time each day for spontaneous actions * Gradually increase the level of randomness in your choices * Share your experiences with friends and family to gain support and encouragement Step 4: Foster Creativity Explanation: Cultivating creativity goes hand-in-hand with being a randomdude. Encouraging creative thinking enables you to come up with innovative ideas and solutions, both in your personal life and professional endeavors. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Dedicate time to artistic pursuits like painting, writing, or playing an instrument * Surround yourself with inspiring environments and people * Participate in brainstorming sessions or group discussions Step 5: Balance Responsibility and Randomness Explanation: While embracing randomness can bring excitement and novelty to your life, it's vital to strike a balance between responsibility and spontaneity. Maintain commitments to work, school, and relationships while incorporating elements of surprise and unpredictability. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Prioritize important tasks and deadlines * Communicate openly with loved ones about your desire for more randomness * Establish boundaries to ensure stability and consistency in your life Conclusion: By following these steps and maintaining a responsible yet adventurous attitude, you can successfully become a randomdude in real life. Remember always to act with kindness, consideration, and respect towards others as you embark on this journey of self-discovery and exploration. Okay so he hasn't seen that animal before? He took a deep breath and started the exam. He was worried at first, but soon he was having fun. Whenever he got stuck, he'd ring the bell and get help from mum or dad. Arguments for the existence of God typically include metaphysical, empirical, inductive, and subjective types. Some theories are built around holes in evolutionary theory, as well as order and complexity in the world. Arguments against the existence of God typically include empirical, deductive, and inductive arguments. Conclusions sometimes include: "God does not exist" (strong atheism); "God almost certainly does not exist" ("de facto" atheism); "no one knows whether God exists" (agnosticism); "God exists, but this cannot be proven or disproven" (deism or theism); and "God exists and this can be proven" (theism). There are many variations on these positions, and sometimes different names for some of them. For example, the position "God exists and this can be proven" is sometimes called "gnostic theism" or "strong theism". On Monday, he ate through one apple, but he was still hungry. How to Make A Balloon Rocket A balloon rocket is threaded through a long string and will fly across the room with the help of propulsion. We learned how to make it from Arvind Gupta's Toys from the Trash. Rocket engines are reaction engines explained by the Newtonian Principle's Third Law "to every action there is an equal and opposite reaction." A rocket engine is throwing mass in one direction and benefiting from the reaction that occurs in the other direction as a result. When you blow up a balloon and let it go so that it flies all over the room, you have created a rocket engine. In this case, what is being thrown is the air molecules inside the balloon. Many people believe that air molecules don't weigh anything, but they do. When molecules are thrown out the nozzle of a balloon, the rest of the balloon reacts in the opposite direction. Tools and Materials: Paperclip or Wire There are two straws in this rocket design. One is to thread through the string to enable the rocket to fly across the room. The other is to attach and blow up the balloon. It is very important when you're designing your rocket to make sure that it is easy to thread a string through the "string straw" when your rocket is glued shut. This means that string straw should be visible, from both ends, when enclosed in the paper. Step 1: Fold a sheet of paper in half and draw your rocket body. Cut it out. Step 2: Place a ruler next to a drinking straw, measure and mark three inches on the straight end. Cut it. This is your "string straw." Step 4: Place the string straw on the glue. Step 5: Place the bent straw next to it. Step 7: Glue the rocket shut. Step 8: Attach a balloon on the bent end of the straw with a rubber band or electrical tape. Step 9: Bend a paper clip straight or take a stiff piece of wire and tie the string to it. The knot should be small enough that the clip or wire needle will be able to pull the thread through the straw. Step 10: After threading the rocket on the string, get a friend and pull the string tight across a room. Blow up the rocket on the string. Hold the end with your finger to keep the air in the rocket until you are ready to let go. Watch it fly across the string. Step 11: Redesign. How far did your rocket go? If very far, congratulations. If not so far, consider the factors (e.g. weight, friction, amount of air) that slowed it down. Fix it and try again. Note: Step 1 gives a suggestion for a very simple rocket design. Some of our Maker Kids made rockets by folding paper into very intricate paper airplane designs. Anything goes! You engineer the plane that flies the best! Links: Arvind Gupta Toys From Trash balloon rocket http://www.arvindguptatoys.com/toys/balloonrocket.html Where do you want' 'I'm [silver man] to go? We'll make them go all the way across the table. Title: How to Add an Interested Party to Your Renter's Insurance Introduction: Renter's insurance is a type of property insurance that covers losses to personal property and provides liability coverage for incidents that occur within your rented residence. It is essential to add an interested party to your renter's insurance if you have roommates or someone else who has a financial stake in your rental unit. This guide will walk you through the process of adding an interested party to your policy with clear, concise steps and helpful insights along the way. Step 1: Understand Who Qualifies as an Interested Party An interested party can be anyone who shares a financial interest in your rental property. Typically, this refers to co-tenants or roommates listed on the lease agreement. However, other individuals such as mortgage lenders or spouses may also qualify as interested parties depending on specific circumstances and insurer policies. Key Tip: Before proceeding, review your current policy documents and consult with your insurance agent to ensure you understand who qualifies as an interested party according to your insurer's definition. Step 2: Gather Necessary Information To add an interested party to your renter's insurance policy, you need certain information about them, including their full name(s), date of birth, contact details, and any relevant claims history. If they are already insured under another policy, ask if there are additional discounts available for bundling coverages. Guideline: Collect all necessary documentation before reaching out to your insurance company or broker. Having everything organized will streamline the process and minimize potential delays. Step 3: Contact Your Insurance Provider Reach out to your renter's insurance provider by phone, email, or online chat, depending on what communication channels they offer. Explain that you would like to add an interested party to your existing policy and provide them with the required information gathered in Step 2. Key Tip: Be prepared to answer questions regarding the living arrangements, shared belongings, and individual responsibilities between yourself and the proposed interested party. Honesty and transparency during this conversation help maintain accurate coverage limits and avoid future complications. Step 4: Discuss Coverage Options and Limits Discuss various coverage options and limits applicable to the added interested party. You might consider increasing your overall policy limit to account for additional personal property or expanding liability coverage due to increased risk exposure from having more occupants in the rental unit. Guideline: Review your current deductible amount and determine whether adjustments should be made based on the revised policy structure. A higher deductible reduces premium costs but increases out-of-pocket expenses when filing a claim. Step 5: Finalize Policy Amendments Once you and your insurance representative agree upon appropriate changes, request written confirmation detailing the updated policy terms, effective dates, and any associated fees or cost adjustments. Carefully read through the document to ensure accuracy and understanding before signing off on the revisions. Key Tip: Maintain copies of all correspondence related to policy amendments for future reference. Should disputes arise concerning coverage or payments, these records serve as valuable evidence supporting your position. Conclusion: By following the outlined steps, you successfully added an interested party to your renter's insurance policy while ensuring adequate protection for both yourself and those sharing a financial interest in your rental property. Regularly review and update your policy as needed to reflect life changes, new acquisitions, or altered living situations. You don't know what that is I don't think. Learn to Count with Pompom Number Toss Wish your new student would develop a love for math? Play this fun number competition to help! Players toss pompoms into an empty ice tray with numbers in each compartment, then identify the numbers their pompoms landed on. It's a fun way for your child to learn numbers, how to count, and perhaps even get a head start on addition! What You Need: - Old ice tray - Sharpie markers - Pompoms (about 1/2" in diameter) - Paper and pencil What You Do: For preschoolers and kindergarteners: - Use a sharpie to write the numbers 0 through 10 randomly in the bottom of each compartment of the ice tray. It's okay if some numbers show up more than once, as long as every compartment has a number at the bottom. - Select two players, and help each player count out 5 pompoms, for five rounds. - Have the first player toss one pompom into the tray. Remove the pompom and have her identify the number underneath; encourage her to remember the number. - Then the next player goes and does the same thing. Then have both players figure out which number is the higher number by counting to ten. - The player with the higher number is the winner of the round! Play for five rounds, and whichever player wins the most rounds is the winner of the game. There's no need to stop playing after round five if kids are having a fun time playing! Keep going with the game as long as they want! This game helps with number recognition, which will be an important skill in kindergarten. - Your child can also play this game by herself for practice counting and identifying numbers. For first and second graders: - Select at least two players and give each a pencil, a piece of paper, and 6 pompoms. - The first player tosses two pompoms into the tray. Then she takes the pompoms out of the compartments and identifies each number. - Have her write down the first number on the piece of paper in an equation format. For example, if the two numbers that her pompoms landed on are 2 and 6, she would write down 6 + 2 =_. Encourage her to add the numbers together and write down her answer. - Have the next player go and do the same. - Then invite players to figure out which number is the higher number. Have the player with the larger number put a star or checkmark on her piece of paper. - Do this for three rounds, and the person with the most stars or checkmarks is the winner! This game can also be played with subtraction and multiplication! [referring to sandwich, although it must be cool by now.] What's over there do you see some books? [gets up and moves away from the table] Title: How to Change a Background in Adobe Illustrator Introduction: Adobe Illustrator is a powerful vector graphics editor that allows you to create and edit various types of artwork. One common task when working with illustrations is changing or removing the background. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing a background in Adobe Illustrator using different methods. By following these steps, you'll be able to modify your image's backdrop according to your needs and preferences. Method 1 - Changing the Artboard Color: The artboard is the area where you place your artwork. You can change its color as an easy way to replace the existing background. Here are the steps: Step 1: Open your document Launch Adobe Illustrator and open the file containing the background you want to change. If you don't have any specific project, you may start by creating a new one (File > New). Step 2: Select the Artboard Tool From the toolbar, select the Artboard Tool (Shift + O) which looks like a square with arrows pointing outwards from all sides. Step 3: Access the Artboard Options Panel Once you've selected the Artboard Tool, look at the top menu bar for the 'Artboards' panel. Alternatively, press Shift + F7 to bring up this panel directly. Step 4: Edit the Fill Color Inside the Artboards panel, click on the fill icon next to 'Name', then choose a new color either via the color picker or by entering RGB values manually. The entire artboard will now display the chosen color. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Use contrasting colors between the foreground elements and the new background for better visual hierarchy and readability. - Keep consistency across multiple artboards if they share similar themes. Method 2 - Masking Object(s): This method involves masking objects over the original background so effectively hiding it while giving the illusion of a changed background. Step 1: Create a shape or import an image Design a custom shape or import an image that will serve as the replacement background. Place it behind other layers or objects whose visibility you wish to maintain. To do this, ensure the newly created object is below the layer stack order than the ones intended to remain visible. Step 2: Group Layers (Optional) If there are several overlapping layers forming the front design, group them together to make sure they move simultaneously during the masking process. Simply select all relevant layers and hit Ctrl+G (Windows) or Command+G (Mac). Step 3: Apply Clipping Mask Select both the background object (the one placed underneath) and the grouped layer(s)/object(s), right-click and navigate to Make Clipping Mask. Or simply go to Object > Clipping Mask > Make. Now only the portion covered by the uppermost object(s) should be visible. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be mindful about what parts of the design you intend to reveal after applying the clipping mask. It might help to temporarily hide the unwanted elements before proceeding with this technique. - Remember that editing becomes more complex once applied since changes made within the mask affect everything inside it. However, double clicking the masked area lets you isolate it for further modifications. Conclusion: Changing a background in Adobe Illustrator opens numerous possibilities for creativity and enhances the overall appearance of your work. Whether you prefer altering the artboard color or employing advanced techniques such as masks, mastering these skills empowers you to manipulate your designs effortlessly. "It is a thing to look at the stars," Ben says. He remembers seeing a picture of a telescope in a book. "Can we try it?" Anna says. "Yes, but we have to wait for the night," Ben says. They put the telescope in a safe place and go back to play. When the sun goes down, they ask their mom if they can use the telescope. "Okay, but be careful and don't stay up too late," mom says. She helps them set up the telescope in the garden. She shows them how to point it at the sky and adjust the lens. "Now you can gaze at the stars," she says. Anna and Ben take turns to look through the telescope. They see many bright dots and some big ones that look like balls. "Wow, they are so pretty," Anna says. "What are they?" Ben asks. "They are stars and planets," mom says. "They are very far away from the earth." "What is the earth?" Ben asks. "The earth is where we live," mom says. "It is a big ball too, but we can't see it from here." "Can we go to the stars and planets?" Anna asks. "Maybe one day, when you grow up," mom says. "But for now, you can enjoy their gentle light and wonder about them." Anna and Ben nod. They gaze at the stars and planets for a long time. They feel happy and curious. They thank mom for the telescope and give her a hug. Then they go to bed and dream of space. [points to a mother hippopotamus who is trying to climb out of the water onto some stairs] No assembly needed. a) Have students hang the mass over the side of a desk and let the toy walk. Resist the temptation to stop the toy when it gets to the edge! Why doesn't it fall off? How does it "know" when to stop? b) Remove the mass from the front of the toy. Place the toy on the board. Raise the board to an angle that just causes the toy to walk down the ramp. Use the angle of the ramp and the mass of the toy to calculate how much weight would have to be attached to the string to get it to move forward. Why does the toy walk? For the toy to move, you must apply a force that is at least as great as the frictional force trying to stop it. The weight of the mass pulls along the string and provides the force that results in the toy's motion. The string pulls diagonally, though, and only the horizontal component of the force makes the toy move forward. As the toy gets closer to the edge, the angle of the pull changes. The component of force pulling forward gets relatively shorter, and the component pulling down gets relatively longer. At the edge of the table, there is no component of force pulling the toy forward, so it stops! Calculating the pulling mass When the toy stands on the ramp, the force that causes its motion comes from its weight. The weight vector, however, acts perpendicular to the table top, not the ramp. So again we break the vector into its components. The vector that is parallel to the ramp is responsible for the forward motion of the toy. The triangles created by the ramp and table and by the toy and ramp are similar, and the angles indicated are congruent. Measure the angle between the board and the ramp as indicated in the drawing above. Get the mass of the toy. Find the mass of a small paper clip. You are now ready to find the number of paper clips necessary to pull the toy forward. The force required to overcome friction for forward motion can be calculated in the following way: sin = Force pulling /Force toy weight Force toy weight(sin ) = Force pulling Weight of the toy: 0.021kg x 10m/s2 *= 0.21N Weight of one paper clip: 0.00043kg x 10m/s2 = 0.0043N To find the force necessary to overcome friction (the Force pulling ), use 0.21N (sin 4o) = 0.015N 0.015N necessary to pull/.0043N per paper clip = 3.4 or 4 paper clips needed to move the toy forward Attach the four paper clips to the toy, place on a level surface, and check to see if (with an initial push) it continues moving. The toy should move at least until the angle between the string and the toy is reduced to less than 4o. (* 10m/s2 used for simplicity) Heidi Strahm Black And the bus left when I broke my shoelace. Title: How to Use the Instyler Rotating Iron for Perfect Curls The Insstyler rotating iron is a popular styling tool that can help you create beautiful curls with ease. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth guide on how to curl your hair using the Instyler, including helpful tips and guidelines for achieving perfect results every time. **Step 1:** Prepare Your Hair Before starting, make sure your hair is clean, dry, and tangle-free. Apply a heat protectant product to prevent damage from the high temperature of the Instyler. If your hair tends to be frizzy or flyaway, consider applying a smoothing serum as well. Key tip: Using a good quality heat protectant is crucial when working with hot tools like the Instyler. It shields your hair from direct heat exposure, reducing the risk of damage and breakage. **Step 2:** Adjust Settings Plug in the Instyler and set the temperature according to your hair type (lower temperatures for fine or damaged hair, higher temperatures for thicker or coarser hair). Wait until it reaches the desired setting before proceeding. Guideline: Keep in mind that excessive heat can cause irreversible damage to your hair. Always start at lower temperatures and increase gradually if needed. **Step 3:** Section Your Hair Divide your hair into manageable sections. Start by clipping up the top layers so they're out of the way. For longer hairstyles, creating multiple horizontal sections may be necessary. Tip: Working with smaller sections allows for better control over the curling process, resulting in more uniform curls. **Step 4:** Position the Instyler Take down one section of hair and hold it straight out from your scalp. Place the Instyler near the roots, ensuring the directional switch is set to 'rotate.' Insert about two inches of hair between the tourmaline ceramic plates. Important note: The Instyler works by pulling the hair through its spinning barrel. Be careful not to grip too tightly; let the device do most of the work. **Step 5:** Begin Curling Press and hold the power button to activate the rotation function. Slowly glide the Instyler downwards along the length of your hair, following the movement with your free hand to maintain tension. Allow the Instyler to complete a full rotation before releasing the button. Crucial point: Timing is essential here. Releasing the button too soon might result in loose waves instead of tight curls. Practice makes perfect! **Step 6:** Finish Each Section Once you reach the ends of your hair, release the rotation function but keep holding the Instyler for a few seconds to ensure a smooth finish. Gently pull the device away from your head while holding onto the end of your hair. Release, then repeat this process for all remaining sections. Remember: Consistency is key. Try to mimic the same speed and pressure throughout the entire curling process. **Step 7:** Final Touches After finishing all sections, allow your curls to cool completely before brushing them out or styling further. This ensures maximum longevity for your newly created curls. Bonus tip: To add extra volume, flip your head upside down and gently scrunch your curls with your fingers. Spray hairspray lightly to secure the style. With these steps followed correctly, you should now have gorgeous, bouncy curls thanks to your Instyler rotating iron. Enjoy your new look! Title: How to Email Someone Who Has Never Given You Their Email Address Without Sounding Like a Stalker Introduction: In today's digital age, email has become an essential form of communication. However, there might be situations where you need to contact someone who hasn't shared their email address with you. This could be a potential employer, a business associate, or even a friend of a friend. It is crucial to approach this situation tactfully so as not to come off as intrusive or creepy. This wiki-style guide will provide you with a step-by-step process on how to email someone who never gave you their email address without sounding like a stalker. Step 1: Research Before sending out any emails, conduct thorough research about the person. Social media platforms such as LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram can be valuable resources. Look for information that indicates how they prefer to be contacted regarding professional matters. Many people list their email addresses publicly on these sites or mention them indirectly through other posts. Remember to respect privacy settings and only gather information that is openly available. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always ensure your search remains respectful and legal. Avoid crossing boundaries by digging too deep into someone's personal life. - Make sure the platform you are using aligns with the nature of your connection with the person. For instance, LinkedIn would be more appropriate for professional contacts while Facebook may suit social connections better. Step 2: Use Alternative Methods If after extensive searching, you still cannot find an email address, consider alternative methods of contact. A phone call, text message, or direct message on social media could serve as initial points of contact. These channels allow you to ask politely for their email address directly. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be clear about why you want their email address. Explain briefly but genuinely what you hope to discuss via email. - Respect their decision if they decline to share their email address. There could be various reasons behind their refusal, including privacy concerns or simply not wanting to mix work and personal communications. Step 3: Composing the Email Once you have obtained their email address, compose a well-crafted introductory email. Start by greeting them warmly and reminding them how you got their contact info. Briefly explain the purpose of your email, making sure to highlight mutual benefits if applicable. Keep it concise yet engaging to increase the likelihood of a response. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Personalize the email based on whatever knowledge you have gathered during your research phase. Mention common interests, experiences, or goals to establish rapport. - Proofread carefully to eliminate errors and improve readability. An error-free email shows attention to detail and professionalism. Step 4: Follow Up Politely If you haven't received a reply within a reasonable timeframe (a week or two depending on the context), send a polite follow-up email. Express gratitude for considering your request, reiterate the value proposition, and gently nudge them towards responding. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Space out your emails appropriately. Sending multiple chaser emails close together can appear pushy and annoying. - If still no response after the second attempt, accept defeat gracefully. They may not be interested at this point in time, but leaving things on good terms keeps doors open for future possibilities. Conclusion: Reaching out to someone who hasn't given you their email address requires patience, tact, and persistence. By following the steps outlined above, you significantly increase your chances of establishing successful email communication without resorting to stalker-like behavior. Basic Math | Basic-2 Math | Prealgebra | Workbooks | Glossary | Standards | Site Map | Help Two-Digit DivisionNot all of division is done with numbers less than ten (10). As you learn about dividing double-digit numbers, you will see patterns similar to the patterns you saw in multiplication. Take a look at these before we move on... 5 x 5 = 25 25 5 = 5 4 x 7 = 28 28 7 = 4 28 4 = 7 Interesting. Division is like the reverse of multiplication. That makes sense since we keep telling you that multiplication is about putting groups together and division pulls them apart. If you remember the two factors that were used to create a number, the division should be easy. You will be dividing the number twenty-four (24) into smaller parts. What are the possible factors? You know that: 3 x 8 = 24 4 x 6 = 24 6 x 4 = 24 8 x 3 = 24 So if you get the problem 24 4 = ? You know the answer is 6 because 6 x 4 = 24. Long and ShortSometimes the answers will be easy when you wind up with a single number. Those easy problems are short division. The number you are dividing is less than ten (1) times the value of the divisor. Examples of short division include... 28 4 = 7 64 8 = 8 54 9 = 6 99 11 = 9 It's as simple as pie. Sometimes you will start with a dividend that is more than ten times the value of the divisor. It's then time for long division. Some examples... 24 2 = 12 (See how you know have a quotient greater than 9?) 44 4 = 11 (Four goes into forty-four eleven times.) 63 3 = 21 80 5 = 16 The key to solving these problems is to see that the number in the tens column is already divisible by the divisor. In the problem 28 2 = 12, the "2" in 28 is divisible by two, one time. You would write down a one (1) and then move to the ones column and see that the "8" is divisible by two, four times. You then write a four (4) in the ones column of your answer (quotient). When you hit these problems break them apart into bite-sized chunks. (1) Do I have a two-digit number for a divisor? - If yes, ask question 2. - If no, do the division. (2) Is the first number of my dividend bigger than my divisor? - If it is, do the division in the tens column first, then move to the ones column. - If not, you can divide the whole dividend by the divisor and your answer is less than ten. RemaindersYes, there are remainders when you divide two-digit numbers. Remember that the remainder will never be larger than your divisor. It doesn't matter whether your answer is ten or greater, that remainder will always stay smaller than the divisor. As you do long division, you will discover remainders in the middle of your division problems. It won't always be nice and even like when you divide sixty (60) by two (2). That even quotient of thirty (30) is just lucky. Think about the number fifty-four (54). When you divide by two, that 5 is divisible by 2, but the re is a remainder of 1. In long division, you hold onto that remainder for the division in the ones column. So you actually have two divisions. Example: 54 2 = ? (1) 5 2 = 2 with remainder of 1 (2) combine the 1 with the 4 value from the ones column to make 14. (3) 14 2 = 7 (4) Put the two values together to get the answer of 27 Practice, practice, and more practice. * The custom search only looks at Rader's sites. Go for site help or a list of mathematics topics at the site map! copyright 2004-2013 Andrew Rader Studios, All rights reserved. Title: Access Your Computer from Your iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of accessing your computer from your iPhone using remote desktop applications. This can be useful when you need to access files or programs on your computer while away from home or office. We'll cover two popular options - Microsoft's Remote Desktop app and Chrome Remote Desktop - providing detailed steps for both methods. **Method 1: Using Microsoft's Remote Desktop App** Step 1: Prepare Your Computer Firstly, ensure that your computer is set up for remote access. On Windows, this typically involves enabling Remote Desktop connections. Here's how: * Open System Properties (Win + Pause/Break). * Click "Remote" tab. * Check "Allow remote connections to this computer". * Click "Apply" then "OK". Key Tip: Make sure your computer isn't set to sleep mode; otherwise, you won't be able to connect remotely. Step 2: Get The IP Address You'll also need your computer's IP address which you can find by searching 'cmd' in Start Menu, typing 'ipconfig' in Command Prompt window and looking next to 'IPv4 Address'. Step 3: Download And Install The RDP Client Download Microsoft's Remote Desktop app from the App Store onto your iPhone. Once installed, open the app. Step 4: Add A PC Tap the "+" button at the top right corner, select "Add PC", enter the name (any), fully qualified domain name or IP address of your computer, username and password. Step 5: Connect To Your PC Go back to the main screen, tap on the added PC under 'My Desktops', wait for connection establishment. You should now see your computer's desktop mirrored on your iPhone. Guideline: Remember, due to screen size limitations, navigation might be challenging. Use pinch-to-zoom gestures to navigate more comfortably. **Method 2: Using Chrome Remote Desktop** Step 1: Set Up Your Computer Install Google Chrome if you haven't already. Visit the Chrome Web Store, search 'Chrome Remote Desktop' and add it to Chrome. After installation, launch the extension. Step 2: Grant Permissions Click 'Enable Remote Connections', download and install the additional required software. Restart Chrome afterwards. Step 3: Name Your Computer Set a name for your computer, create a PIN with at least six digits. Confirm the PIN. Step 4: Download CRD For iOS Visit the App Store, download and install Chrome Remote Desktop app. Launch the app after successful download. Step 5: Code Verification On your computer, note down the code displayed. Input this same code into your iPhone's CRD app where prompted. Step 6: Connection Request Acceptance Your computer will send a request for connection to your iPhone. Tap 'Accept' on your computer screen. Voila! You are connected. Conclusion: Both these methods allow you to control your computer from your iPhone. However, they have different requirements and nuances. Choose the one that best suits your needs based on compatibility, convenience, and security considerations. In this lesson, students will learn about the many different kinds of trees. When the lesson is complete, you'll have a beautiful paper tree to hang in your classroom. Change the leaves every so often to reinforce the lesson. Students will learn about the growth cycle of a tree, and the various types of trees, by comparing the shapes and sizes of leaves. Fiskars scissors; brown and green construction paper; various types of leaves Maple and Birch Leaf Reproducible 1 (PDF) SET UP AND PREPARE 1. Before the lesson begins, take 5 to 10 minutes to collect leaves with your class. Encourage them to choose leaves that are different shapes, colors, and sizes. Note: If your school is not located near trees, use the Leaf Reproducibles instead. 2. Once you're back in the classroom, ask students to examine and sort their leaves. While they are busy, use a scroll of brown paper and cut out a large tree to hang on a wall in your classroom. Include roots, a trunk, branches, and any other details you'd like. 1. Teach students about the growth cycle of a tree. Label its various parts (trunk, roots, branches) and explain how the changing of the seasons affects the tree. 2. Pass around samples of the leaves you've collected, and have a discussion about how a tree's leaves indicate what type of tree it is. Some questions to ask could be: What shapes do they come in? How many points do they have? Are some round and others not? 3. Briefly review common types of trees with students: Maple, Birch, Ginkgo, Elm 4. Ask students to trace each leaf on a piece of colorful construction paper, and then cut out the shapes. 5. Make a Maple tree by asking the students to attach their Maple leaves to the paper tree's branches. Do the same with the Birch leaves or other types of leaves you've collected. 6. Compare and discuss. For a literature-based tie-in, read The Giving Tree, by Shel Silverstein, to learn how important trees are to the environment. Did we practice an' say the ol' words? Title: How to Deal with Siblings Who Don't Like You Introduction: Having siblings who don't seem to like you can be challenging and emotionally draining. However, improving your relationship is possible with patience, understanding, and effective communication. This guide will provide you with steps and tips to help deal with siblings who don't get along with you. Step 1: Understand the Reasons Behind Their Dislike Explanation: To effectively address the issue, it is crucial to understand why your siblings might not like you. Reflect on past experiences, family dynamics, and individual personalities to identify potential reasons. By recognizing these factors, you can better tailor your approach to resolving conflicts and fostering a healthier relationship. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be honest with yourself during self-reflection. * Consider seeking an outside perspective from trusted friends or relatives. * Avoid jumping to conclusions; instead, focus on gathering information. Step 2: Improve Communication Skills Explanation: Effective communication plays a vital role in any relationship, including sibling bonds. Learning how to express thoughts, emotions, and needs clearly can significantly improve interactions between you and your siblings. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Practice active listening by paying full attention when others speak. * Use "I" statements rather than blaming language (e.g., say "I feel hurt when..." instead of "You always..."). * Show empathy towards your siblings' feelings and viewpoints. Step 3: Set Boundaries Explanation: Establishing clear boundaries ensures that both parties respect one another's physical space, emotional wellbeing, and personal values. It also sets expectations for behavior during interactions, reducing misunderstandings and disagreements. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Clearly communicate what behaviors are unacceptable to you. * Stick to your established boundaries even if your siblings initially resist them. * Revisit and adjust boundaries as necessary over time. Imagine you have a big box of different colored balls, let's say red, blue, and green. You can arrange these balls in many ways, but one interesting way is to create lines or rows with just one color. So, you might have a line of red balls, followed by a line of blue balls, and then a line of green balls. This arrangement continues until you run out of balls. Now, imagine making things more complicated by adding some rules. What if you couldn't repeat the same pattern of colors (red, blue, green) twice in a row? In other words, after placing a red-blue-green sequence, you must change the next set of balls to something like blue-green-red or green-red-blue. This concept is somewhat similar to what mathematicians call "rank 3 subgroups," which involves organizing items (vectors) into groups based on certain rules. When dealing with the Euclidean plane (a fancy name for a flat surface), we want to know if there's a special type of rank 3 subgroup that looks like a circle when seen from above. More specifically, can this subgroup contain infinite points that lie exactly on the edge of the circle? Unfortunately, our ball example doesn't quite match up with this mathematical question because circles and spheres behave differently. However, let's try another analogy. Think of a hula hoop lying flat on the ground. Now, picture several paths going through the center of the hoop made by jumping beans hopping along the inside edge of the hoop. These bean trails represent what mathematicians would call vectors. And since the beans always stay on the inner side of the hoop, their positions form a sort of rank 3 subgroup. But do you think there could ever be an infinite number of beans on the hula hoop itself while still maintaining the rule that each beam follows its neighbor without crossing over them? That's the kind of situation mathematicians are trying to understand! In summary, mathematicians enjoy exploring patterns and rules involving various shapes and locations. They ask questions like whether a specific type of organized structure called a rank 3 subgroup can have an infinite number of elements coinciding with a circle. While explaining this concept fully requires advanced math knowledge, understanding the basics isn't impossible-just remember those colorful balls arranged in unique sequences and the hula hoop full of jumping beans following specific paths! Title: Understanding Patterns in Repeated Multiplication with a Spinner Game Imagine you have a spinner numbered from 1 to 6, and you spin it eight times, writing down each resulting number. One way to find the total number of possible outcomes would be to multiply 6 (the maximum value on the spinner) by itself eight times. But that's a lot of multiplication! Is there another way? Let's explore patterns in repeated multiplication using our spinner game first, then we will discover some cool math tricks! **Spinner Game:** 1. Create a spinner numbered from 1 to 6. You can draw your own or find one online. 2. Spin the wheel eight times, recording each outcome. 3. Determine the total number of unique results. At first glance, finding all possibilities seems challenging. However, let's think about the different combinations involving various numbers of ones, twos, threes, fours, fives, and sixes required to reach our desired power. Let T(n) represent the sum of these series up to the nth power. Then, we want to calculate T(8): T(8)=C(8,0)*1+C(8,1)*2+C(8,2)*3+...+C(8,7)*6 where C(a,b) represents the combination of choosing b items from a set without considering their order. This pattern relates directly to Pascal's Triangle, but instead of adding adjacent values here, we multiply them. To simplify this calculation, consider reducing high powers through integration techniques like those found in calculus called "reduction formulas." While the term may sound complex, its concept boils down to breaking large problems into smaller, manageable pieces. In fact, you already know reduction formulas-you probably call them "simplifying expressions!" Here's a trick for handling exponents when dealing with cosines: Remember that cos (x) equals 1/2 + 1/2*cos(2x)? Well, something interesting happens when applying this idea repeatedly. By employing De Moivre's theorem along with Euler's formula, you arrive at a beautiful expression for cos (x): cos (x) = (1/2)^n * [n choose k] * cos[(2k - n)x], where the symbol indicates summing over integer values of k from 0 to n. Although written differently than our original spinner equation, notice the resemblance between both equations concerning combinations and cosine functions. These connections showcase the elegance of mathematics across varying levels of complexity. So, returning to our spinner game example, although calculating every possibility directly appears daunting initially, exploring underlying patterns and utilizing mathematical tools allow us to break down seemingly insurmountable tasks. Ultimately, understanding reduction formulas helps demystify intricate problems while highlighting the inherent beauty within mathematics.
