[mother is pointing to the minute hand on her wristwatch] Title: Draw an Airy Star: A Step-by-Step Tutorial Introduction: Drawing an airy star can be both fun and rewarding. This type of star is characterized by its lightness and delicate appearance, making it perfect for various art projects or decorative purposes. By following this comprehensive guide, you will learn how to draw an airy star with precision and ease. Let's get started! Materials Needed: * Pencil (preferably HB) * Eraser * Ruler * Compass or circular objects for tracing * Paper Step 1: Prepare Your Workspace Before starting your drawing, ensure that you have enough space on your paper and all necessary materials within reach. This organization will help maintain focus and efficiency throughout the process. Step 2: Outline the Center Point Using your ruler, lightly draw two intersecting lines, forming an 'X'. The center point where they meet serves as the foundation for your airy star. Make sure this central intersection is clear and precise since it determines the symmetry of your design. Key Tip: Keep your pencil strokes light during these initial stages; doing so allows easy erasure if needed without leaving noticeable marks. Step 3: Trace Five Circles With your compass or traced circles from other round objects like bottle caps, trace five equally spaced circles around the central point. These should touch but not overlap one another. For best results, choose three larger circles and two smaller ones - creating alternating sizes gives your airy star more dimension. Step 4: Connect Intersections Locate the points at which each pair of adjacent circles intersect along the horizontal axis (top and bottom). Using straight lines, connect these pairs together. Repeat this step for the vertical axis (left and right), connecting opposite pairs. You now have what resembles a pentagon shape surrounded by five circles. Step 5: Add Curves to Edges Now comes the crucial part that transforms the geometric shape into an airy star. At every corner where two straight lines meet, add curved lines that flow smoothly towards the next intersection point. Essentially, you are replacing sharp angles with gentle arcs while maintaining connection between each point. Key Guideline: As you curve the edges, visualize them being pulled outward gently rather than abruptly changing direction. This mental image ensures graceful transitions between segments. Step 6: Refine Lines and Shading Go over your entire sketch once again, reinforcing any faint lines and ensuring consistency across all sections. Apply subtle shading to enhance depth perception, focusing mainly on areas closer to the center where shadows would naturally occur due to lighting conditions. Step 7: Clean Up and Final Touches Erase unnecessary construction lines carefully, preserving only those integral to the structure of the airy star. If required, refine details further until satisfied with the overall look and feel. Congratulations! You've successfully drawn an airy star. Conclusion: Remember, practice makes perfect. Don't be discouraged if your first few attempts don't turn out exactly as envisioned. With patience and persistence, you'll soon master the technique outlined here. Happy drawing! At the beginning of this week, we learned about the Shift key, combined with the navigation keys, to select text. We learned how to use Alt+Tab to switch back and forth between applications. And we covered Ctrl+C to copy, Ctrl+X to cut, and Ctrl+V to paste. So, let's put all of that together. Suppose you want to copy some text from a Notepad document into an e-mail. Here's what to do: - Hold down the Alt key, and press Tab until Notepad is selected, then let go of the Alt key. - Use the arrow keys or other navigation keys to move to the beginning or end of the text you want to select. - Use Shift key and navigation keys to move to the other side of the text; notice how it highlights as you go as long as you're holding down the Shift key. - Once the text is highlighted, press Ctrl+C. Nothing to seems to happen, but behind the scenes, the text you selected has now been copied to the clipboard. - Now press Alt the key again and hold it down while you hit the Tab key until the e-mail is selected. - Move your cursor using the arrow keys to the point where you want the text to appear. - Press Ctrl+V. The text appears. You've just copied and pasted text between two different applications without using the mouse. Congratulations! Now you got all four of your muffins. " A sundial shows the current solar time during the day. It does this because the sun appears to move through the sky. At different times in the day when the sun is shining, a shadow is cast in different places on the dial. A person marks the dial with the time at a certain shadow. This lets users easily see the time. There are a few commonly seen designs, such as the 'ordinary' or standard horizontal garden sundial. However, sundials can be designed for any surface where a fixed object casts a predictable shadow. They may be horizontal or vertical or tilted. But the just walked to the other side of a flat. Title: Convince Your Parents to Let You Go Out Whenever You Want Introduction: This guide is designed to help you build a strong case for why your parents should trust you with more freedom regarding going out. It's important to remember that this process requires patience, maturity, and respectful communication. The goal is not just to win an argument but to demonstrate your responsibility and establish a healthy relationship based on mutual understanding and trust. Step 1: Understand Their Perspective Before starting any conversation about gaining more independence, put yourself in your parents' shoes. They are responsible for your safety and well-being, so their concerns are valid. Reflect on why they might be hesitant about letting you go out freely - past experiences, protective instincts, fear of negative influences, etc. Recognizing these underlying fears will help you address them effectively. Key Tip: Show empathy during discussions; acknowledging their worries shows respect for their feelings and positions. Step 2: Evaluate Your Readiness Be honest with yourself about whether you're ready for increased freedom. Can you handle being accountable for your actions? Are you capable of making wise decisions when faced with peer pressure or other challenging situations? If not, work on improving these skills first. Guideline: Keep track of times when you've proven reliable and mature. Use these instances as evidence of your readiness during conversations with your parents. Step 3: Establish Clear Boundaries Discuss specific rules and expectations around curfews, destinations, and behaviors. This demonstrates your willingness to take responsibility for your actions while providing structure and limits. Key Tip: Propose a trial period where you gradually earn more privileges based on consistent good behavior. Step 4: Improve Communication Skills Practice active listening and clear articulation. Avoid getting defensive or dismissive when discussing sensitive topics. Instead, focus on maintaining open dialogue and expressing your thoughts calmly and rationally. Guideline: Consider role-playing potential scenarios with friends or family members to hone your conversational skills. Step 5: Provide Detailed Plans When asking permission to go somewhere, offer thorough information such as who you'll be with, what you'll be doing, transportation arrangements, contact numbers, and return time. Showing forethought assures them that you understand the importance of planning ahead. Key Tip: Send regular updates throughout the event, especially if plans change unexpectedly. This proactive approach reinforces their confidence in your ability to manage situations independently. Step 6: Demonstrate Responsibility Consistently meet obligations like chores, homework, and appointments. By showing reliability in everyday tasks, you prove that you can handle additional responsibilities associated with greater freedoms. Guideline: Overdeliver by completing tasks earlier than required or volunteering for extra duties to further illustrate your dedication to responsibility. Step 7: Build Trust Gradually Earning your parents' trust takes time. Start small by requesting incremental increases in autonomy (e.g., coming home 30 minutes later than usual). Consistent adherence to agreements builds credibility, paving the way for bigger leaps towards full freedom. Key Tip: Always follow through on promises, even if it means sacrificing something you wanted to do. Conclusion: Remember that building trust is a two-way street. While advocating for your desires, also consider how you can support your parents' wishes and needs. Maintaining balance and harmony within your relationship fosters mutual respect and understanding, creating fertile ground for granting you the freedom you seek. One day, you decide to create your own board game, where players need to move around the board based on the roll of the dice. But instead of just moving in straight lines, you want to add some excitement by making the movements dependent on each other. This is similar to what we see in a correlation matrix! In our game, there will be three special spots on the board - A, B, and C. When a player lands on spot A, they will sometimes also affect the positions of their friends on spots B and C. Similarly, when another friend lands on spot B or C, they could influence the position of the first player on spot A. These connections between the spots form a kind of "relationship map," showing how changes in one spot impact the others. To organize these relationships, we can write them down using numbers in a table called a "correlation matrix." Each number tells us how closely connected any two spots are, like this: Spot A -> Spot A : 1 (always affected by itself) Spot A -> Spot B : 0.2 (slight effect on Spot B) Spot A -> Spot C : 0.3 (moderate effect on Spot C) Spot B -> Spot A : 0.2 (small effect coming back from Spot B) Spot B -> Spot B : 1 (always affected by itself) Spot B -> Spot C : 0 (no direct effect on Spot C) Spot C -> Spot A : 0.3 (medium effect coming from Spot C) Spot C -> Spot B : 0 (no direct effect on Spot B) Spot C -> Spot C : 1 (always affected by itself) This is the same concept shown in the code snippet provided, except that the code uses mathematical symbols and functions to represent the relationship map. Just remember, both the example here and the code snippet describe ways to connect things together so they depend on each other, just like in our fun new board game! First I found a rocket then I put on my space helmet, and then I flew to the moon. You threw a puppy at Mary that's what you did. | This article is part of the grammar course. You may choose to follow it in a structured way, or read each item separately. One of the basic word classes is the verbs. (When Verb is written with a capital, we mean the functional element which forms part of the structure of a clause; written with a lowercase we mean verb the word class.) Verbs are one of the semantic group which carry most of the meaning of the sentence. There are several ways of looking at them. - Native speakers of English probably remember the Primary School definition of a verb as 'a doing word'. - In Secondary School, this may have been expanded to 'a word that expresses an action or a state'. - A more modern way of looking at a verb is as a word that can have tense - i.e. a word that can change, or be changed, to express past, present or future (etc). If you can say 'I do it today' (or 'I am doing it today') and then change it to 'I will do it tomorrow' or 'I did it yesterday', then it is probably a verb. This covers the case of verbs that express a state (e.g. to be and to become) as well as verbs of action (e.g. to fight, to play, to run.) (It is sometimes useful to be able to classify verbs into such groups as verbs of utterance (e.g. 'to say', 'to argue', 'to define' etc), verbs of motion ('to go', 'to come' etc) or verbs of thought ('to think', 'to consider', 'to agree' etc). These can be useful ways of thinking about verbs, particularly for foreign speakers learning some of the patterns of English; but they are not necessary as part of understanding grammar. Such groupings are more often useful as a semantic concept: they link words that deal with very similar ideas, but varied usage.) A b c d e f g... All the toys in the back seat went flying up. Title: How to Choose the Perfect Name for Your Chicken ===================================================== Introduction: Naming your chicken can be a fun and creative process that allows you to bond with your new feathered friend. A good name will make it easier for you to identify and interact with your chicken, as well as add personality to your backyard coop. This tutorial provides an in-depth guide on choosing the perfect name for your chicken while offering key tips and guidelines along the way. ### Step 1: Observe Your Chicken's Personality Traits Explanation: Every chicken has its unique character and behaviors. By observing these traits, you can select a name that suits their individuality. * **Key Tip**: Spend time with your chicken daily to get familiar with its quirks and characteristics. ### Step 2: Consider Physical Attributes Explanation: The appearance of your chicken may also inspire a fitting name based on colors, patterns, or size. * **Key Guideline**: Refrain from naming chickens after breeds, as they might change over time due to molting or crossbreeding. ### Step 3: Brainstorm Ideas Based on Interests and Hobbies Explanation: Draw inspiration from your hobbies, favorite books, movies, TV shows, or historical figures when selecting a name. * **Key Tip**: Select names easy to pronounce clearly around other animals, so there is no confusion between commands and playful calls. ### Step 4: Test Names by Calling Out Loud Explanation: To ensure ease of use, try calling out potential names aloud. If you find yourself struggling to say it comfortably or frequently mispronouncing it, consider another option. * **Key Guideline**: Avoid using offensive language or words that could potentially cause embarrassment when sharing stories about your pet chicken. ### Step 5: Research Meanings Behind Potential Names Explanation: Knowing the meaning behind a chosen name adds depth and significance to your decision. It can create connections between your chicken and its moniker, making interactions more engaging. * **Key Tip**: Use reputable sources such as baby name websites, dictionaries, or encyclopedias during research. ### Step 6: Make Sure the Name Is Easy to Remember Explanation: Choosing a simple yet memorable name ensures both you and others can recall it effortlessly when needed. * **Key Guideline**: Steer clear of complicated spellings or similar sounding names within your flock. ### Step 7: Get Input From Family Members and Friends Explanation: Sharing your shortlist of potential names with loved ones encourages collaboration and builds excitement around welcoming a new member into your family. * **Key Tip**: Encourage everyone involved in caring for the chicken to participate in this process, fostering ownership and responsibility towards the bird. Step 8: Finalize Your Decision ------------------------------ Explanation: After considering all factors discussed above, narrow down your options until you settle on the ideal name for your beloved chicken. * **Key Guideline**: Once decided, commit to consistently using the selected name to reinforce positive associations and strengthen bonds between human and fowl. How to Field a Ground Ball in Baseball Learning to field a ground ball in baseball can be an easy task to do if you do it correctly. It is a skill that you will need to know how to do no matter what position that you play on the field, but it will take practice to become proficient at fielding a ground ball in baseball. - Keep your eye on the ball. Follow the trajectory of the ball off of the end of the bat. This way you can anticipate where the ball is going and get there quicker. - Get in position to catch the ball. Move your feet as fast as you can so that you are directly in line with the ball. You may have to run to the spot, but once you get there be sure to square your shoulders to the ball. Your feet should be shoulder width apart and if you are in the correct position, the ball should be rolling towards you, between your feet. - Get low to the ground. Bend your knees so that you are closer to the ground. As the ball is approaching you, your glove should be on the ground, ready to make the catch. - Move your glove so that the ball rolls into your glove. Use your other hand to secure the ball. Sometimes the ball may take an unexpected bounce. Move your glove as needed. - Stand up and be prepared to make the throw. Transfer the ball to your throwing hand so that you are ready to throw the ball. Title: How to Evolve Budew into Roserade in Pok mon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum Introduction: In the Pok mon Diamond, Pearl, and Platinum games, evolving your Budew into Roserade can be an exciting milestone. This guide will provide you with clear, concise, and easy-to-follow steps to help you accomplish this evolution. By following these instructions, you'll have a beautiful new addition to your team - the elegant Grass/Poison type Roserade! Step 1: Catch or Obtain a Budew To begin, you need to obtain a Budew. You can either catch one in the wild (found primarily in the Floaroma Meadow) or get it through breeding a male Roselia with a female Roserade or a Ditto. Make sure that your Budew is at least level 16 before attempting to evolve it. Key tip: Save your game progress before proceeding with any attempts to evolve your Budew. If anything goes wrong during the process, such as losing the battle needed to trigger evolution, you won't lose all your hard work by returning to your saved point. Step 2: Increase Your Budew's Level to 18 Budew needs to reach level 18 to evolve into Roselia. To increase its level, engage in battles against wild Pok mon or participate in trainer battles. Each defeated opponent awards experience points which contribute towards increasing your Budew's level. Keep battling until your Budew reaches level 18. Guideline: Try using moves like Razor Leaf, Stun Spore, Absorb, or Growth during battles to maximize damage dealt while conserving energy. Step 3: Trigger Evolution Through Battle Once your Budew has reached level 18, the next requirement for evolution is engaging it in a battle. Note that merely participating in a battle isn't enough; your Budew must defeat another Pok mon or faint itself during combat. Once the criteria are met, after exiting the battle screen, you should see the cutscene where your Budew transforms into Roselia. Key Tip: Remember to keep your Budew healthy throughout this process. Using items like Potions or visiting Pok mon Centers between fights ensures it remains fit for the challenges ahead. Step 4: Further Evolving Roselia into Roserade After successfully evolving Budew into Roselia, there's only one more step left to reaching our ultimate goal - turning Roselia into Roserade. However, unlike the previous stage, no specific action triggers this transformation. Instead, allow Roselia to gain experience naturally from further battles until it levels up again. When it does, provided that the current time is between 8 PM and 4 AM, Roselia will finally evolve into Roserade! Guideline: Be aware of the real-time clock within the game world. Set alarms if necessary so you don't miss out on the opportunity due to incorrect timing. Conclusion: With patience, persistence, and careful adherence to these steps, congratulations! You now possess a powerful Grass/Poison type Roserade. Its unique move pool, combined with excellent stats, makes it a valuable asset to any well-rounded Pok mon squad. Enjoy exploring Sinnoh with your newly evolved companion! May the gods do so and more also to them. But tell me why hast thou come hither? Ask your child: How much is $500? How long would it take you to earn it? To start, have her pretend she makes $5 an hour. How many hours would she have to work to earn $500 or $5,000? (For the answers, she would divide the total earnings by her hourly wage.) Also have her figure how many days it would take her to earn each amount of money, working a certain number of hours a day. (Suppose she works eight hours a day; she would divide the total number of hours worked by eight.) Keep using larger numbers, up to a million dollars. How long would it take to make $1,000,000 if she earns $5 an hour working eight hours a day, five days a week, 50 weeks a year? Also figure different wages. Copyright Parent Institute Title: How to Destress Yourself Introduction: Stress is a normal part of life, but when it becomes chronic or overwhelming, it can have negative effects on both your physical and mental health. Fortunately, there are many techniques you can use to manage stress and improve your overall well-being. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to destress yourself, complete with in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines. Step 1: Identify the Source of Stress The first step in managing stress is identifying its source. Take some time to reflect on what's causing you to feel stressed. It could be work, relationships, finances, or any number of things. Once you know what's causing your stress, you can begin to address it directly. Key Tip: Be honest with yourself during this process. Ignoring the source of your stress won't make it go away. Step 2: Practice Deep Breathing Deep breathing is a simple yet effective way to reduce stress. When you breathe deeply, you send a message to your brain to calm down and relax. This, in turn, slows your heart rate and lowers your blood pressure. Guidelines: * Find a quiet place where you won't be disturbed. * Sit comfortably with your back straight. * Place one hand on your chest and the other on your stomach. * Breathe in slowly through your nose, feeling your stomach rise as you do so. Should we put her thumb in her mouth? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/Interviews/11/5630WS.cha = = = Quarks also have color charge and react via the weak force. For baryons, each quark is green, red or blue. One can be one color at one time. For mesons, the quark is red, green or blue and the antiquark is antired (cyan), antiblue (yellow) or antigreen (pink). Quarks can change color by emitting a W boson. For example, if a green color-chrged quark emits an red-antigreen W boson to a red quark, the green quark will become red and the red quark will become green. Therefore, W bosons along with Z bosons (which mediate the weak force for neutrinos) help particles react via the weak interaction. We return to face our superiors, our kindred, our friends--those whom we obey, and those whom we love; but even they who have neither, the most free, lonely, irresponsible and bereft of ties,--even those for whom home holds no dear face, no familiar voice,--even they have to meet the spirit that dwells within the land, under its sky, in its air, in its valleys, and on its rises, in its fields, in its waters and its trees--a mute friend, judge, and inspirer. Say what you like, to get its joy, to breathe its peace, to face its truth, one must return with a clear conscience. All this may seem to you sheer sentimentalism; and indeed very few of us have the will or the capacity to look consciously under the surface of familiar emotions. There are the girls we love, the men we look up to, the tenderness, the friendships, the opportunities, the pleasures! Title: How to Deal with Teachers Who Play Favorites Introduction: Teachers play favorites for various reasons - they might have a personal connection with some students, find certain behaviors or learning styles more appealing, or simply have unconscious biases. Regardless of the cause, favoritism can negatively impact your self-esteem, motivation, and academic performance. This guide will provide you with practical steps and strategies to deal with teachers who play favorites while maintaining respect and professionalism. Step 1: Identify the Situation Analyze your situation carefully and determine if favoritism is indeed taking place. Key signs include: The teacher frequently praises specific students while ignoring others. Certain students receive privileges (e.g., extra help, extensions) that aren't offered to everyone. The teacher seems to have preconceived expectations based on their relationship with particular students. Identifying these patterns will allow you to respond appropriately rather than jumping to conclusions. Step 2: Reflect on Yourself Consider whether there are any factors contributing to the perceived unfairness. For example, do you participate actively during class discussions? Have you submitted all assignments on time? Are your grades satisfactory? By evaluating yourself honestly, you may discover areas where improvement is needed, which could change the way the teacher perceives you. Step 3: Improve Academically and Socially Strive for better academic performance by submitting quality work consistently, engaging in class activities, and seeking assistance when necessary. Additionally, build positive relationships with both peers and faculty members; this increased visibility can lead to improved treatment from teachers who previously showed little interest in your progress. Step 4: Maintain Professional Communication Approach your teacher privately to discuss your concerns. Use "I" statements instead of blaming language, e.g., "I feel overlooked at times because I don't seem to receive feedback like other students," as opposed to "You always favor X over me." Be open to hearing their perspective without getting defensive or emotional. Key Tips: Choose an appropriate time and location for the conversation. Stay calm and composed throughout the discussion. Avoid making assumptions about the teacher's intentions. Step 5: Document Evidence Keep records of instances where favoritism appears evident, such as emails, grading discrepancies, or missed opportunities for support. Having tangible evidence will strengthen your case should further action be required. Step 6: Seek Support Reach out to trusted friends, family members, school counselors, or mentors for advice and encouragement. Sharing your experiences can provide valuable insights and alleviate feelings of isolation. Step 7: Approach School Administration If informal conversations with the teacher haven't resolved the issue, consider involving school administration. Provide them with documented examples of favoritism and express your desire for fair treatment. Keep in mind that administrative intervention may result in varying outcomes depending on the policies and culture of your educational institution. Step 8: Focus on Personal Growth Regardless of how the situation unfolds, prioritize your own development. Continue working diligently towards academic success, nurturing healthy relationships, and cultivating resilience. Remember that one person's opinion does not define your worth or potential. Conclusion: Addressing favoritism requires patience, tact, and persistence. While it may take time to see improvements, following these steps can help create a healthier learning environment and foster mutual respect between you and your teacher. Ultimately, focusing on personal growth and self-improvement allows you to rise above petty prejudices and succeed academically despite challenging circumstances. [the coin falls off the black base] No lets try to make a tunnel ROM. You be mad to me [Your mad at me.]. Walking Should Be SafeWalking is a fun and easy way for children to be active. Crossing streets, walking or playing on the road can bring children into the path of cars and trucks. Your child's age, neighbourhood and traffic all make a difference to your child's safety on roads. Plan Ahead. Children grow fast. Make sure you're ready for the next stage.Where does your child need to walk? Know your neighbourhood: Learn about programs to help your child walk safely to school, such as the Walking School Bus. - How busy is your neighbourhood? - Are there sidewalks to walk along? - Are the intersections busy? - Are there traffic lights at crossings? - Do drivers keep to the speed limit and watch out for children? - Should an adult walk with your child? What's the rush? Does your child have the judgment and skill needed to play or walk on the road alone?Most children aren't ready to walk or bike on their own until they are at least 9 years old. Some children aren't ready until they are even older. Young children have a greater chance of getting hit by a car because they: - Cannot judge how far away cars are from them or how fast cars are going. - Do not understand that they can be hurt or killed. - Can be easily distracted by many different things. - Do not see out of the corner of their eyes as well as adults, because their vision is not fully developed yet. - Are small and hard for drivers to see. - Have trouble seeing around objects. - Have trouble telling the direction of various sounds, such as a moving car. Learn How. Make sure your child learns how to do it right. Learn what to do if someone is hurt.It is never too early to teach your child the rules of the road. Walk around the neighbourhood with your child and teach her to: Safe Kids Canada has more information about safe pedestrian communities. - Stop at street curbs, alleys, driveways and pedestrian crossings. They need to make sure the street is clear before crossing by looking left, right and left again; by listening for traffic; and by making eye contact with the driver to make sure the driver sees them. - Use the sidewalk. If there is no sidewalk, show your child how to walk facing the on coming traffic, as far as possible away from the traffic on the road. - Read, understand and obey the road signs. - Choose the best place to cross the street. - Obey the crossing guard. - Use the designated crossing to cross train tracks and never to play near them. Maidstone is the county town of Kent, meaning the local government is based there. Its name means "stone of the maidens" . Title: How to Get a Scary Thought Out of Your Mind Introduction: Scary thoughts can be intrusive and difficult to deal with, but there are effective strategies to help manage and overcome them. This guide will provide you with detailed steps and useful information on how to get a scary thought out of your mind. By following these methods, you'll learn how to regain control over your thoughts and reduce anxiety related to unwanted mental intrusions. Step 1: Identify the Scary Thought Recognize and label the specific scary thought that is bothering you. Understanding what triggers your fear or anxiety is essential for addressing the issue effectively. Key tip: Be honest with yourself during this process; acknowledging the thought allows you to take action against it. Step 2: Challenge the Scary Thought Analyze the content of the thought by asking questions like: Is this thought based on facts or assumptions? What evidence supports or contradicts this thought? Are there any cognitive distortions present (e.g., catastrophizing, all-or-nothing thinking)? Confronting the thought with logic and reason can help diminish its power. Guideline: Remember that challenging a thought does not mean denying its existence; instead, focus on evaluating its validity. Step 3: Reframe the Scary Thought Transform the negative thought into a more positive or neutral one using techniques such as reappraisal, putting things in perspective, or employing humor. For example, if the scary thought is "I am going to fail at everything," try reframing it as "I may face challenges, but I have the skills and resources necessary to succeed." Key tip: Practice self-compassion when reframing thoughts - treat yourself as kindly as you would treat a friend in the same situation. Step 4: Distract Yourself from the Scary Thought Engage in an activity that requires concentration and keeps your mind occupied. Examples include reading, listening to music, exercising, or pursuing hobbies. The goal is to create distance between yourself and the scary thought so that it loses some of its grip on your attention. Guideline: Choose activities that genuinely interest you, as they will be more effective at holding your attention away from the distressing thought. Step 5: Implement Relaxation Techniques Practice relaxation exercises like deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, meditation, or yoga to calm your body and mind. These techniques promote physical and emotional well-being while reducing stress and anxiety associated with scary thoughts. Key tip: Consistency is crucial; make relaxation practices part of your daily routine for optimal benefits. Step 6: Seek Professional Help If scary thoughts persist despite consistent efforts to address them, consider seeking professional assistance from a licensed therapist or counselor specializing in cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) or acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT). Both therapies offer proven strategies for managing intrusive thoughts and improving overall mental health. Guideline: Don't hesitate to reach out for support; taking care of your mental health is just as important as caring for your physical health. Conclusion: Dealing with scary thoughts takes patience, practice, and perseverance. However, implementing the steps outlined above can significantly improve your ability to manage and eliminate disturbing mental intrusions. Remember always to prioritize self-care, seek professional guidance when needed, and maintain a growth mindset throughout the process. With time and dedication, you can successfully remove scary thoughts from your mind and enjoy greater peace and happiness. Seals balance a ball on their noses. Mount Cotopaxi is a volcano in Ecuador. Of the intense desire which Mr Harding felt to be assured on fit authority that he was wronging no man, that he was entitled in true equity to his income, that he might sleep at night without pangs of conscience, that he was no robber, no spoiler of the poor; that he and all the world might be openly convinced that he was not the man which _The Jupiter_ had described him to be; of such longings on the part of Mr Harding, Sir Abraham was entirely ignorant; nor, indeed, could it be looked on as part of his business to gratify such desires. Write a Review 13 April 2010 Mood is a statement of fact or a question. When a statement or expression or verb expresses an action, that will be called Moods. Actually there three kinds of moods, they are; 1. Indicative mood Indicative mood is used to make a statement of fact and ask a question. - Statement of fact: He read a lot of book years ago. She was punished by her parent. - Ask A Question: Are you sure? Will you be back again? Have you finished your duty? 2. Imperative Mood Imperative mood is used to express a supposition. Read the passage loudly! Take care of yourself! Try to do the best! - An entreaty or prayer: May god bless you! Have mercy upon us! 3. Subjunctive Mood Subjunctive is a statement which is used to express possibility, hypothesis or a condition. There are two kinds of subjunctive moods, they are: 1) Present Subjunctive 2) Past Subjunctive For Present subjunctive, statement will be used to express Present subjunctive occurs. - Traditional Phrases God save the king! Heaven help us! - Modern English It is suggested that the names be mentioned in the meeting. I recommended that all files be submitted immediately. Take notice that, actually, there is /should/ before the be. The sentence then will be: The names should be mention ....... All files should be submitted ...... a. Subjunctive "WISH" I wish I could fly Take notice that the statement after /I wish/ is impossible thing. In other words, the statement is the same as, "I know that I can not fly". study this following sentence structure! b. Conditional Sentence Conditional "IF" or "IF" Clause is also subjunctive because it contains untrue things or fact in the present time. If you were a bee, I would be a beautiful flower. c. As if and As though /As if/ or /as though/ is like the conditional sentence. This expression is called as subjunctive mood because it pretends to action. To make this sentence, turn into past form! He walks as if he got drunk. He commands me as though he was a commander. Take notice that there is no differences between /as if/ and /as though/. d. Form of /It is time/ + Subject It is time we went. It is time they did the deed. e. /Would rather + subject/ which indicates preferences. I would rather you went on foot. She would rather you treated us. The coaches know better than you, I think Dad. You height give me some money because I height pay for things. I want to put up to the top. Leave the cat alone at the table. [1.6 sec; S starts to speak as D does] A faggot is a kind of meatball, a traditional dish in the UK, especially the southwest of England, Wales, and the Black Country. It is made from meat off-cuts and offal, especially pork. A faggot is usually made from pig heart, liver and fatty belly meat or bacon minced together, with herbs for flavouring and sometimes breadcrumbs. The mixture is shaped in the hand into balls, wrapped round with caul (a membrane from the pig's abdomen), and baked. A similar dish, "alm ndega", is traditional in Portugal. [points to a spot on the table] To learn to read and spell words, children need the alphabetic insight that letters stand for phonemes and spellings map out the phonemes in spoken words. Before children can match letters to phonemes, they have to recognize phonemes. This lesson focuses on the /k/ phoneme. The goal of this lesson is that children will learn to identify /k/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation and a letter symbol, and then practice finding /k/ in printed words. Primary paper and pencil Chart with "Carol and Claire can cook carrots, corn, cabbage, and candy" Class set of cards with c on one side and a question mark on the other Drawing paper and crayons Chicka Boom Boom by Bill Martin Jr. by Bill Martin Jr. New York: Scholastic, 1991. Copies of a camera handout (clipart) Printed pictures with Carol, Claire, can, cook, carrots, corn, Introduce the lesson by explaining that our written language is a secret code. The tricky part is learning what letters stand for - the mouth moves we make as we say words. Today we're going to work on spotting the mouth move /k/. Today we are going to search for /k/ in words! students: Do you ever take pictures? Does the camera make a clicking sound? Whenever we hear /k/, we can click like 3. Let's try a tongue twister [on chart.] "Carol and Claire can cook carrots, corn, cabbage, and candy." Let's say it three times together. Now, this time let's stretch out our clicking /k/'s at the beginning of our words. "CCCCarol and CCCClaire ccccan ccccook ccccarrots, ccccorn, ccccabbage, and ccccandy." Try it again, and this time break it off the word: " /k/ arol and /k/ laire /k/ an /k/ ook /k/ arrots, /k/ orn, /k/ abbage, and /k/ andy." students take out their primary paper and pencils. We can use the letter c to spell /k/. Let's write it on our paper. Start like little a. Go up and touch the fence, then around and up. I want to see everybody's c. After I put a sticker on it, I want you to make eight more just like it. When you see letter c all by itself in a word, that's our signal to say /k/ like the 5. Call on students to answer and tell how they know: - Do you ever hear /k/ in cat or dog? - Do you ever hear /k/ in camera or - Do you ever hear /k/ in dessert or - Do you ever hear /k/ in camp or 6. Pass out c/? cards to each student. Say: Let's see if you can spot the mouth move /k/ in some words. Show me the /k/ if you hear the sound or the ? if you don't. Give the words one at a time: Carol, and, Claire, can, cook, carrots, corn, cabbage, and, candy. Read Chicka Chicka Boom Boom by Bill Martin Jr. and talk about the story. Read it once again. Have students raise their hands when they hear words with /k/. List their words they find on a marker board. Have pre-made handouts with a camera on them for students to write in a message about the book using invented spelling. Display their work in the classroom. assessment, distribute the picture page [made with clipart] and have students name each picture. Circle the pictures that include /k/. Martin Jr, Bill. Nearly all languages using the Roman alphabet include diacritics, which are symbols found above or below the letters. They are used for things such as tones and pronunciation. English is the only major European language that does not have any of these marks, at least not for native words. Words taken from other languages sometimes use diacritics to make clear the correct pronunciation. Why don't you move the piano over. Yes it's shining like the sun. Title: How to Deal with a Person Who Calls and Comes Over Too Much Introduction: It's important to maintain healthy relationships with others while also setting boundaries that allow you time for yourself and your own needs. If someone close to you is calling or visiting too frequently, causing discomfort or stress, this guide will help you navigate through the situation with respect and assertiveness. Remember, clear communication is essential to resolving conflicts and maintaining positive connections. Step 1: Identify Your Feelings and Needs Analyze why their frequent calls and visits make you uncomfortable. Are they intrusive? Do they disrupt your routine? Or are they simply too much to handle emotionally? Once you understand what triggers these feelings, consider what you need to feel comfortable again (e.g., more personal space, less distraction). This self-awareness will be crucial when expressing your concerns later on. Key tip: Be honest about your emotions without blaming or attacking the other person. Focus on how their actions affect you rather than attributing fault. Step 2: Set Clear Boundaries Decide on appropriate frequency limits for phone calls and visits based on your comfort level. Then communicate these limits clearly but gently, emphasizing that they are necessary for your wellbeing. For example: "I value our relationship and enjoy spending time together, but I find myself feeling overwhelmed when we talk multiple times per day. Could we agree to speak only once daily?" Guideline: Use "I" statements instead of "you" statements to avoid sounding accusatory. Step 3: Suggest Alternatives Offer alternative ways for them to stay connected, such as text messaging or video chats at predetermined intervals. By suggesting alternatives, you show that you still want to keep in touch while enforcing healthier interaction patterns. Example response: "If emergencies arise during the day, please feel free to send me a quick message instead of calling. We can then catch up properly in the evening." Step 4: Prepare for Resistance The person may resist your new boundaries initially, so prepare yourself for potential pushback. Stay firm yet empathetic in your responses, reiterating that your intentions come from a place of self-care. Example response: "I understand that my request might seem unusual, but establishing some distance allows me to better appreciate our conversations when they do happen." Step 5: Reinforce Positive Interactions When the person adheres to your established boundaries, acknowledge and reinforce their efforts by expressing gratitude. This encourages continued compliance and strengthens your bond. Example praise: "Thank you for understanding and giving me the space I needed. Our conversation today was really enjoyable!" Step 6: Evaluate and Adjust if Necessary Periodically reassess whether the agreed-upon boundaries continue meeting both parties' needs. If adjustments are required, discuss them openly and collaboratively, making sure to listen actively to one another's perspectives. Example discussion: "Lately, I've been feeling like our current arrangement isn't quite working anymore. Would you be open to discussing possible changes?" Conclusion: By addressing excessive contact issues diplomatically, you foster mutual respect, promote healthier interactions, and preserve valuable relationships. The process demands patience, empathy, and strong communication skills - qualities that ultimately contribute to building stronger bonds with those around us. In your first example you have (2,-1),(4,2) That really means you have two points that you KNOW. The first point is (2,-1), and the second is (4,2). When we write down a point as (2,-1), the first number is assumed to be the "along" or horizontal value (which we usually call x), and the second number is the up-down value (called y). So we could restate the problem in simple english as: What is the line that passes through the point 2 spaces along and 1 space down (-1 is down) and also through the point 4 spaces along and 2 spaces up. OK, you probably knew all that, so on with the meaty stuff. First you work out the slope, which is how fast the line rises. Well, we know the two points (2,-1),(4,2), so we can see that the line moves along from 2 to 4 while moving from 1 space down to 2 up. So it moves along (4-2)=2 spaces while moving up (2-(-1))=3 spaces. So the slope is 3/2. (Just think, if you were walking uphill, and you went up 1 meter for every 10 meters forward, that is a light slope upwards, and is a 1/10 slope, but if you had to go up 3m for every 2m along that would be a steep slope of 3/2) The formula we just used is: m (the slope) = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1) In other words m = (2-(-1)) / (4-2) = 3/2. Next you work out the "intercept", which is where the line crosses through x=0 (which is the y axis if you draw a proper graph) Well, we know that the slope is 3/2, so we can just start at any point and figure where the line would be when x=0 So, let's start at the first point (2,-1) (2 steps along, one down) And we know that the line moves 3 steps up for every 2 steps along. But we need to move 2 steps BACK to get to zero, so in the same time the line must move 3 steps DOWN. So, the y-value (the up-down value) started at "one down" and moved a further 3 steps down, so it must be at 4 steps down when x is at 0. The formula we just used is: b (the intercept) = y1 - (m * x1) in other words, start at y1 and travel backwards along the slope x1 steps, because we want x1 to be zero. m (the slope) = 3/2 b (the intercept) = -4 This can be written even more neatly as y = (3/2)x - 4 Proof: at 2, y = 1.5*2-4 = 3-4 = -1, at 4, y = 1.5*4-4 = 6-4 = 2 Your other solutions can be done the same way, but because we now have the formulas to work them out, we can just trust to mathematics that they will work. m (the slope) = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1) = (-1-1) / (-1-(-3)) = (-2)/(-1+3) = -2/2 = -1 b (the intercept) = y1 - (m * x1) = 1 - (-1)*-3 = 1 - 3 = -2 y = -1x-2 m (the slope) = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1) = (1-1) / (6-2) = 0/4 = 0 b (the intercept) = y1 - (m * x1) = 1 - 0*2 = 1 y = 0x + 1 = 1 (ie the equation of the line is just "y=1", no matter what value x is, y will always equal 1) Hey, you can do the rest for practice ! Free computer Tutorials Microsoft Excel 2007 to 2013 Entering Simple Addition Formula The first thing we'll do to our spreadsheet from the previous section is to add up all those numbers, the ones going down under the days of the week headings. The total for each day of the week will be placed on Row 9. So Monday's total will go in cell B9, Tuesday's total will go in cell C9, and so on. Here's our spreadsheet again: Our first total will go in cell B9. Adding up in Excel Excel needs to know which cells you want to add up. Look at the numbers for the Monday column. We have a 1 in cell B4, a 7 in cell B5, an 8 in cell B6, and a 1 in cell B7. So we want the answer to this: B4 + B5 + B6 + B7 To let Excel know that this is what we want, try this: B4 + B5 + B6 + B7 When you have entered the formula in the formula bar, press the enter key on your keyboard. Your spreadsheet should look like ours below: Something has gone wrong! This is not quite what we were expecting. We wanted Excel to add up the numbers for us, but it hasn't done anything except enter the cells we typed. What went wrong was that we didn't "tell" excel to add up. Excel needs you to type an equals (=) sign first, and then those cell references. If you don't include the equals sign, Excel thinks it's just plain text, and so doesn't do any calculating. So enter this inside of your formula bar instead: = B4 + B5 + B6 + B7 In other words, put an equals sign ( = ) before B4. Press your enter key and you should have the correct answer in cell B9. Now click back inside the formula bar, and delete the equals sign. Press the enter key again. You should then just have the same text as in the image above. We're doing this to show you an easier way to add up - with the SUM function. When your spreadsheet looks like ours in the image at the top of the page, you can nove on to the next part. Title: How to Entertain 3-4 Year Olds: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Engaging with 3-4 year olds can be both rewarding and challenging. At this age, children are developing their social skills, creativity, and independence. The following steps will provide you with various strategies and activities that cater to these developmental milestones while ensuring an entertaining experience for them. Remember, every child is unique, so feel free to adapt these suggestions based on individual preferences and needs. Step 1: Engage in Imaginative Play Imaginative play encourages creativity, problem-solving, and emotional understanding. Set up scenarios using dolls, action figures, or stuffed animals, and ask open-ended questions like "What do you think happens next?" This type of engagement also fosters language development as they describe actions and situations. Key Tip: Follow your child's lead during imaginative play; let them dictate the narrative and rules. Step 2: Create Art Together Art projects allow self-expression and fine motor skill development. Offer different mediums such as finger painting, watercolors, collages, or playdough. Encourage but don't insist on specific outcomes - remember, the process matters more than the product at this stage. Guideline: Always supervise art sessions to ensure safety, especially when dealing with potentially messy materials. Step 3: Organize Simple Games Games that involve simple rules help teach turn-taking, cooperation, and patience. Examples include Simon Says, Red Light Green Light, or Duck Duck Goose. These games promote physical activity and build gross motor skills too. Tip: Make sure everyone understands the game rules before starting to avoid frustration. Step 4: Read Interactive Books Choose books with interactive elements like flaps, tabs, or textures. Ask questions about the storyline, characters, or pictures to stimulate critical thinking. Regular reading exposes kids to new vocabulary and enhances listening comprehension. Key Guideline: Opt for age-appropriate content that holds their interest without being overly complex or scary. Step 5: Perform Puppet Shows Create homemade puppets from socks, paper bags, or gloves, then put on a show! Storylines needn't be elaborate - even retelling daily routines can captivate young audiences. Puppetry boosts verbal communication and promotes empathy by acting out different roles and emotions. Step 6: Teach Basic Yoga Poses Introduce basic yoga poses (like tree pose, downward dog, or cat cow) through fun animal names. Besides promoting flexibility and strength, yoga instills mindfulness and relaxation techniques beneficial for managing stress later in life. Step 7: Arrange Nature Walks or Scavenger Hunts Exploring outdoor environments enriches sensory experiences while teaching respect for nature. For scavenger hunts, prepare a list of common items found outside (e.g., leaves, rocks, flowers), guiding children to observe details closely. Safety Tips: Stay within safe boundaries, watch out for potential hazards, and wash hands thoroughly after handling natural objects. Conclusion: This extensive guide offers multiple ways to entertain 3-4 year olds effectively. By incorporating imaginative play, arts, games, literacy, movement, and exploration, you foster cognitive, socioemotional, and physical growth. Most importantly, maintain a positive attitude, express enthusiasm, and enjoy spending quality time together! I don't think she's USTA having undivided play attention. Title: How to Beat the Sixth Gym Leader in Pok mon Emerald Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of defeating the sixth gym leader, Winona, in Pok mon Emerald. Located in Fortree City, Winona specializes in Flying-type Pok mon. To succeed against her, you'll need a well-prepared team with moves that counteract Flying types effectively. This guide provides crucial steps and strategies to help you emerge victorious. Step 1: Understanding Flying-Type Strengths and Weaknesses: Flying-types are strong against Fighting, Bug, and Grass-types but weak against Rock, Electric, and Ice-types. They also have no effect on Steel-types. Keep these strengths and weaknesses in mind when building your team or selecting which Pok mon to bring into battle. Key Tip: Bring at least one Electric-, Rock-, or Ice-type Pok mon to exploit Winona's vulnerabilities while minimizing damage from her attacks. Step 2: Prepare Your Team: Ensure your party meets the following requirements before challenging Winona: * At least one Electric-, Rock-, or Ice-type Pok mon. * A balanced mix of levels between 35-40. * Status healing items such as Potions, Antidotes, Paralyze Heals, etc., if necessary. Guideline: Ideally, your lead Pok mon should be an Electric type like Manectric or Electabuzz since they resist most of Winona's team and can deal significant damage. Step 3: Enter Fortree City: Travel to Fortree City via Route 119 after obtaining HM04 (Strength) from Steven Stone in Granite Cave. Once there, head north towards the city center where you'll find the gym. Step 4: Navigate Through the Maze: Winona's gym is designed like a maze with many trainers and hidden paths. Make sure to explore carefully and engage every trainer until you reach the end. Defeating all trainers gives you valuable experience points and prepares your team for the final showdown. Key Trainers & Tips: * Bird Keeper Colin uses two Skarmory and a Pelipper. Use Fire or Electric moves to defeat them quickly. * Senior Mario has a Noctowl and a Swellow. Utilize Rock-type attacks here. * Bird Keeper Brittany owns three Pidgeot. Again, employ Rock-type moves. Step 5: Challenge Winona: After navigating the labyrinth, approach Winona to initiate the battle. Her team consists of the following Pok mon: 1. Skarmory Lv. 37 2. Swellow Lv. 38 3. Well you've gotta careful so you don't bite your finger. A constitutional monarchy is a form of government that is usually a democracy and has a constitution, with the monarch as head of state. Either the monarch has to obey the laws like everyone else, or there are special laws that say what the monarch can and cannot do. The monarch usually can not decide their special laws on their own. There may be laws about whom the monarch's children can marry, for example, that are passed by the Parliament. For example, in the Netherlands, if a member of the royal family marries without the permission of Parliament, they cannot become king or queen themselves. The army and navy may swear an oath to the monarch, but the real control is given to the elected government. There are laws about property and the order of succession (who gets to be the next monarch) that can only be changed by the elected government. Usually the monarch must sign laws into effect, but is required to follow the will of the elected government. Why does that robot like to hop? No we kicked him aside and said George this is my ride. Once upon a time, there lived an orange tree. All the oranges on the tree were delicious and juicy. The tree wanted to share its delicious oranges with the world. One day, it decided to give away all of its oranges to the local animals. The animal friends were delighted! The squirrels, birds, rabbits, and more all shared the oranges. They each had as much as they wanted to eat. But the tree remained sad, for it no longer had any oranges left to share. Just then, the sun began to shine brighter and the sun s warmth brought new life to the tree. Its branches were soon filled with many delicious oranges. This time, the tree decided to be more careful. It only gave away five oranges each day and kept the rest. This made it happy and it could remain generous without becoming empty. The moral of this story is that if we are generous, we can remain happy and still have a lot to give. No look at these clouds over here. Building Blocks to Reading The directions you find here are simply suggestions. Please feel free to get creative and be sure to have fun with this project. Mystery Gift is a project that can help to develop reasoning and early science skills. This project can be modified for ages 3 years to adult. For this project you will need two boxes; one for a gift box the other for an experiment box. You will also need a small gift, gift wrap, tape, blank pages, and a pencil. The Gift Box The parent or provider must complete this step alone. The idea is to exercise reasoning skills by letting the child shake, rattle, squeeze, and roll this package in an attempt to figure out what is inside. For a gift I recommend a small car, doll bottle, crayon, marble, or other common age appropriate toy. Remove the toy from it's packaging and place it in a gift box. The gift box should be too large for the gift. You want the gift to rattle around in the box. The box and gift must be durable. If a toy like crayons or marbles has been chosen, select only one or two of the crayons or marbles to be placed in the box. Wrap the box and be sure to tape the gift wrap well. By nature of this project peeking is a constant temptation. Save some wrapping paper for your book cover. Make Your Book With the child's help, print or copy 10 to 15 Blank Pages. Stack them neatly and staple them together. For the cover you will need a piece of construction paper that measures about 9 inches by 6 inches or wrapping paper that is twice that size. If you use wrapping paper fold the paper in half and use double thickness of paper to increase it's strength. Fold the sheet in half, choose a title for your book , write the title on the front cover, and decorate. Do not forget to put the author's name on the front cover. Your child will work hard and deserves to have his name on the front of his book. Staple the cover in place. Attach the book to the gift. What Is Inside? The object is for the child (this is fun for adults too) to figure out what is in the package without damaging, opening, or other wise mangling the package. The book is to keep notes of observations, ideas, and hypothesis or guesses about the package. Let your child shake the package. How does it sound? Does it sound like it is dragging or rolling? Does is sound the same if you shake it hard and soft? What does it sound like if we gently tilt the package. Does it sound the same when it is upside down? Use the book as a journal and describe the what they have learned about the package and what they think might be inside. Take dictation, write in the book what your child says about the package. Let your child draw pictures of himself with the package. Let them write in the book what they think is in the package. The Experiment Box The second box should be the same size as the first box. This box can be used to experiment. Select different objects from around the house. Put one in the box and give the box a shake. What does it sound like? Does it sound like the first box? Do you think the object in the gift box is like the object in the experiment box? Before the package is opened, let each child make a final guess as to what is in their gift box. Then in turn open the packages and see what kind of a prize you find. Copyright 1996 Karen M. Potter. Permission is granted to print "What Is Inside" and "Pages for Your Book" for instructional use by parents and teachers. Copyright information must remain intact. These pages are not for resale! He liked to hop all over the forest and show off his skills. He made many friends with the other animals, who cheered for him and gave him carrots. One day, Bunny saw a hut in a clearing. He had never seen a hut before. He was curious and wanted to hop inside. He hopped closer and closer, until he reached the door. He knocked with his paw, but no one answered. He pushed the door and it opened. Bunny hopped inside the hut. It was dark and dusty. He saw a big bed, a table, a chair, and a cupboard. He also saw a basket full of food. He smelled honey and bread and cheese. He was hungry and wanted to taste them. He hopped to the basket and took a bite of the cheese. It was yummy. He took another bite, and another. He did not hear the footsteps outside. He did not see the shadow on the wall. He did not notice the big bad wolf who lived in the hut. The wolf came in and saw Bunny eating his cheese. He was angry and hungry. He roared and showed his teeth. He wanted to eat Bunny for his lunch. Bunny was scared and sorry. He dropped the cheese and tried to hop away. But the wolf was faster and blocked the door. Bunny was trapped. He wished he had not hopped inside the hut. He wished he had stayed with his friends in the forest. He wished he had listened to his mother, who told him not to go near the hut. He closed his eyes and waited for the end. But then, he heard a loud noise. He opened his eyes and saw a big axe. It smashed the door and made a hole. He saw a man with a beard and a hat. He was a woodcutter, who worked in the forest. He saw the wolf and the bunny. He was brave and strong. He swung his axe and hit the wolf. The wolf howled and ran away. Bunny was saved. He was happy and grateful. He thanked the woodcutter, who smiled and patted his head. He gave him some honey
